# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 1]`
**Define the kernel and image of a linear transformation $T: V \rightarrow W$. State the Rank–Nullity Theorem and verify it for a $2 \times 2$ identity matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Kernel** (1 mark): $\ker(T) = \{\mathbf{v} \in V : T(\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}_W\}$.
- **Image** (1 mark): $\text{im}(T) = \{T(\mathbf{v}) : \mathbf{v} \in V\}$.
- **Rank–Nullity** (0.5 mark): $\dim(\ker T) + \dim(\text{im}\, T) = \dim V$.
- **Verification for $I_2$** (0.5 mark): $I_2 \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ implies $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$, so $\ker I_2 = \{\mathbf{0}\}$, $\dim = 0$. $\text{im}(I_2) = \mathbb{R}^2$, $\dim = 2$. Sum $= 0 + 2 = 2 = \dim \mathbb{R}^2$. ✓

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 1]`
**Write the update rule for Gradient Descent with Momentum. Explain the role of the hyperparameter $\beta$ in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Update rule** (2 marks):
  $$\mathbf{v}_t = \beta \mathbf{v}_{t-1} + \alpha \nabla J(\theta_{t-1}), \quad \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \mathbf{v}_t$$
- **Role of $\beta$** (1 mark): $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** that controls how much of the previous velocity is retained; higher $\beta$ gives smoother, faster descent along consistent gradient directions but can overshoot minima.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1, PEERT525]`**

**(a)** For the linear transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ given by the matrix
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$
find (i) the kernel of $T$ and (ii) the image of $T$. **7 marks**

**(b)** Derive the Rank–Nullity Theorem for a linear map $T : V \to W$ with $\dim V = n$ and state its significance in dimensionality reduction. **7 marks**

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Row-reduce $A$** (2 marks):

Row 2 $\to$ Row 2 $-$ 2·Row 1, Row 3 $\to$ Row 3 $-$ Row 1:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row 3 $\to$ Row 3 $-$ Row 2:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row 1 $\to$ Row 1 $-$ Row 2:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{(RREF)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify free variables** (1 mark): $x_3$ and $x_4$ are free.

**Step 3 — Solve** (2 marks):
- $x_1 = -x_3 + x_4$
- $x_2 = -x_3 - 2x_4$

$$\mathbf{x} = x_3 \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + x_4 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — State kernel and image** (2 marks):

$$\ker(T) = \text{span}\left\{\begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix},\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\right\},\quad \dim\ker T = 2$$

Image is spanned by pivot columns 1 and 2 of the **original** $A$:
$$\text{im}(T) = \text{span}\left\{\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix},\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\right\},\quad \dim\text{im}\,T = 2$$

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b)

**Derivation of Rank–Nullity** (5 marks):

Let $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ be a basis of $\ker T$, where $k = \dim(\ker T)$. Extend this to a basis $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k, \mathbf{v}_{k+1}, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_n\}$ of $V$.

**Claim:** $\{T(\mathbf{v}_{k+1}), \ldots, T(\mathbf{v}_n)\}$ is a basis of $\text{im}(T)$.

- *Spanning:* For any $\mathbf{v} \in V$, write $\mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \mathbf{v}_i$. Then $T(\mathbf{v}) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} a_i T(\mathbf{v}_i)$ (since $T(\mathbf{v}_i) = 0$ for $i \le k$).
- *Linear Independence:* Suppose $\sum_{i=k+1}^{n} c_i T(\mathbf{v}_i) = 0$. Then $T\left(\sum_{i=k+1}^{n} c_i \mathbf{v}_i\right) = 0$, so $\sum_{i=k+1}^{n} c_i \mathbf{v}_i \in \ker T$. Writing this as a combination of the basis $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ and equating coefficients of the linearly independent set forces all $c_i = 0$.

Therefore $\dim(\text{im}\, T) = n - k$, giving:
$$\dim(\ker T) + \dim(\text{im}\, T) = k + (n - k) = n = \dim V$$

**Significance in dimensionality reduction** (2 marks):
- In PCA, we project data onto a $d$-dimensional subspace that spans the **image** of a learned linear map; the **kernel** corresponds to discarded directions.
- Rank–Nullity guarantees that the *sum* of retained and discarded dimensions equals the input dimension — a fundamental budget trade-off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1, PEERT525]`**

**(a)** Consider the loss function $J(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2$ with $\theta_0 = 0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$, and momentum $\beta = 0.9$. Perform **four iterations** of gradient descent **with momentum** and tabulate $\theta_t$ and the loss $J(\theta_t)$. **7 marks**

**(b)** Show mathematically how gradient descent with momentum **scales the effective learning rate** by a factor of $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ in steady-state descent along a constant gradient direction. Hence comment on why $\alpha$ is usually set **smaller** for momentum GD than for vanilla GD. **7 marks**

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a)

Setup: $\nabla J(\theta) = 2(\theta - 5)$.

**Iteration 1** (1.5 marks): $\theta_0 = 0$, $\mathbf{v}_0 = 0$.
- $\mathbf{v}_1 = 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot 2(0 - 5) = -1.0$
- $\theta_1 = 0 - (-1.0) = 1.0$
- $J(\theta_1) = (1 - 5)^2 = 16$

**Iteration 2** (1.5 marks): $\theta_1 = 1$, $\mathbf{v}_1 = -1$.
- $\mathbf{v}_2 = 0.9 \cdot (-1) + 0.1 \cdot 2(1 - 5) = -0.9 - 0.8 = -1.7$
- $\theta_2 = 1 - (-1.7) = 2.7$
- $J(\theta_2) = (2.7 - 5)^2 = 5.29$

**Iteration 3** (1.5 marks): $\theta_2 = 2.7$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = -1.7$.
- $\mathbf{v}_3 = 0.9 \cdot (-1.7) + 0.1 \cdot 2(2.7 - 5) = -1.53 - 0.46 = -1.99$
- $\theta_3 = 2.7 - (-1.99) = 4.69$
- $J(\theta_3) = (4.69 - 5)^2 \approx 0.0961$

**Iteration 4** (1.5 marks): $\theta_3 = 4.69$, $\mathbf{v}_3 = -1.99$.
- $\mathbf{v}_4 = 0.9 \cdot (-1.99) + 0.1 \cdot 2(4.69 - 5) = -1.791 - 0.062 = -1.853$
- $\theta_4 = 4.69 - (-1.853) = 6.543$
- $J(\theta_4) = (6.543 - 5)^2 \approx 2.381$

> [!WARNING]
> **Overshoot warning:** Notice $\theta_4 = 6.543 > 5$ — the parameter has *overshot* the optimum due to accumulated momentum. This is exactly why $\alpha$ is reduced when using momentum. Students lose 1–2 marks if they do not comment on this.

**Summary Table** (1 mark):

| $t$ | $\theta_t$ | $\mathbf{v}_t$ | $J(\theta_t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 25.000 |
| 1 | 1.000 | $-1.000$ | 16.000 |
| 2 | 2.700 | $-1.700$ | 5.290 |
| 3 | 4.690 | $-1.990$ | 0.096 |
| 4 | 6.543 | $-1.853$ | 2.381 |

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b)

**Mathematical derivation** (5 marks):

Assume the iterate is descending in a fixed direction with constant gradient $\mathbf{g}$. By unrolling the velocity recursion (from §3.2):

$$\mathbf{v}_t = \alpha \sum_{\tau=0}^{t-1} \beta^{\tau}\, \mathbf{g} = \alpha \mathbf{g}\, \frac{1 - \beta^t}{1 - \beta}$$

In the limit $t \to \infty$ (and $\vert \beta \vert < 1$):

$$\mathbf{v}_\infty = \alpha \mathbf{g} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\beta}$$

So the *effective* step size per iteration is
$$\Delta \theta = -\mathbf{v}_\infty = -\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta}\, \mathbf{g}$$

Compared to vanilla GD's step $-\alpha \mathbf{g}$, this is a multiplicative factor of $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ along consistent gradient directions. With $\beta = 0.9$, the effective step is **$10\alpha$**.

**Why $\alpha$ is set smaller** (2 marks):
- The effective step along steady directions is $\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta} \mathbf{g}$. To match vanilla GD's convergence behaviour, one must reduce $\alpha$ to compensate. The conventional rule of thumb in PyTorch/TensorFlow is to multiply $\alpha$ by $(1 - \beta)$ when adding momentum. Otherwise, the parameter overshoots the optimum, as demonstrated in Q-B(a).
- Momentum also amplifies high-curvature directions (eigenvectors of the Hessian with large eigenvalues), so an excessive $\alpha$ leads to **divergence** in those directions even when descent is stable in low-curvature ones.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Patterns Reported by KTU Valuators)**
> 1. **Confusing "image" with "output dimension":** The image is a *subspace of the codomain*, not the codomain itself. Many students write $\text{im}(T) = W$ — full credit lost.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify Rank–Nullity numerically:** Always plug numbers back. A correct proof of kernel/image without the final $2 + 2 = 4$ check loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in momentum update:** The velocity is *added* with $\beta$, but the parameter is *subtracted* by velocity. Reversing this is a classic $-1$ per sub-part error.
> 4. **Wrong $\mathbf{v}_0$:** Some students initialise $\mathbf{v}_0$ randomly; it must be the zero vector by convention.
> 5. **Mixing up Nesterov gradient evaluation point:** Nesterov uses $\nabla J(\theta - \beta \mathbf{v})$, not $\nabla J(\theta)$ — full 7 marks lost on Nesterov sub-questions if confused.
> 6. **Not explaining hyperparameter roles:** Quoting $\alpha$ and $\beta$ without stating their effect on optimization trajectory is a partial-credit trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Kernel of $T$:** set of inputs mapped to $\mathbf{0}$ — a subspace of the **domain** $V$.
- **Image of $T$:** set of all outputs — a subspace of the **codomain** $W$.
- **Rank–Nullity Theorem:** $\dim(\ker T) + \dim(\text{im}\, T) = \dim V$ — always state the full formula; KTU 2024 Scheme awards partial credit for stating without proof, full credit with proof.
- $T$ is **injective** $\iff \ker T = \{\mathbf{0}\}$; $T$ is **surjective** $\iff \text{im}\, T = W$.
- For a matrix $A$, $\text{im}(A) = \text{col}(A)$; the basis is the pivot columns of the **original** $A$, not the RREF.
- **Vanilla GD:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla J(\theta)$.
- **Momentum GD:** first update $\mathbf{v} = \beta \mathbf{v} + \alpha \nabla J$, then $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \mathbf{v}$.
- **Default $\beta = 0.9$** — quote this in answers; it is the KTU expected value.
- **Effective horizon** of momentum: $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ steps; for $\beta = 0.9$ this is 10 steps.
- **Effective learning rate scaling** in steady descent: $\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta}$ — i.e. 10$\times$ for $\beta = 0.9$.
- **Nesterov variant:** gradient is evaluated at the "look-ahead" point $\theta - \beta \mathbf{v}$ — strictly better convex convergence $O(1/t^2)$.
- **Overshoot behaviour** is a feature (escaping shallow minima/saddles) but requires a smaller $\alpha$ to remain stable near sharp minima.
- **Memory cost** of momentum GD is still $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per parameter — only the velocity vector is stored.
- **Connection to ML:** Image = representable features; Kernel = invariances; Momentum = acceleration in optimization; together they enable deep learning.
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "find kernel and image," always (a) row-reduce, (b) name pivots, (c) write spanning sets, (d) state dimensions, and (e) verify Rank–Nullity.
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "show convergence benefit of momentum," present the unrolled sum, take the limit, and identify the $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent, Constrained Optimization and Lagrange Multipliers - Convex Optimization - Linear Programming - Quadratic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Gradient Descent, Constrained Optimization and Lagrange Multipliers - Convex Optimization - Linear Programming - Quadratic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is an iterative first-order optimization algorithm used to minimize an objective function $J(\theta)$ that is written as a sum of differentiable functions, by updating parameters $\theta$ using the gradient evaluated on a **single randomly chosen sample** (or a small mini-batch) at each iteration, in the direction of steepest descent of the loss.*

The general update rule is given by:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J_i(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** and $\nabla J_i(\theta_t)$ is the gradient computed using only the $i$-th training example.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are blindfolded at the top of a hill and want to reach the valley. **Batch Gradient Descent** is like feeling the entire slope of the mountain (looking at every point) before taking a careful step — slow but precise. **SGD** is like feeling the ground *under your single foot* and stepping immediately — noisy and zig-zag, but extremely fast. The randomness helps escape small local dips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature of SGD:** The "stochasticity" introduces noise that can help escape shallow local minima — a property exploited heavily in non-convex deep learning loss landscapes.

---

## 1.2 Constrained Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Constrained Optimization is the minimization (or maximization) of an objective function $f(x)$ subject to a set of equality and/or inequality constraints that restrict the feasible region of $x$.*

The general form is:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(x) \leq 0,\ \ h_j(x) = 0$$

where $g_i(x)$ are inequality constraints and $h_j(x)$ are equality constraints.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of it as finding the **lowest point in a valley**, but you are *not allowed* to step outside a fenced boundary. You may walk along the fence edge if that is where the lowest point occurs.

---

## 1.3 Lagrange Multipliers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *The Lagrange Multiplier method is a strategy for finding the local extrema of a function subject to equality constraints. It introduces auxiliary variables (multipliers) $\lambda$ to convert a constrained problem into an unconstrained one via the Lagrangian function.*

For a problem $\min f(x)$ subject to $h(x) = 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \lambda \, h(x)$$

Stationary points satisfy $\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = 0$ and $\nabla_\lambda \mathcal{L} = 0$.

### Conceptual Analogy
If a constraint is a wall, the **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$** is the "force" the wall exerts on you to keep you on the wall. At the optimum, the gradient of $f$ (the natural pull) is exactly balanced by $\lambda$ times the gradient of the constraint (the wall's push).

---

## 1.4 Convex Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Convex Optimization is the problem of minimizing a convex objective function over a convex feasible set. A function $f$ is convex if for any $x, y$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:*
> $$f(\theta x + (1-\theta) y) \leq \theta f(x) + (1-\theta) f(y)$$

### Conceptual Analogy
A convex function is shaped like a **bowl**. Any local minimum is automatically a *global* minimum — there are no deceptive dips. This is why convex problems are mathematically "easy."

---

## 1.5 Linear Programming (LP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Linear Programming is the optimization of a linear objective function subject to linear equality and inequality constraints. The standard form is:*
> $$\min\ c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax = b,\ \ x \geq 0$$

### Conceptual Analogy
You are a factory manager with a **linear budget** trying to maximize profit, where every product uses a fixed amount of resources (labor, material, time). The feasible region is a polygon (polyhedron), and the optimum is always at a **corner** (vertex) of that polygon.

---

## 1.6 Quadratic Programming (QP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Quadratic Programming is the optimization of a quadratic objective function subject to linear constraints. The standard form is:*
> $$\min\ \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x + c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \leq b,\ \ A_{eq} x = b_{eq}$$

Here $Q$ must be positive semidefinite for convexity.

### Conceptual Analogy
LP is a flat plain; QP is a **tilted bowl inside the same flat plain**. You still walk along the polygon's edges, but the cost landscape now curves, so the optimum may lie on an edge, not just at a vertex.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stochastic Gradient Descent — Theory

**Why SGD?** For large datasets with $N$ samples, Batch Gradient Descent requires computing:
$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla J_i(\theta)$$
which is expensive per iteration. SGD approximates this with one sample:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla J_{i_t}(\theta_t)$$

**Variants to know:**
- **Mini-batch GD:** Uses $m$ samples, $1 < m < N$.
- **Momentum SGD:** Adds $\gamma v_{t-1}$ to smooth updates:
$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla J_i(\theta_t),\quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t$$
- **Learning rate schedule:** $\eta_t$ must satisfy $\sum_t \eta_t = \infty,\ \sum_t \eta_t^2 < \infty$ for convergence (Robbins-Monro conditions).

### Convergence Behavior
- **Convex** functions: SGD converges to the global minimum at rate $O(1/\sqrt{t})$.
- **Non-convex:** Converges to a stationary point $\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla J(\theta)\|^2] \to 0$.

---

## 2.2 Constrained Optimization & Lagrange Multipliers — Theory

For $\min f(x)$ subject to $h(x) = 0$:

1. Form the Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \lambda^T h(x)$.
2. Compute first-order conditions:
$$\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(x) + \lambda^T \nabla h(x) = 0$$
$$\nabla_\lambda \mathcal{L} = h(x) = 0$$
3. Solve simultaneously.

For **inequality** constraints $g(x) \leq 0$, the **KKT (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker)** conditions extend the framework:

| Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x^*) = 0$ | Stationarity |
| $g_i(x^*) \leq 0$ | Primal feasibility |
| $\lambda_i \geq 0$ | Dual feasibility |
| $\lambda_i g_i(x^*) = 0$ | Complementary slackness |

---

## 2.3 Convex Optimization — Theory

**Convex Set:** A set $\mathcal{C}$ is convex if for all $x, y \in \mathcal{C}$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:
$$\theta x + (1-\theta)y \in \mathcal{C}$$

**Key properties:**
- The intersection of convex sets is convex.
- A linear function is both convex and concave.
- The optimal value of a convex program is unique in many practical cases.

---

## 2.4 Linear Programming — Theory

The feasible region $\{x : Ax = b,\ x \geq 0\}$ is a **polyhedron** (polytope if bounded).

**Fundamental Theorem of LP:** *If an LP has an optimal solution, there exists an optimal solution at a **vertex** (basic feasible solution) of the feasible polyhedron.*

**Dual LP:** Every LP has a dual. The primal
$$\min c^T x \quad \text{s.t.}\ Ax \geq b,\ x \geq 0$$
has the dual
$$\max b^T y \quad \text{s.t.}\ A^T y \leq c,\ y \geq 0$$

**Duality Theorem:** $\min c^T x = \max b^T y$ (Strong duality under Slater's condition).

---

## 2.5 Quadratic Programming — Theory

For the convex QP:
$$\min\ \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x + c^T x \quad \text{s.t.}\ Ax \leq b$$

The KKT conditions yield a system:
$$\begin{bmatrix} Q & A^T \\ A & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x^* \\ \lambda^* \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -c \\ b \end{bmatrix}$$

If $Q \succ 0$ (positive definite), the solution is unique.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD — Memorize this table. KTU frequently asks for the formula directly.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SGD update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla J_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta_t$ = learning rate |
| 2 | Robbins-Monro | $\sum \eta_t = \infty,\ \sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$ | Convergence conditions |
| 3 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}(x,\lambda,\mu) = f + \lambda^T h + \mu^T g$ | Full KKT form |
| 4 | KKT Stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i = 0$ | Optimality |
| 5 | Complementary slackness | $\lambda_i g_i(x^*) = 0$ | $\lambda_i \geq 0,\ g_i \leq 0$ |
| 6 | Convexity test | $f(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \leq \theta f(x) + (1-\theta)f(y)$ | Definition |
| 7 | LP standard form | $\min c^T x,\ Ax = b,\ x \geq 0$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | LP dual | $\max b^T y,\ A^T y \leq c,\ y \geq 0$ | Strong duality holds |
| 9 | QP standard form | $\min \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x + c^T x,\ Ax \leq b$ | $Q \succeq 0$ for convex |
| 10 | QP KKT system | $\begin{bmatrix} Q & A^T \\ A & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ \lambda \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -c \\ b \end{bmatrix}$ | Block KKT matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** SGD powers deep learning (training neural networks). LP is used in logistics, supply chain, and airline scheduling. QP appears in portfolio optimization, SVMs, and Model Predictive Control (MPC). Lagrange multipliers underpin the training of every constrained ML model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SGD as an Approximation of Batch GD

**Start with the empirical risk:**
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} J_i(\theta)$$

**Compute the full batch gradient:**
$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla J_i(\theta)$$

**SGD approximation:** Replace the sum with a single sample $i_t$ drawn uniformly at random:

$$\nabla J(\theta) \approx \nabla J_{i_t}(\theta)$$

**Unbiasedness check:**
$$\mathbb{E}_{i_t}[\nabla J_{i_t}(\theta)] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla J_i(\theta) = \nabla J(\theta)$$

**Variance check:** The estimator has variance
$$\text{Var}[\nabla J_{i_t}] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\|\nabla J_i - \nabla J\|^2$$
which does not vanish as $N$ grows — this is the source of the "noise" in SGD.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lagrange Multiplier Theorem

**Problem:** $\min f(x)$ subject to $h(x) = 0$, where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $h: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$.

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian:**
$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \lambda^T h(x)$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives:**
$$\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(x) + J_h(x)^T \lambda = 0$$
$$\nabla_\lambda \mathcal{L} = h(x) = 0$$

where $J_h(x) = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is the Jacobian of the constraints.

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation:** At a local optimum, the gradient of $f$ must be a linear combination of the gradients of active constraints:
$$\nabla f(x^*) = -\lambda^T \nabla h(x^*)$$

**Step 4 — Second-order sufficient condition:** For a minimum,
$$d^T \nabla^2_{xx} \mathcal{L}(x^*, \lambda^*) d > 0$$
for all $d \neq 0$ such that $\nabla h(x^*) d = 0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Lagrange Multiplier on a Simple 2D Problem

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2$ subject to $x + y = 1$.

**Step 1:** Lagrangian
$$\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + \lambda(x + y - 1)$$

**Step 2:** Partial derivatives
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x + \lambda = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 2y + \lambda = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = x + y - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve. From the first two: $2x = -\lambda = 2y$, so $x = y$.
Substitute into the third: $2x = 1 \Rightarrow x = y = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 4:** $\lambda = -2x = -1$, and $f^* = (1/2)^2 + (1/2)^2 = 1/2$.

**Result:** The optimum is $(1/2, 1/2)$ on the line $x+y=1$, with multiplier $\lambda = -1$ and minimum value $f^* = 1/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** $x^2+y^2$ is the squared distance from the origin. The closest point on the line $x+y=1$ to the origin is $(1/2, 1/2)$ — exactly what the math says.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: KKT Conditions for Inequality Constraints

**Problem:** $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0,\ i = 1,\ldots,m$.

**Lagrangian:**
$$\mathcal{L}(x, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mu_i g_i(x)$$

**Necessary conditions (assuming constraint qualification, e.g., LICQ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mu_i \nabla g_i(x^*) &= 0 \quad \text{(Stationarity)} \\
g_i(x^*) &\leq 0 \quad \forall i \quad \text{(Primal Feasibility)} \\
\mu_i &\geq 0 \quad \forall i \quad \text{(Dual Feasibility)} \\
\mu_i \, g_i(x^*) &= 0 \quad \forall i \quad \text{(Complementary Slackness)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complementary slackness meaning:** If $\mu_i > 0$, then $g_i(x^*) = 0$ (the constraint is **active** at the optimum). If $g_i(x^*) < 0$ (inactive), then $\mu_i = 0$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: QP Solved via KKT

**Problem:** Minimize $\tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + x_1$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 1$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Identify $Q = I_2$, $c = (1, 0)^T$, $A = [1\ 1]$, $b = 1$, plus non-negativity.

**Step 2:** Form the KKT system. Let $\lambda \geq 0$ for $x_1 + x_2 \leq 1$, and $\mu_1, \mu_2 \geq 0$ for non-negativity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + \mu_1 - \lambda &= -1 \\
x_2 + \mu_2 - \lambda &= 0 \\
\lambda(x_1 + x_2 - 1) &= 0 \\
\mu_1 x_1 &= 0 \\
\mu_2 x_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Guess active set. Try $x_1 + x_2 = 1$ active, $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = 0$:
$$x_1 = \lambda - 1, \quad x_2 = \lambda, \quad x_1 + x_2 = 2\lambda - 1 = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$$

So $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 1$. Verify: $x_1 + x_2 = 1 \leq 1$ ✓, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$ ✓, $\lambda = 1 \geq 0$ ✓.

**Step 4:** Minimum value $= \tfrac{1}{2}(0 + 1) + 0 = 0.5$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Reference Matrix — KTU commonly asks "which method applies to which problem?"**

| Problem Type | Objective | Constraints | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unconstrained, smooth | Nonlinear | None | Gradient Descent / SGD / Newton |
| Constrained, smooth | Nonlinear | Equality $h(x)=0$ | Lagrange Multipliers |
| Constrained, smooth | Nonlinear | Inequality $g(x) \leq 0$ | KKT Conditions |
| Linear objective | Linear | Linear | Simplex / Interior Point |
| Quadratic objective | Quadratic | Linear | KKT / Active Set / Interior Point |
| Convex program | Convex | Convex set | Any convex solver; guarantees global optimum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SGD Iteration Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize theta randomly] --> B[Shuffle training data]
    B --> C[Pick one sample i_t]
    C --> D[Compute gradient of J_i_t at theta]
    D --> E[Update: theta = theta - eta times grad]
    E --> F{Has convergence criterion been met?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Output: theta_star]
    H[Decay learning rate eta after each epoch] -.-> E
```

## 4.2 Constrained Optimization Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Objective f x] -->|Gradient pull| B(Optimum point x star)
    C[Constraint g x less than 0] -->|Wall push lambda| B
    D[Feasible Region] -->|Contains| B
    B --> E[KKT balance: grad f = -lambda times grad g]
```

## 4.3 Convex vs Non-Convex Loss Landscapes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONVEX["Convex Landscape"]
        C1[Local min = Global min] --> C2[Single bowl shape]
    end
    subgraph NONCONVEX["Non-Convex Landscape"]
        N1[Multiple local minima] --> N2[SGD noise helps escape shallow ones]
    end
```

## 4.4 LP/QP Feasible Region (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[LP or QP problem] --> F[Feasible Region: Polyhedron from Ax leq b]
    F --> V1[Vertex 1: Basic Feasible Solution]
    F --> V2[Vertex 2]
    F --> V3[Vertex 3]
    F --> V4[Vertex 4]
    V1 --> S[Solver]
    V2 --> S
    V3 --> S
    V4 --> S
    S --> O[Optimal Vertex: Linear Program]
    S --> O2[Optimal Edge point: Quadratic Program]
```

## 4.5 KKT Condition Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Stationarity grad f + sum lambda grad g = 0] --> K[KKT System]
    P[Primal Feasibility g leq 0] --> K
    D[Dual Feasibility lambda geq 0] --> K
    C[Complementary Slackness lambda g = 0] --> K
    K --> SOL[Solution x star, lambda star]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note:** KTU ESE follows Part A (3-mark short answers) and Part B (14-mark long answers with internal choice). The model answers below match the official KTU valuation key style — concise, formula-first, with clear final conclusions.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Stochastic Gradient Descent. Write its update rule and mention one advantage over Batch Gradient Descent.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SGD Definition:** Stochastic Gradient Descent is an iterative optimization algorithm that updates parameters using the gradient of the loss computed on a **single random training example** (or small mini-batch) at each step, rather than the entire dataset.

**Update Rule:**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla J_{i_t}(\theta_t)$$

**Advantage over Batch GD:** SGD requires only **one gradient evaluation per iteration**, making it computationally cheap and scalable to large datasets ($N \to \infty$). Additionally, the stochastic noise helps escape shallow local minima in non-convex problems (e.g., neural network training).

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark advantage)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the KKT conditions for a constrained optimization problem with inequality constraints.**

**Model Answer:**

For the problem $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0,\ i = 1, \ldots, m$, the KKT conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1)\ \nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mu_i \nabla g_i(x^*) &= 0 \quad &\text{(Stationarity)} \\
(2)\ g_i(x^*) &\leq 0 \quad &\text{(Primal feasibility)} \\
(3)\ \mu_i &\geq 0 \quad &\text{(Dual feasibility)} \\
(4)\ \mu_i \, g_i(x^*) &= 0 \quad &\text{(Complementary slackness)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks: 0.75 each condition)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Solve the following constrained optimization problem using the **Lagrange Multiplier method**:
$$\min f(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad x + y = 4$$
Identify whether the solution is a maximum or minimum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a brief note on **Convex Optimization**, stating the definition of a convex set and a convex function, and explain why any local minimum of a convex function over a convex set is a global minimum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Form the Lagrangian
$$\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + 2y^2 + \lambda(x + y - 4)$$

**Step 2:** Take partial derivatives
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} &= 2x + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = -2x \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} &= 4y + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = -4y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $\lambda$:
$$-2x = -4y \Rightarrow x = 2y$$

**Step 4:** Use the constraint $x + y = 4$:
$$2y + y = 4 \Rightarrow 3y = 4 \Rightarrow y = \tfrac{4}{3}$$
$$x = 2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} = \tfrac{8}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the value: $f^* = (\tfrac{8}{3})^2 + 2(\tfrac{4}{3})^2 = \tfrac{64}{9} + \tfrac{32}{9} = \tfrac{96}{9} = \tfrac{32}{3}$.

**Step 6:** Nature check using bordered Hessian. The bordered Hessian is $H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2x \end{bmatrix}$ (with second-order term in $y$ direction 4 > 0), and the bordered determinant at $(8/3, 4/3)$ is $-1 < 0$, indicating a **minimum**.

> **Final Answer:** $(x^*, y^*) = (\tfrac{8}{3}, \tfrac{4}{3})$, $f^* = \tfrac{32}{3}$, **minimum**. *(7 marks: 1 Lagrangian + 2 derivatives + 2 solving + 1 evaluation + 1 nature)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Convex Set:** A set $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is convex if for all $x, y \in \mathcal{C}$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:
$$\theta x + (1-\theta) y \in \mathcal{C}$$

**Convex Function:** A function $f: \mathcal{C} \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex if for all $x, y \in \mathcal{C}$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:
$$f(\theta x + (1-\theta)y) \leq \theta f(x) + (1-\theta) f(y)$$

Equivalently, $f$ has a non-negative definite Hessian everywhere: $\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0$.

**Why local minimum = global minimum:**

Suppose $x^*$ is a local minimum of a convex function $f$ over a convex set $\mathcal{C}$. Assume for contradiction there exists $y \in \mathcal{C}$ with $f(y) < f(x^*)$. For any $\theta \in (0, 1)$, the point $z = \theta y + (1-\theta) x^*$ is in $\mathcal{C}$ (convexity of the set). By convexity of $f$:

$$f(z) \leq \theta f(y) + (1-\theta) f(x^*) < \theta f(x^*) + (1-\theta) f(x^*) = f(x^*)$$

As $\theta \to 1$, $z \to y$, and points arbitrarily close to $x^*$ have $f(z) < f(x^*)$, contradicting the local minimality of $x^*$. Hence no such $y$ exists, and $x^*$ is a **global minimum**.

**Examples:** $f(x) = x^2$, $f(x) = e^x$, linear functions. *(7 marks: 2 definitions + 5 proof)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Programming (LP)** problem in standard form. State and explain the **Fundamental Theorem of LP**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following **Quadratic Programming (QP)** problem:
$$\min\ \tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2) - 4x_1 - 2x_2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_1 + x_2 \leq 4,\ x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$
**Solve it using the KKT conditions.** Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Standard Form of LP:**
$$\min\ c^T x \quad \text{subject to} \quad Ax = b,\ \ x \geq 0$$
where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the decision variable, $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the cost vector, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $\text{rank}(A) = m < n$, and $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$.

**Feasible Region:** $\mathcal{P} = \{x : Ax = b,\ x \geq 0\}$ is a **polyhedron** (polytope if bounded).

**Basic Feasible Solution (BFS):** A solution $x$ with at most $m$ non-zero components (the basic variables) corresponding to $m$ linearly independent columns of $A$ (the basis $B$). Setting non-basic variables to zero and solving $x_B = B^{-1} b$ gives the BFS.

**Fundamental Theorem of LP:** *If an LP has an optimal solution, then there exists an optimal solution which is a Basic Feasible Solution (i.e., a vertex of the feasible polyhedron). Equivalently, the optimum of an LP — if it exists and is feasible — is attained at an extreme point of the feasible region.*

**Consequence:** Algorithms (Simplex, Interior Point) only need to search vertices — the search space is finite (at most $\binom{n}{m}$ vertices). This is the **theoretical foundation of the Simplex method**.

> **Note:** The dual LP is given by $\max b^T y$ s.t. $A^T y \leq c$. Under Slater's condition, strong duality holds: $\min c^T x = \max b^T y$. *(7 marks: 2 standard form + 3 BFS + 2 theorem)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Identify matrices:
$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad c = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix},\quad A = [1\ 1],\quad b = 4$$

**Step 2:** Form KKT system. Let $\lambda \geq 0$ for $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$, and $\mu_1, \mu_2 \geq 0$ for $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 - 4 + \mu_1 - \lambda &= 0 \quad &(1)\\
x_2 - 2 + \mu_2 - \lambda &= 0 \quad &(2)\\
\lambda(x_1 + x_2 - 4) &= 0 \quad &(3)\\
\mu_1 x_1 &= 0 \quad &(4)\\
\mu_2 x_2 &= 0 \quad &(5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Guess active set. The objective is unbounded below without the constraint, so the constraint is **active**: $x_1 + x_2 = 4$, and non-negativity will also be active at the optimum (since the unconstrained minimum is at $(4, 2)$ which violates $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$). So $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = 0$, $\lambda > 0$.

From (1) and (2): $x_1 = 4 - \mu_1 + \lambda = 4 + \lambda$, $x_2 = 2 + \lambda$.

**Step 4:** Apply active constraint $x_1 + x_2 = 4$:
$$(4 + \lambda) + (2 + \lambda) = 4 \Rightarrow 6 + 2\lambda = 4 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1$$

But this gives $\lambda < 0$, which violates dual feasibility. Hence the constraint is **not active**, and $\lambda = 0$.

**Step 5:** Re-try with $\lambda = 0$ and inactive $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$. From (1) and (2):
$$x_1 = 4 - \mu_1, \quad x_2 = 2 - \mu_2$$

Try $x_1 > 0$ and $x_2 > 0$: then $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = 0$, so $x_1 = 4$, $x_2 = 2$. Check: $x_1 + x_2 = 6 > 4$ — **violates the constraint!**

**Step 6:** Re-try with constraint active. Set $\lambda \geq 0$, but try $x_1 > 0$ ($\mu_1 = 0$), $x_2 = 0$ ($\mu_2 \geq 0$).
From (1): $x_1 = 4 + \lambda$.
From (2): $-2 + \mu_2 - \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \mu_2 = 2 + \lambda$.
Apply $x_1 + x_2 = 4$: $x_1 = 4$, so $4 + \lambda = 4 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0$.
Then $\mu_2 = 2$. Check: $x_1 = 4, x_2 = 0$, $\lambda = 0, \mu_1 = 0, \mu_2 = 2 \geq 0$ ✓.

**Step 7:** Verify by trying $x_1 = 0$ symmetrically: $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 4, \lambda = 2, \mu_1 = 6, \mu_2 = 0$.
Objective: $f = 0 + 8 - 0 - 8 = 0$. Higher than $x_1 = 4, x_2 = 0$ case: $f = \tfrac{1}{2}(16) - 16 = -8$.

**Final Answer:** $(x_1^*, x_2^*) = (4, 0)$ with $\lambda^* = 0, \mu_1^* = 0, \mu_2^* = 2$, and minimum value $f^* = -8$. *(7 marks: 1 KKT setup + 2 active set analysis + 2 solving + 1 verification + 1 final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **SGD:** Students often confuse the update rule with Batch GD — *write the single-sample gradient, not the sum*.
> 2. **Lagrange:** Forgetting to check the **second-order condition** (bordered Hessian) costs 1 mark. Always state whether the answer is a max, min, or saddle.
> 3. **KKT:** Violating **complementary slackness** silently is the most common error. If a constraint is inactive, its multiplier MUST be zero.
> 4. **LP:** Confusing the primal and dual forms. *Primal min → dual max; primal $\geq$ → dual $\leq$.*
> 5. **QP:** Forgetting to verify that the KKT solution satisfies **all** constraints (primal feasibility) before declaring it optimal. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **SGD update:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla J_{i_t}(\theta_t)$. Robbins-Monro conditions ensure convergence.
- ✅ **Mini-batch SGD:** compromise between speed and stability. Most production systems use mini-batches (e.g., batch size 32, 64, 128).
- ✅ **Lagrangian:** Always form $\mathcal{L} = f + \lambda^T h$ first, then take partials w.r.t. all variables including $\lambda$.
- ✅ **KKT = 4 conditions:** Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness. All four must be satisfied.
- ✅ **Convex function test:** Hessian is positive semidefinite, OR use the definition with the chord-above-graph property.
- ✅ **Convex optimization = global optimum guaranteed** — this is the *single most important practical fact*.
- ✅ **LP standard form:** $\min c^T x,\ Ax = b,\ x \geq 0$. Optimal solution (if exists) is at a **vertex** of the polyhedron.
- ✅ **LP Dual:** Min primal → Max dual. Matrix $A$ transposed. Inequality direction flips.
- ✅ **QP standard form:** $\min \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x + c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \leq b$. Need $Q \succeq 0$ for convexity.
- ✅ **Solving QP:** Set up KKT, guess active set, verify $\lambda \geq 0$ (dual feasibility) and all primal constraints.
- ✅ **Duality Theorem:** Strong duality holds for convex problems satisfying Slater's condition. Useful for certificate of optimality.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** LP → supply chain & scheduling; QP → portfolio optimization & SVMs; SGD → deep learning; Lagrange/KKT → all of constrained ML.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Norms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Norm

In the context of Computational Fundamentals for Machine Learning, a **norm** is a real-valued function that maps a vector (or matrix) from a vector space to a non-negative real number, quantifying its "magnitude" or "size" in a mathematically rigorous way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is called a **norm** (denoted as $\|\cdot\|$) if it satisfies the following three axioms for all vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and scalar $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$:
>
> 1. **Non-negativity / Positive Definiteness:** $\|\mathbf{x}\| \geq 0$, and $\|\mathbf{x}\| = 0 \iff \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$
> 2. **Absolute Homogeneity (Scaling):** $\|\alpha \mathbf{x}\| = \vert \alpha \vert \cdot \|\mathbf{x}\|$
> 3. **Triangle Inequality:** $\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\| \leq \|\mathbf{x}\| + \|\mathbf{y}\|$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Universal Measuring Tape"

Imagine you are a **delivery driver in a city** carrying packages represented as vectors. To measure the "total size" of a route, you could use different "measuring tapes":

- A **Manhattan taxi meter (L1 Norm)** measures only horizontal and vertical distance traveled in a grid-locked city like Manhattan.
- A **Standard straight-line ruler (L2 Norm)** measures the shortest direct "as the crow flies" distance — the Euclidean route.
- A **GPS Speedometer that tracks peak velocity (L∞ Norm)** records only the maximum coordinate movement, ignoring the smaller ones.

Each "tape" is a valid **norm** because it always returns a non-negative number, scales linearly if you travel twice the distance, and obeys the triangle rule (the direct path is never longer than the sum of the detour path).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Norms Matter in Machine Learning:** Norms are the **backbone of loss functions** (Mean Squared Error uses L2), **regularization** (Lasso uses L1, Ridge uses L2), **distance metrics** (k-Nearest Neighbors), and **gradient clipping** (preventing exploding gradients in deep networks).

## 1.3 Terminology used in KTU Valuation

- **Unit Ball:** The set of all vectors satisfying $\|\mathbf{x}\| \leq 1$.
- **Equivalence of Norms:** All norms on a finite-dimensional vector space are equivalent (they define the same topology).
- **Dual Norm:** The norm of the linear functional defined by the inner product.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Three Axioms

### Axiom 1: Non-Negativity (Positive Definiteness)
- **Why:** Magnitude cannot be negative; only the zero vector has zero magnitude.
- **How:** Ensures the norm returns a meaningful scalar usable in optimization objectives (e.g., minimizing error).
- **Engineering Utility:** Guarantees that loss functions are bounded below by $\mathbf{0}$, making gradient descent convergence well-defined.

### Axiom 2: Absolute Homogeneity
- **Why:** Scaling a vector by $\alpha$ must scale its magnitude by exactly $\vert \alpha \vert$.
- **How:** Prevents inconsistency when input data is normalized or rescaled.
- **Engineering Utility:** Critical in **deep learning batch normalization** — when we scale weights, the loss must scale predictably.

### Axiom 3: Triangle Inequality
- **Why:** The shortest path between two points is always less than or equal to any detour.
- **How:** Underpins convergence proofs for iterative ML algorithms.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used to prove **Cauchy sequences converge**, justifying the stability of stochastic gradient descent.

## 2.2 Classification of Vector Norms

Vector norms are parameterized by an integer $p \geq 1$, collectively known as the **$L_p$ family**:

$$L_p: \|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$$

The three most important special cases are $p=1$, $p=2$, and $p=\infty$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table: Vector & Matrix Norms for KTU Board Exams**

| **Norm Name** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Geometric Shape (Unit Ball in 2D)** | **Primary ML Use Case** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L0 "Norm"** | $\sum_{i} \mathbf{1}(x_i \neq 0)$ | Not a true norm | Feature selection sparsity count |
| **L1 (Manhattan)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert$ | Diamond / Rhombus | **Lasso Regularization** ($L_1$) |
| **L2 (Euclidean)** | $\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | Circle | **Ridge Regularization** ($L_2$), MSE |
| **Lp (General)** | $\left(\sum_{i} \vert x_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Convex shape between diamond and circle | Theoretical foundations |
| **L∞ (Chebyshev)** | $\max_i \vert x_i \vert = \lim_{p\to\infty} \|\mathbf{x}\|_p$ | Square | Max-error metric, adversarial robustness |
| **Frobenius (Matrix)** | $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} a_{ij}^2}$ | N/A (matrix) | Weight decay, tensor analysis |
| **Spectral (Matrix)** | $\|A\|_2 = \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(A^T A)}$ | N/A (matrix) | Condition number $\kappa(A)$ |
| **Nuclear (Matrix)** | $\|A\|_* = \sum_i \sigma_i$ | N/A (matrix) | Low-rank matrix completion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** $\|\mathbf{x}\|_0$ is **NOT a true norm** because it violates the scaling axiom (if you double a vector, the number of non-zero entries does not double). The Board examiner expects you to mention this if asked.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application of Norms** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Computer Vision (CNN)** | L2 normalization of feature embeddings for face recognition |
| **NLP / Transformers** | L2 norm of attention weights for token importance |
| **Robotics / Control** | L∞ norm bounds maximum actuator effort |
| **Signal Processing** | Frobenius norm measures reconstruction error in compressed sensing |
| **Recommender Systems** | Nuclear norm enables low-rank matrix factorization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalence Between L2 Norm Squared and Dot Product

**Goal:** Prove that $\|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1:** Write the formal definition of the L2 norm.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \cdots + x_n^2}$$

**Step 2:** Square both sides to remove the square root.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \cdots + x_n^2$$

**Step 3:** Express the right-hand side as a vector-matrix product.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Identify this product as the dot product.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} = \langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x} \rangle$$

**Conclusion:** This identity is the foundation for proving gradient calculations in linear regression and is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Triangle Inequality for L2 Norm

**Goal:** Prove $\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$ using the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality.

**Step 1:** Square both sides (both sides are non-negative, so squaring preserves inequality).

$$\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2^2 \leq \left(\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the left side using the dot product identity from Derivation 1.

$$\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2^2 &= (\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}) \\
&= \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} \\
&= \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the right side.

$$\left(\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2\right)^2 = \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2$$

**Step 4:** Compare both sides. We need to show:

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2$$

**Step 5:** Cancel common terms on both sides.

$$2\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} \leq 2\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the **Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality** ($\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$), which completes the proof.

$$\therefore \|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: L∞ Norm as the Limit of Lp Norm

**Goal:** Prove that $\lim_{p \to \infty} \|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \max_i \vert x_i \vert$.

**Step 1:** Factor out the maximum absolute value $M = \max_i \vert x_i \vert$ from the Lp definition.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p} = M \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\vert x_i \vert}{M}\right)^p \right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 2:** Note that each term $\frac{\vert x_i \vert}{M} \leq 1$, so the bracketed sum is bounded between $1$ and $n$.

$$1 \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\vert x_i \vert}{M}\right)^p \leq n$$

**Step 3:** Take the $p$-th root. As $p \to \infty$, $n^{1/p} \to 1$ and $1^{1/p} = 1$.

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} 1^{1/p} \leq \lim_{p \to \infty} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\vert x_i \vert}{M}\right)^p\right)^{1/p} \leq \lim_{p \to \infty} n^{1/p}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Squeeze Theorem.

$$1 \leq \lim_{p \to \infty} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\vert x_i \vert}{M}\right)^p\right)^{1/p} \leq 1$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the limit equals $1 \times M$.

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} \|\mathbf{x}\|_p = M = \max_i \vert x_i \vert = \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute L1, L2, and L∞ norms for $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 1: L1 Norm**

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = \vert 3 \vert + \vert -4 \vert = 3 + 4 = \mathbf{7}$$

**Step 2: L2 Norm**

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = \mathbf{5}$$

**Step 3: L∞ Norm**

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = \max(\vert 3 \vert, \vert -4 \vert) = \mathbf{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For this vector, L2 (5) ≤ L∞ (4)... wait, this violates the general inequality. **Correction:** $4 \leq 5 \leq 7$. This correctly demonstrates that **for any vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we have** $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a norm. State and explain the three properties that a function must satisfy to qualify as a norm.**

**Model Answer:**

A **norm** is a real-valued function $\|\cdot\| : \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a non-negative "size" to every vector. To be a valid norm, the function must satisfy:

1. **Positive Definiteness:** $\|\mathbf{x}\| \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$, and $\|\mathbf{x}\| = 0$ if and only if $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$. *(Ensures zero vector is the only vector with zero magnitude.)*
2. **Absolute Homogeneity:** $\|\alpha \mathbf{x}\| = \vert \alpha \vert \cdot \|\mathbf{x}\|$ for any scalar $\alpha$. *(Scaling the vector scales its norm linearly.)*
3. **Triangle Inequality:** $\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\| \leq \|\mathbf{x}\| + \|\mathbf{y}\|$. *(The direct path is never longer than the sum of detour paths.)*

A function violating any of these is not a norm (e.g., L0 "norm" violates homogeneity).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between L1 and L2 norms with suitable examples and mention their use in machine learning.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **L1 Norm (Manhattan)** | **L2 Norm (Euclidean)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formula** | $\sum_i \vert x_i \vert$ | $\sqrt{\sum_i x_i^2}$ |
| **Geometry** | Diamond-shaped unit ball | Circular unit ball |
| **Example for $\mathbf{x}=[3,4]$** | $\vert 3 \vert + \vert 4 \vert = 7$ | $\sqrt{9+16} = 5$ |
| **ML Use Case** | **Lasso Regularization** (induces sparsity) | **Ridge Regularization / MSE** |
| **Differentiability** | Not differentiable at zero | Differentiable everywhere |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** For Part B questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the third axiom** (Triangle Inequality) when listing norm properties — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing L2 and Frobenius norms** — L2 is for vectors (and is a special induced matrix norm); Frobenius is strictly for matrices.
> 3. **Not mentioning units or context** — Always state the ML application (e.g., "L2 norm is used in Ridge regression") to score the application marks.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps** — Partial credit is given for each step; never write "by direct computation" without showing work.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Lp norm for $p \geq 1$. For the vector $\mathbf{x} = [2, -3, 1, -4]^T$, compute $\|\mathbf{x}\|_1$, $\|\mathbf{x}\|_2$, and $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$. Justify why each function qualifies as a norm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the role of L1 and L2 regularization in machine learning. Show with a sketch how L1 induces sparsity whereas L2 shrinks weights uniformly. Mention the optimization objective for both.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Definition of Lp norm.

For $p \geq 1$, the Lp norm of a vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is:

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 2:** Verification that it satisfies the three norm axioms (briefly):
- **Non-negativity:** Sum of non-negative terms raised to positive power and $p$-th root is non-negative; zero only when all $x_i = 0$.
- **Homogeneity:** $\|\alpha \mathbf{x}\|_p = \left(\sum \vert \alpha x_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p} = \vert \alpha \vert \left(\sum \vert x_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p} = \vert \alpha \vert \|\mathbf{x}\|_p$.
- **Triangle Inequality:** Minkowski's Inequality guarantees this for all $p \geq 1$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\|\mathbf{x}\|_1$.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = \vert 2 \vert + \vert -3 \vert + \vert 1 \vert + \vert -4 \vert = 2 + 3 + 1 + 4 = \mathbf{10}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\|\mathbf{x}\|_2$.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2 + 1^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt{30} \approx \mathbf{5.477}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$.

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = \max(\vert 2 \vert, \vert -3 \vert, \vert 1 \vert, \vert -4 \vert) = \mathbf{4}$$

**Final Ordering:** $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = 4 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \approx 5.48 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = 10$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Regularization Concept.** Regularization adds a penalty term to the loss function to prevent overfitting:

$$J_{reg}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda \cdot R(\theta)$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the regularization strength.

**Step 2: L1 Regularization (Lasso).**

$$R_1(\theta) = \lambda \sum_{i} \vert \theta_i \vert = \lambda \|\theta\|_1$$

The L1 constraint region is a **diamond**. The optimal solution tends to hit the **corners** of the diamond, where some $\theta_i = 0$, inducing **sparsity** (feature selection).

**Step 3: L2 Regularization (Ridge).**

$$R_2(\theta) = \lambda \sum_{i} \theta_i^2 = \lambda \|\theta\|_2^2$$

The L2 constraint region is a **circle**. The optimal solution is on the smooth boundary, shrinking weights **uniformly** toward zero but rarely making them exactly zero.

**Step 4: Geometric Sketch Description.**

- L1 contour (diamond) intersects loss contour at axis-aligned corners $\Rightarrow$ exact zeros.
- L2 contour (circle) intersects loss contour on smooth boundary $\Rightarrow$ small but non-zero weights.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** Use **L1** when you need automatic feature selection (interpretability). Use **L2** when all features are expected to contribute and computational stability is paramount.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Frobenius norm and Spectral norm for matrices. Prove that for any matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, the Frobenius norm is greater than or equal to the spectral norm: $\|A\|_F \geq \|A\|_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the equivalence of norms theorem for finite-dimensional vector spaces. Show with an example that all norms on $\mathbb{R}^2$ are equivalent up to constants.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Definition of **Frobenius Norm**.

$$\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}^2} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(A^T A)}$$

**Step 2:** Definition of **Spectral Norm**.

$$\|A\|_2 = \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(A^T A)} = \sigma_{\max}(A)$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $A^T A$ and $\sigma_{\max}$ is the largest singular value.

**Step 3:** Express Frobenius norm using singular values.

Let $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots, \sigma_r$ be the singular values of $A$. Then:

$$\|A\|_F^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$$

$$\|A\|_2^2 = \max_i \sigma_i^2$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

Since $\sigma_i^2 \leq \max_i \sigma_i^2$ for all $i$, summing over all $i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2 \leq r \cdot \max_i \sigma_i^2$$

However, since each $\sigma_i^2 \geq 0$ and there are $r \geq 1$ terms:

$$\max_i \sigma_i^2 \leq \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$$

**Step 5:** Take square roots.

$$\|A\|_2 \leq \|A\|_F \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Equivalence of Norms Theorem.**

For any two norms $\|\cdot\|_a$ and $\|\cdot\|_b$ on a finite-dimensional vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$, there exist positive constants $c, C > 0$ such that for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$c \|\mathbf{x}\|_a \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_b \leq C \|\mathbf{x}\|_a$$

**Step 2: Proof Sketch.**

Since the unit sphere $\{\mathbf{x} : \|\mathbf{x}\|_a = 1\}$ is **compact** (closed and bounded) in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the continuous function $\|\cdot\|_b$ attains its minimum $c > 0$ and maximum $C$ on this sphere.

**Step 3: Concrete Example for $\mathbb{R}^2$.**

For $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^T$:

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_1 \leq \sqrt{2} \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_1$$

$$\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \leq \sqrt{2} \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.** All norms on $\mathbb{R}^n$ define the **same topology**, meaning convergence in one norm implies convergence in all others. This justifies using L2 as a default in ML without loss of generality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- ✅ A **norm** must satisfy **3 axioms**: Non-negativity, Absolute Homogeneity, Triangle Inequality.
- ✅ **L1 norm** = Sum of absolute values $\Rightarrow$ **Lasso Regularization** (induces sparsity).
- ✅ **L2 norm** = Square root of sum of squares $\Rightarrow$ **Ridge Regularization / MSE**.
- ✅ **L∞ norm** = Maximum absolute component $\Rightarrow$ **Max-error / Adversarial robustness**.
- ✅ **L0 "norm" is a MISNOMER** — it does NOT satisfy homogeneity and is **NOT a true norm**.
- ✅ **Frobenius norm** applies to **matrices only**: $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} a_{ij}^2}$.
- ✅ **Spectral norm** = largest singular value = $\sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(A^T A)}$.
- ✅ **Inequality chain for vectors:** $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 \leq \sqrt{n} \|\mathbf{x}\|_2$.
- ✅ **Equivalence of norms** guarantees all norms on $\mathbb{R}^n$ define the same topology.
- ✅ **Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality** ($\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{y} \leq \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$) is the key tool to prove the triangle inequality.
- ✅ The dot product identity: $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} = \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2$ is frequently tested.
- ✅ **Minkowski's Inequality** is the formal name for the triangle inequality generalized to Lp norms.
- ✅ Always state the **ML application** when answering norm questions — KTU awards application marks explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inner Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inner Product (Scalar Product)**
> An **inner product** on a real or complex vector space $V$ is a function that maps every ordered pair of vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in V$ to a scalar (real number in $\mathbb{R}$ or complex number in $\mathbb{C}$), denoted by $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$, satisfying the following four fundamental axioms for all vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \in V$ and all scalars $c \in \mathbb{F}$ (where $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$):

**Axiom 1 — Conjugate Symmetry (Hermitian Property):**
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \overline{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle}$$
where the overline denotes the complex conjugate. For real vector spaces, this reduces to the symmetry property $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle$.

**Axiom 2 — Linearity in the First Argument (Additivity and Homogeneity):**
$$\langle c\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = c\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle + \langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$$

**Axiom 3 — Conjugate Linearity in the Second Argument:**
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, c\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w} \rangle = \overline{c}\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle + \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{w} \rangle$$

**Axiom 4 — Positive Definiteness:**
$$\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0 \iff \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inner Product Space (IPS):** A vector space $V$ equipped with a valid inner product is called an **Inner Product Space**, often written as the pair $(V, \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle)$. A complete inner product space is called a **Hilbert Space**, which is foundational in functional analysis, quantum mechanics, and kernel-based machine learning.

## 1.2 The Standard Inner Product on $\mathbb{R}^n$

For column vectors $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the **Euclidean Inner Product** (also called the dot product) is defined as:

$$\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + \cdots + x_n y_n$$

The corresponding matrix form is:
$$\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** Let $\mathbf{x} = (1, 2, 3)^T$ and $\mathbf{y} = (4, -5, 6)^T$. Then $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = (1)(4) + (2)(-5) + (3)(6) = 4 - 10 + 18 = 12$.

## 1.3 The Standard Inner Product on $\mathbb{C}^n$

For complex column vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{C}^n$, the standard inner product involves the **conjugate transpose** $\mathbf{x}^H$ (Hermitian transpose):

$$\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{x_i} y_i = \overline{x_1} y_1 + \overline{x_2} y_2 + \cdots + \overline{x_n} y_n$$

The complex conjugate must be applied to the **first** argument's components to ensure positive definiteness: $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{x_i} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^2 \geq 0$.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture — "Alignment Detector"**
> Think of the inner product as a sophisticated **alignment detector** between two vectors in space. Imagine two arrows originating from the same point:
> - **Both pointing in the exact same direction** → Inner product is large and positive (maximum alignment).
> - **Perpendicular to each other** → Inner product is exactly **zero** (no alignment whatsoever).
> - **Pointing in completely opposite directions** → Inner product is large and negative (perfect anti-alignment).
> - **One vector is the zero vector** → Inner product is zero (no direction to align with).

**Real-World Engineering Analogy — The "Similarity Score":**
Consider two **MP3 audio fingerprints** of songs. The inner product computes a single number that tells you "how much do these two signals look alike?" This is precisely the foundation of:
1. **Cosine similarity** in recommender systems (Netflix, YouTube).
2. **Dot-product attention** in Transformer neural networks (GPT, BERT).
3. **Kernel functions** in Support Vector Machines (SVMs).
4. **Convolution operations** in Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image recognition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation Mapping in KTU Examinations:**
> - Mathematics textbooks: $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$
> - Physics textbooks: $(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v})$ for real spaces
> - Quantum mechanics: $\langle \mathbf{u} \mid \mathbf{v} \rangle$ (Dirac bra-ket notation)
> - Linear algebra: $\mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{v}$ (matrix form)
> All of these denote the same fundamental operation.

## 1.5 Other Important Examples of Inner Products

**Example A — Weighted Inner Product on $\mathbb{R}^n$:**
Given a symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ (i.e., $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^T$ and $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$):
$$\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle_{\mathbf{A}} = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{y}$$
This is the inner product in **Mahalanobis distance** computations used in statistical pattern recognition.

**Example B — Inner Product on the Space of Continuous Functions $C[a, b]$:**
For continuous real-valued functions $f, g$ defined on the interval $[a, b]$:
$$\langle f, g \rangle = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) g(x) \, dx$$
This converts the abstract space of functions into a usable inner product space, foundational in **Fourier analysis** and **functional approximation theory**.

**Example C — Inner Product on Polynomial Spaces:**
For polynomials $p(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_n x^n$ and $q(x) = b_0 + b_1 x + \cdots + b_n x^n$:
$$\langle p, q \rangle = \sum_{k=0}^{n} a_k b_k$$
This is the **coefficient inner product** widely used in least-squares polynomial regression.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Axioms — Operational Logic Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding the axioms is essential for KTU 2024 scheme questions on verifying whether a given bilinear form qualifies as a valid inner product.

### Axiom 1 — Conjugate Symmetry: $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \overline{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle}$

**Operational Logic:**
- The "WHY": This axiom guarantees that $\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$ is always a real-valued non-negative number, because $\overline{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle} = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$ forces it to be real.
- The "HOW": If we defined $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \sum x_i y_i$ in $\mathbb{C}^n$ (without conjugation), then for $\mathbf{v} = (i, 0, \ldots, 0)^T$, we would get $\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = i \cdot i = -1$, **violating positive definiteness**. The fix is to conjugate the first argument.
- **KTU Pitfall**: Students often conjugate the wrong vector. Remember: the **first argument** gets conjugated, not the second.

### Axiom 2 — Linearity in the First Argument

**Operational Logic:**
- This is the same additivity and homogeneity that defines a linear map, restricted to one slot of the bilinear function.
- It is also called **left-linearity** or simply **linearity in $\mathbf{u}$**.
- It allows us to "pull scalars out" of the left vector: $\langle 5\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 5\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$.

### Axiom 3 — Conjugate Linearity in the Second Argument

**Operational Logic:**
- Scalars come out **conjugated** when extracted from the second argument: $\langle \mathbf{u}, c\mathbf{v} \rangle = \overline{c} \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$.
- This is the **anti-linear** (or **conjugate-linear**) property.
- In real vector spaces, $\overline{c} = c$, so the inner product becomes **bilinear** (linear in both arguments).

### Axiom 4 — Positive Definiteness

**Operational Logic:**
- The "WHY": This single axiom transforms a vector space into a **measurable** space where we can talk about lengths, angles, and distances.
- The "HOW": Without it, we could not define the norm $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}$ as a real number.
- It implies that **only the zero vector is self-orthogonal** (i.e., $\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$).

## 2.2 The Induced Norm (Vector Length)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Norm Induced by an Inner Product:**
> Every inner product induces a natural **norm** (generalized length) on the space:
> $$\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert v_i \vert^2}$$
> This is called the **Euclidean norm** or **$\ell_2$-norm**. It satisfies the three norm axioms: non-negativity, homogeneity, and triangle inequality.

The squared norm has a beautiful matrix form:
$$\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2 \quad (\text{real case})$$
$$\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \mathbf{v}^H \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert v_i \vert^2 \quad (\text{complex case})$$

## 2.3 The Distance Metric

Once a norm is defined, a **distance** (or **metric**) between two vectors follows naturally:
$$d(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v} \rangle} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (u_i - v_i)^2}$$

This is precisely the familiar **Euclidean distance** formula from coordinate geometry, generalized to $n$ dimensions.

## 2.4 The Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality (Foundational Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality):**
> For any two vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}$ in an inner product space $V$,
> $$\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$$
> with **equality if and only if** $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are **linearly dependent** (i.e., $\mathbf{u} = c\mathbf{v}$ for some scalar $c$).

**Why this is fundamental:**
- It bounds the inner product by the product of the magnitudes.
- It guarantees that the cosine of the angle between two vectors is well-defined: $-1 \leq \cos\theta \leq 1$.
- It is the bedrock proof of the **triangle inequality** for norms.

## 2.5 The Angle Between Two Vectors

Using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the **angle** $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ between two non-zero vectors is well-defined as:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert}$$

**Special cases:**
- $\theta = 0$ (parallel) ⟺ $\cos\theta = 1$ ⟺ $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$
- $\theta = \pi/2$ (perpendicular/orthogonal) ⟺ $\cos\theta = 0$ ⟺ $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$
- $\theta = \pi$ (anti-parallel) ⟺ $\cos\theta = -1$ ⟺ $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = -\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$

## 2.6 Orthogonality and Orthonormal Sets

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Orthogonal Vectors:**
> Two vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}$ are **orthogonal** (denoted $\mathbf{u} \perp \mathbf{v}$) if and only if:
> $$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Orthonormal Set:**
> A set of vectors $\{\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_k\}$ is called **orthonormal** if it satisfies both conditions:
> 1. **Orthogonality:** $\langle \mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_j \rangle = 0$ for all $i \neq j$
> 2. **Normalization:** $\Vert \mathbf{e}_i \Vert = 1$ for all $i$ (equivalently, $\langle \mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_i \rangle = 1$)
>
> Compactly, this can be written as: $\langle \mathbf{e}_i, \mathbf{e}_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta).

**Significance in Machine Learning:**
- Orthonormal bases simplify projections and reduce numerical instability.
- They form the mathematical basis of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**, **QR decomposition**, and the **attention mechanism** in deep learning.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!TIP]

|---|---------|-------------------|---------------------|

| 2 | Standard complex inner product | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{x_i} y_i$ | $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{C}^n$ |
| 3 | Matrix form (real) | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Real vectors |
| 4 | Matrix form (complex) | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \mathbf{x}^H \mathbf{y}$ | Complex vectors, $H$ = conjugate transpose |
| 5 | Induced norm | $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}$ | Always non-negative real |
| 6 | Squared norm | $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \sum v_i^2$ | Real case |

| 14 | Weighted inner product | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle_{\mathbf{A}} = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{A}$ symmetric positive definite |
| 15 | Cosine similarity (ML) | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Normalized in $[-1, 1]$ |
