**Step 4: Termination** — The process halts at $k = n$, producing $n$ mutually orthogonal (or orthonormal) vectors.

---

### 2.2 Modified Gram-Schmidt (MGS) — Numerically Stable Variant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks about the **instability of classical Gram-Schmidt**. The classical version suffers from **round-off error accumulation** due to non-orthogonality drift. MGS reorders operations to compute projections one at a time against the *most up-to-date* orthogonal vector, dramatically improving numerical stability.

$$u_k^{(0)} = v_k$$
$$u_k^{(j)} = u_k^{(j-1)} - \frac{\langle u_k^{(j-1)}, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} u_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, k-1$$
$$u_k = u_k^{(k-1)}$$

---

### 2.3 Determinant — Core Properties

| Property | Formula | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Multiplicativity | $\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)$ | Sequential transformations multiply volumes |
| Transpose Invariance | $\det(A^T) = \det(A)$ | Row and column operations preserve volume scaling |
| Inverse Relation | $\det(A^{-1}) = \dfrac{1}{\det(A)}$ | Undoing a transformation inverts the scaling |
| Scalar Pull-out | $\det(\lambda A) = \lambda^n \det(A)$ | Scales every dimension by $\lambda$ |
| Singularity Test | $\det(A) = 0 \iff A$ is singular | Collapsed transformations have zero volume |
| Eigenvalue Product | $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Product of all eigenvalues |
| Row Swap | $\det$ changes sign | Each swap flips orientation |
| Triangular | $\det(A) = \prod a_{ii}$ | Diagonal product for triangular matrices |

---

### 2.4 Trace — Core Properties

| Property | Formula | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linearity | $\text{tr}(A + B) = \text{tr}(A) + \text{tr}(B)$ | Diagonal sums combine additively |
| Scalar Pull-out | $\text{tr}(\lambda A) = \lambda \cdot \text{tr}(A)$ | Scalar factors pass through |
| Transpose Invariance | $\text{tr}(A^T) = \text{tr}(A)$ | Diagonal unchanged by transpose |
| **Cyclic Property** | $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$ | Trace invariant under cyclic permutation |
| Eigenvalue Sum | $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Sum of eigenvalues |
| Frobenius Norm | $\|A\|_F^2 = \text{tr}(A^T A)$ | Squared Frobenius norm via trace |
| Similarity Invariance | $\text{tr}(P^{-1}AP) = \text{tr}(A)$ | Trace is a similarity invariant |

---

### 2.5 The Fundamental Trace-Determinant-Eigenvalue Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an $n \times n$ matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$:
> $$\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i, \qquad \text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$
> These two scalars completely determine the **characteristic polynomial** of a $2 \times 2$ matrix: $\lambda^2 - \text{tr}(A)\lambda + \det(A) = 0$.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **QR Decomposition via Gram-Schmidt** is the workhorse for solving **least-squares problems** in linear regression, signal processing, and control systems.
- **Determinant** appears in the **Jacobian** of multivariate transformations, critical in robotics (manipulability index) and computer vision (homography).
- **Trace** is foundational in **deep learning attention mechanisms** and **kernel methods** (e.g., $\text{tr}(K) = \sum$ eigenvalues of kernel matrix).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization Formula

**Goal:** Show that $u_k = v_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} u_j$ is orthogonal to all $u_i$ for $i < k$.

**Setup:** Assume $u_1, \dots, u_{k-1}$ are mutually orthogonal (inductive hypothesis).

**Step 1:** Compute $\langle u_k, u_i \rangle$ for any $i < k$.

$$\langle u_k, u_i \rangle = \left\langle v_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} u_j, \; u_i \right\rangle$$

**Step 2:** Apply linearity of inner product (the first property of inner product spaces).

$$\langle u_k, u_i \rangle = \langle v_k, u_i \rangle - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} \langle u_j, u_i \rangle$$

**Step 3:** Use the orthogonality of $u_j$ and $u_i$. Since $j$ ranges over $1, \dots, k-1$ and $i < k$, the term $\langle u_j, u_i \rangle = 0$ for all $j \neq i$, and $\langle u_j, u_i \rangle = \langle u_j, u_j \rangle$ for $j = i$.

$$\langle u_k, u_i \rangle = \langle v_k, u_i \rangle - \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} \langle u_i, u_i \rangle$$

**Step 4:** Simplify. The second term equals $\langle v_k, u_i \rangle$.

$$\langle u_k, u_i \rangle = \langle v_k, u_i \rangle - \langle v_k, u_i \rangle = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conclusion:** $u_k$ is orthogonal to all previous $u_i$. By induction, the full set $\{u_1, \dots, u_n\}$ is mutually orthogonal.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Gram-Schmidt on $\mathbb{R}^3$

Let $v_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$, $v_2 = (1, 0, 1)^T$, $v_3 = (0, 1, 1)^T$.

**Step 1:** $u_1 = v_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$.

$\|u_1\| = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2}$, so $e_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T$.

**Step 2:** Compute $u_2$.

$\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle = (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$

$\langle u_1, u_1 \rangle = 2$

$u_2 = v_2 - \frac{1}{2} u_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T - \frac{1}{2}(1, 1, 0)^T = \left(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T$

**Verification:** $\langle u_2, u_1 \rangle = (\frac{1}{2})(1) + (-\frac{1}{2})(1) + (1)(0) = 0$ ✓

$\|u_2\| = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} + 1} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}$, so $e_2 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}\left(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, -1, 2)^T$.

**Step 3:** Compute $u_3$.

$\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle = (0)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$

$\langle v_3, u_2 \rangle = (0)(\frac{1}{2}) + (1)(-\frac{1}{2}) + (1)(1) = \frac{1}{2}$

$u_3 = v_3 - \frac{1}{2}u_1 - \frac{1/2}{3/2}u_2 = (0, 1, 1)^T - \frac{1}{2}(1, 1, 0)^T - \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T$

$u_3 = \left(-\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{6},\; \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{6},\; 1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^T = \left(-\frac{2}{3},\; \frac{2}{3},\; \frac{2}{3}\right)^T$

$u_3 = \frac{2}{3}(-1, 1, 1)^T$

**Verification of orthogonality:** 
- $\langle u_3, u_1 \rangle = \frac{2}{3}[(-1)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(0)] = \frac{2}{3}(0) = 0$ ✓
- $\langle u_3, u_2 \rangle = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{2}[(-1)(1) + (1)(-1) + (1)(2)] = \frac{2}{3}[(-1-1+2)] \cdot (\text{after normalization}) = 0$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cyclic Property of Trace

**Claim:** $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$ for conformable matrices $A, B, C$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Expand $\text{tr}(ABC)$ using the definition $\text{tr}(M) = \sum_i M_{ii}$.

$$\text{tr}(ABC) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (ABC)_{ii} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{ij} B_{jk} C_{ki}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the triple sum. The summand $A_{ij} B_{jk} C_{ki}$ is a product of three scalars, so summation order is interchangeable.

$$\text{tr}(ABC) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{ki} A_{ij} B_{jk}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the inner double sum as the $(k, k)$-th element of the matrix product $CAB$.

$(CAB)_{kk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ki} A_{ij} B_{jk}$

**Step 4:** Therefore,

$$\text{tr}(ABC) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (CAB)_{kk} = \text{tr}(CAB) \quad \blacksquare$$

The other cyclic permutation $\text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(ABC)$ follows by the same re-indexing argument.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Determinant and Trace of a $3 \times 3$ Matrix

Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Determinant via Cofactor Expansion along Row 1:**

$$\det(A) = 2 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} - 1 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} + 3 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Evaluating each $2 \times 2$ minor:**

- $\det\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = (-1)(5) - (4)(2) = -5 - 8 = -13$

- $\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = (0)(5) - (4)(1) = -4$

- $\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = (0)(2) - (-1)(1) = 1$

**Combining:**

$$\det(A) = 2(-13) - 1(-4) + 3(1) = -26 + 4 + 3 = -19$$

**Trace:**

$$\text{tr}(A) = 2 + (-1) + 5 = 6$$

**Characteristic Polynomial Verification:**
For a $3 \times 3$ matrix, $\lambda^3 - \text{tr}(A)\lambda^2 + (\text{sum of principal } 2 \times 2 \text{ minors})\lambda - \det(A) = 0$.

- Sum of principal $2 \times 2$ minors: $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} + \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} + \det\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = -2 + 7 + (-13) = -8$

So: $\lambda^3 - 6\lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 19 = 0$ is the characteristic polynomial of $A$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Gram-Schmidt Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Linearly Independent Vectors v1 v2 v3 ... vn"] --> B["INITIALIZE: Set u1 = v1, Compute e1 = u1 over norm of u1"]
    B --> C["ITERATION K: For k = 2 to n"]
    C --> D["PROJECT: Compute inner product of vk with each previous uj"]
    D --> E["SUBTRACT: Form uk = vk minus sum of projections onto uj"]
    E --> F["VERIFY: Check orthogonality of uk with all previous ui"]
    F --> G["NORMALIZE: Compute ek = uk over norm of uk"]
    G --> H{"More vectors to process?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> C
    H -- "No" --> I["OUTPUT: Orthonormal Basis e1 e2 ... en"]
    I --> J["CONSTRUCT: Q Matrix with columns e1 e2 ... en"]
    J --> K["APPLICATION: QR Decomposition A = QR for Least Squares"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Determinant, Trace, and Eigenvalues

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Matrix A of size n by n"] --> B["Characteristic Polynomial p of lambda"]
    B --> C["Roots lambda 1 to lambda n of p of lambda"]
    C --> D["DETERMINANT: Product of all eigenvalues"]
    C --> E["TRACE: Sum of all eigenvalues"]
    D --> F["Geometric Meaning: Signed n-dimensional volume scaling"]
    E --> G["Geometric Meaning: Net axis-aligned stretching"]
    F --> H["APPLICATION: Singularity test, Jacobian, Change of variables"]
    G --> I["APPLICATION: Frobenius norm, Kernel methods, Loss functions"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#17a2b8,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Modified Gram-Schmidt (Numerical Stability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: v1 v2 ... vn"] --> B["k equals 1: u1 = v1"]
    B --> C["k equals 2: u2 = v2 minus proj of v2 on u1"]
    C --> D["k equals 3: v3 minus proj on u1, then proj on u2 SEQUENTIALLY"]
    D --> E["General uk: Sequentially subtract projections one by one using updated vectors"]
    E --> F["COMPARE: Classical GS subtracts all projections simultaneously"]
    F --> G["ADVANTAGE: MGS reduces round-off error accumulation"]
    G --> H["OUTPUT: Numerically Stable Orthogonal Basis"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define **Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization** and write the formula to compute $u_2$ from the linearly independent vectors $v_1$ and $v_2$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization is an iterative procedure that converts a set of linearly independent vectors $\{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ into an orthogonal set $\{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n\}$ by sequentially subtracting from each vector its projection onto all previously constructed orthogonal vectors.

> **Formula for $u_2$ (1.5 Marks):** 
> $$u_1 = v_1, \qquad u_2 = v_2 - \frac{\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle}{\langle u_1, u_1 \rangle} u_1$$

---

**Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

State **any four properties** of the trace of a matrix.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. $\text{tr}(A + B) = \text{tr}(A) + \text{tr}(B)$ — **Linearity (0.75 Marks)**
2. $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$ — **Cyclic Property (0.75 Marks)**
3. $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ — **Sum of Eigenvalues (0.75 Marks)**
4. $\text{tr}(A^T) = \text{tr}(A)$ — **Transpose Invariance (0.75 Marks)**

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question 1A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Apply the **Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization procedure** to the vectors $v_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$, $v_2 = (1, 0, 1)^T$, and $v_3 = (1, 1, 1)^T$ to obtain an **orthonormal basis** for $\mathbb{R}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If $Q$ is the matrix whose columns are the orthonormal vectors obtained in part (a), **verify that $Q^T Q = I$** and compute $\det(Q)$. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $u_1$ and $e_1$ (2 Marks)**
$u_1 = v_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$
$\|u_1\| = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2}$
$e_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T$

**Step 2: Compute $u_2$ and $e_2$ (2.5 Marks)**
$\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle = (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$
$\langle u_1, u_1 \rangle = 2$
$u_2 = v_2 - \frac{1}{2} u_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T - (\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}, 0)^T = (\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1)^T$
$\|u_2\| = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} + 1} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}$
$e_2 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1)^T = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, -1, 2)^T$

**Step 3: Compute $u_3$ and $e_3$ (2.5 Marks)**
$\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle = (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(0) = 2$
$\langle v_3, u_2 \rangle = (1)(\frac{1}{2}) + (1)(-\frac{1}{2}) + (1)(1) = 1$
$u_3 = v_3 - \frac{2}{2} u_1 - \frac{1}{3/2} u_2 = (1, 1, 1)^T - (1, 1, 0)^T - \frac{2}{3}(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}, 1)^T$
$u_3 = (0, 0, 1)^T - (\frac{1}{3}, -\frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3})^T = (-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3})^T = \frac{1}{3}(-1, 1, 1)^T$
$\|u_3\| = \frac{1}{3}\sqrt{1+1+1} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$e_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{1}{3}(-1, 1, 1)^T \cdot \text{after normalization} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(-1, 1, 1)^T$

**Orthonormal Basis:**
$$\left\{ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T, \; \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, -1, 2)^T, \; \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(-1, 1, 1)^T \right\}$$

**(b) Verification of $Q^T Q = I$ and $\det(Q)$ (7 Marks):**

**Forming $Q$ (1 Mark):**
$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ 0 & 2/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{3} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Computing $Q^T Q$ (3 Marks):**
Each entry $(Q^T Q)_{ij} = \langle e_i, e_j \rangle$. By construction, $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta):
- Diagonal entries: $\|e_i\|^2 = 1$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$.
- Off-diagonal entries: $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle = 0$ for $i \neq j$ (verified by orthogonality).

$$Q^T Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_{3 \times 3}$$

**Computing $\det(Q)$ (2 Marks):**
$\det(Q) = \det(Q^T)$ since $\det(Q^T) = \det(Q)$. So $\det(Q)^2 = \det(Q^T Q) = \det(I) = 1$. Thus $\det(Q) = \pm 1$.

Direct computation: Each column of $Q$ is normalized, so volume = 1 in magnitude. Checking orientation (the columns form a right-handed system based on the construction), $\det(Q) = +1$.

**Comment (1 Mark):** The matrix $Q$ is an **orthogonal matrix** (a member of the orthogonal group $O(3)$). Since $\det(Q) = +1$, it belongs to the **special orthogonal group $SO(3)$**, representing a **pure rotation** in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with no reflection.

---

### **Question 1B (Alternative, 14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** For the matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $\det(A)$ and $\text{tr}(A)$ using **cofactor expansion** and **direct diagonal summation** respectively. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove the **cyclic property of trace**: $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(CAB)$ for any three conformable square matrices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Determinant and Trace Computation (7 Marks):**

**Trace (1.5 Marks):**
$$\text{tr}(A) = 3 + 2 + 5 = 10$$

**Determinant via Cofactor Expansion along Row 1 (5.5 Marks):**

$$\det(A) = 3 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} - 1 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} + 0 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Evaluating each minor:**

- $\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = (2)(5) - (4)(0) = 10$ (2 Marks)

- $\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = (0)(5) - (4)(1) = -4$ (2 Marks)

- Third term = 0 (1.5 Marks)

**Combining:**
$$\det(A) = 3(10) - 1(-4) + 0 = 30 + 4 = 34$$

**(b) Proof of Cyclic Property of Trace (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ are conformable.

**To Prove:** $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(CAB)$.

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Express trace using element-wise summation.
$$\text{tr}(ABC) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (ABC)_{ii} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{k=1}^{p} A_{ij} B_{jk} C_{ki}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Rearrange the summation. Since the summand is a product of three scalars, summation order is interchangeable.
$$= \sum_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{k=1}^{p} \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{ki} A_{ij} B_{jk}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Recognize the inner double sum as the $(k,k)$-th element of $CAB$.
$$(CAB)_{kk} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{ki} A_{ij} B_{jk}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclude.
$$\text{tr}(ABC) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} (CAB)_{kk} = \text{tr}(CAB) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize:** In Gram-Schmidt, students often compute the orthogonal set $\{u_k\}$ but forget to divide by $\|u_k\|$. If the question asks for **orthonormal**, you lose **2 marks** immediately. Always read carefully: "orthogonal" vs "orthonormal."
> 2. **Wrong sign in cofactor expansion:** The cofactor expansion alternates signs $(+, -, +, -, \dots)$. Forgetting the alternating sign pattern is the **#1 determinant mistake** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Cyclic ≠ Commutative:** The cyclic property $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(BCA) = \text{tr}(CAB)$ does **NOT** mean trace is fully commutative. $\text{tr}(AB) \neq \text{tr}(BA)$ in general, wait — actually $\text{tr}(AB) = \text{tr}(BA)$ is true for square matrices, but be careful with rectangular cases.
> 4. **Confusing row vs column operations:** Cofactor expansion can be along **any row or column** — pick the one with the most zeros to minimize computation. KTU examiners award **partial credit** for choosing strategically.
> 5. **Not verifying orthogonality:** In Gram-Schmidt problems, always include a one-line verification $\langle u_k, u_i \rangle = 0$. It earns you **1 bonus mark** and shows conceptual clarity.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Gram-Schmidt** converts linearly independent vectors to orthogonal (or orthonormal) vectors via sequential projection subtraction.
- The **classical Gram-Schmidt** formula is: $u_k = v_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} u_j$.
- **Modified Gram-Schmidt (MGS)** is numerically more stable and preferred in computational implementations.
- The **QR decomposition** $A = QR$ arises directly from Gram-Schmidt applied to columns of $A$.
- The **determinant** is multiplicative: $\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)$, and equals the **product of eigenvalues**.
- $\det(A) = 0$ if and only if $A$ is **singular** (linearly dependent rows/columns).
- The **trace** is the **sum of diagonal elements** and equals the **sum of eigenvalues**.
- The **cyclic property** of trace: $\text{tr}(ABC) = \text{tr}(CAB) = \text{tr}(BCA)$.
- For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, the characteristic polynomial is $\lambda^2 - \text{tr}(A)\lambda + \det(A) = 0$.
- **Orthogonal matrices** satisfy $Q^T Q = I$ and $\det(Q) = \pm 1$; those with $\det(Q) = +1$ form $SO(n)$ (rotations).
- Gram-Schmidt assumes **linearly independent** input; if vectors are dependent, the algorithm breaks (division by zero norm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Mathematical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> Let **A** be a square matrix of order **n × n** over the field of real numbers (**ℝ**) or complex numbers (**ℂ**). A non-zero vector **x** in **ℂⁿ** is called an **eigenvector** of **A** if there exists a scalar **λ ∈ ℂ** (called the **eigenvalue** corresponding to **x**) such that the following matrix equation is satisfied:

$$A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}, \quad \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$$

In this equation:
- **A** is the *transformer matrix* (the system under study).
- **x** is the *eigenvector* (a special direction that survives the transformation).
- **λ** is the *eigenvalue* (a scalar that simply *stretches* or *shrinks* **x** without rotating it).

The word **"Eigen"** is German for *"self"* or *"characteristic"*. Hence, eigenvalues and eigenvectors are also called **characteristic values** and **characteristic vectors**, respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Stretching a Rubber Sheet":**
> Imagine you have a **square rubber sheet** pinned at the origin. When you apply a linear transformation (say, *shearing* it), almost every point on the sheet **changes direction** in some complicated way. However, there are a few *magical* directions (the eigenvectors) along which points simply **slide outward, inward, or stay put** — they never rotate off their own axis. The *amount* they slide is governed by the eigenvalue **λ**.
> - If **λ > 1** → vector is **stretched** outward.
> - If **0 < λ < 1** → vector is **shrunk** inward.
> - If **λ = 1** → vector is **unchanged**.
> - If **λ < 0** → vector is **flipped** to the opposite direction.
> - If **λ = 0** → vector is **collapsed** to the origin.

In Machine Learning, this concept is *the* fundamental engine behind:
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — finding directions of maximum variance.
- **Spectral Clustering** — using the *spectrum* (set of eigenvalues) to detect clusters.
- **Google's PageRank Algorithm** — dominant eigenvalue of the link matrix.
- **Stability of Neural Networks** — eigenvalues of the Jacobian determine convergence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential vocabulary for KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Spectrum (σ(A))**: The complete set of all eigenvalues of **A**.
> - **Spectral Radius (ρ(A))**: The *maximum absolute* eigenvalue, $\rho(A) = \max_{i} \vert \lambda_i \vert$.
> - **Characteristic Polynomial**: The polynomial $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$ of degree **n**.
> - **Multiplicity**:
>   - *Algebraic Multiplicity (AM)* — how many times **λ** is a root of $p(\lambda)$.
>   - *Geometric Multiplicity (GM)* — dimension of the eigenspace, i.e., $\dim(\ker(A - \lambda I))$.
> - **Diagonalizable Matrix**: A matrix is diagonalizable if and only if it has **n** linearly independent eigenvectors.
> - **Standard Identity Matrix**: **I** (denoted as **Iₙ** for clarity of size).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How to Find Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

The procedure is a **four-stage pipeline**. Every KTU numerical problem on this topic will require a student to march through this pipeline without skipping stages.

### Stage 1: Formulate the Characteristic Equation
Starting from $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$, we rewrite as $(A - \lambda I) \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

For a non-trivial solution ($\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$) to exist, the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ **must be singular** (non-invertible). Therefore:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This is called the **Characteristic Equation** of **A**, and expanding it gives the **Characteristic Polynomial** of degree **n**.

### Stage 2: Solve the Characteristic Polynomial
Find the **n** roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$ of the polynomial. These are the **eigenvalues** of **A**.

### Stage 3: Solve the Homogeneous System
For each $\lambda_i$, plug it back into $(A - \lambda_i I)\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ and solve the resulting linear system using **Gaussian elimination**. The solution space is the **Eigenspace** $E_{\lambda_i}$.

### Stage 4: Express the Eigenvector
Any non-zero vector in $E_{\lambda_i}$ is an eigenvector for $\lambda_i$. By KTU convention, students are expected to **normalize** eigenvectors (or at least present them in their simplest integer/rational form).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why set $\det = 0$?** — A homogeneous system $M\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ has a non-trivial solution **iff** the determinant of $M$ is zero. Since we *need* $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (by definition of eigenvector), this condition is unavoidable.
- **Why at most n distinct eigenvalues?** — The characteristic polynomial of an **n × n** matrix has degree **n**, hence at most **n** complex roots (by the **Fundamental Theorem of Algebra**).
- **Why do complex eigenvalues appear in conjugate pairs for real A?** — A real-coefficient polynomial has roots that are either real or occur in complex conjugate pairs (this is critical for KTU's 3D transformation problems).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — Memorize this block before any KTU exam.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Conditions / Remarks** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Core Equation | $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{C}^{n}$ |
| 2 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | Singular matrix condition |
| 3 | Characteristic Polynomial | $p(\lambda) = (-1)^{n} \lambda^{n} + \dots + \det(A)$ | Degree is exactly $n$ |
| 4 | Sum of Eigenvalues | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{trace}(A)$ | Equals the trace |
| 5 | Product of Eigenvalues | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$ | Equals the determinant |
| 6 | Eigenvalues of $A^{k}$ | $\lambda_{i}^{k}$ (each eigenvalue raised) | Same eigenvectors as $A$ |
| 7 | Eigenvalues of $A^{-1}$ | $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$ | Exists only if $\det(A) \neq 0$ |
| 8 | Eigenvalues of $A + cI$ | $\lambda_{i} + c$ | Same eigenvectors, scalar shift |
| 9 | Eigenvalues of $cA$ | $c \cdot \lambda_{i}$ | Scalar multiplication |
| 10 | Eigenvalues of $A^{T}$ | Identical to $A$ | $\sigma(A^{T}) = \sigma(A)$ |
| 11 | Diagonalization | $A = PDP^{-1}$ | $D = \text{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n)$ |
| 12 | Cayley–Hamilton | $p(A) = 0$ | Matrix satisfies its own polynomial |
| 13 | Spectral Radius | $\rho(A) = \max_{i} \vert \lambda_{i} \vert$ | Used in convergence analysis |
| 14 | Definiteness | $\lambda_i > 0 \ \forall i \iff A$ is **positive definite** | Vital in optimization (ML cost) |
| 15 | Symmetric Real Matrix | All $\lambda_i \in \mathbb{R}$ | Real symmetric ⇒ orthogonal diagonalization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU cares about this in a Machine Learning course:**
> 1. **Covariance Matrix in PCA**: The eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix are the *principal components* (directions of maximum spread).
> 2. **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)**: Used in image compression, recommendation systems; relies entirely on eigen-decomposition.
> 3. **Markov Chains & PageRank**: Steady-state vector is the eigenvector corresponding to eigenvalue **1** of the stochastic transition matrix.
> 4. **Stability of Dynamic Systems**: In control theory, the eigenvalues of the system matrix $A$ dictate whether a linear system $x'(t) = Ax$ *grows*, *decays*, or *oscillates* (real part positive/negative/zero).
> 5. **Google Search Algorithm**: Dominant eigenvector of the link graph gives the ranking vector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, mark-fetching** derivations that KTU examiners reward. *No step is skipped.*

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A Complete 3×3 Eigen-Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Standard KTU Board Type — 14 Marks):**
> Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix:
> $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$
> Also verify that $A$ is diagonalizable, and find the matrix $P$ and $D$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Characteristic Equation
We compute $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.

$$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Expand the Determinant (Co-factor Expansion Along Row 1)

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2 - \lambda) \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} - (-1) \cdot \begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} + 0 \cdot (\dots)$$

We evaluate each $2 \times 2$ minor:

$$\begin{vmatrix} 2 - \lambda & -1 \\ -1 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (2 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - (-1)(-1) = (2 - \lambda)^{2} - 1$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (-1)(2 - \lambda) - (-1)(0) = -(2 - \lambda) = \lambda - 2$$

Substituting back:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2 - \lambda)\bigl[(2 - \lambda)^{2} - 1\bigr] + 1 \cdot (\lambda - 2)$$

### Step 3: Factor and Solve
Let $u = (2 - \lambda)$. Note that $(\lambda - 2) = -u$. The expression becomes:

$$u(u^{2} - 1) + (-u) = u^{3} - u - u = u^{3} - 2u = u(u^{2} - 2)$$

Substituting $u = 2 - \lambda$:

$$(2 - \lambda)\bigl[(2 - \lambda)^{2} - 2\bigr] = 0$$

Setting each factor to zero:

- **Factor 1**: $2 - \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 2$
- **Factor 2**: $(2 - \lambda)^{2} - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow (2 - \lambda)^{2} = 2 \Rightarrow 2 - \lambda = \pm\sqrt{2}$

This gives the remaining two eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_2 = 2 - \sqrt{2}, \qquad \lambda_3 = 2 + \sqrt{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of KTU Formula Sheet items (sum and product):**
> - Sum: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 2 + (2 - \sqrt{2}) + (2 + \sqrt{2}) = 6 = \text{trace}(A)$ ✔
> - Product: $2 \cdot (2 - \sqrt{2}) \cdot (2 + \sqrt{2}) = 2 \cdot (4 - 2) = 4 = \det(A)$ ✔

### Step 4: Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 2$
Substitute $\lambda = 2$ into $(A - 2I)\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Reading the rows: $-x_2 = 0$ and $-x_1 - x_3 = 0$. Thus $x_2 = 0$ and $x_1 = -x_3$. Letting $x_3 = t$:

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = t \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{(Choosing } t = 1 \text{ for the canonical eigenvector)}$$

### Step 5: Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2 - \sqrt{2}$
Substitute into $(A - (2 - \sqrt{2})I)\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{2} & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & \sqrt{2} & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From row 1: $\sqrt{2} x_1 - x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = \sqrt{2} x_1$.
From row 3: $-x_2 + \sqrt{2} x_3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = \sqrt{2} x_3$.
Combining: $x_1 = x_3$. Letting $x_1 = 1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ \sqrt{2} \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 6: Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_3 = 2 + \sqrt{2}$
Substitute into $(A - (2 + \sqrt{2})I)\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -\sqrt{2} & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & -\sqrt{2} & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & -\sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From row 1: $-\sqrt{2} x_1 - x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -\sqrt{2} x_1$.
From row 3: $-x_2 - \sqrt{2} x_3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -\sqrt{2} x_3$.
Hence $x_1 = x_3$. Letting $x_1 = 1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -\sqrt{2} \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 7: Construct P and D
The modal matrix $P$ and the diagonal matrix $D$:

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & \sqrt{2} & -\sqrt{2} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad D = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 - \sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 + \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}$$

The matrix **A** is **diagonalizable** because $A = PDP^{-1}$ and $P$ is invertible (its three eigenvectors are linearly independent — they are mutually orthogonal since A is symmetric).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cayley–Hamilton Theorem Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern — 7 Marks):**
> Verify the Cayley–Hamilton theorem for $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ and hence compute $A^{-1}$.

### Step 1: Find the Characteristic Polynomial

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \begin{vmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 2 \\ 3 & 4 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (1 - \lambda)(4 - \lambda) - 6$$

Expanding: $(4 - \lambda - 4\lambda + \lambda^{2}) - 6 = \lambda^{2} - 5\lambda - 2$

The characteristic polynomial is $p(\lambda) = \lambda^{2} - 5\lambda - 2$.

### Step 2: Apply Cayley–Hamilton (Substitute A into its Polynomial)

$$p(A) = A^{2} - 5A - 2I = 0$$

Compute $A^{2}$:

$$A^{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1+6 & 2+8 \\ 3+12 & 6+16 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 10 \\ 15 & 22 \end{pmatrix}$$

Now compute $A^{2} - 5A - 2I$:

$$A^{2} - 5A - 2I = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 10 \\ 15 & 22 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 10 \\ 15 & 20 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Cayley–Hamilton is **verified**. ✔

### Step 3: Find $A^{-1}$ by Rearrangement
From $A^{2} - 5A - 2I = 0$, multiply both sides by $A^{-1}$ on the left (assuming $A$ is invertible):

$$A - 5I - 2A^{-1} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2A^{-1} = A - 5I \quad \Rightarrow \quad A^{-1} = \frac{1}{2}(A - 5I)$$

Compute:

$$A - 5I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 5 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 - 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -4 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} -4 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 3/2 & -1/2 \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Sanity Check:** $\det(A) = 4 - 6 = -2$. The reciprocal of the determinant is $-1/2$, and the adjugate matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. So $A^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 3/2 & -1/2 \end{pmatrix}$. This matches! ✔

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Power Method for Dominant Eigenvalue

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Important for Numerical/ML Application):**
> Using the **Power Method**, find the dominant eigenvalue and corresponding eigenvector of:
> $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$
> Start with $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Perform 3 iterations.

### Step 1: Iteration 1
Compute $\mathbf{y}^{(1)} = A \mathbf{x}^{(0)}$:

$$\mathbf{y}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

The largest-magnitude component is 2 (first entry). So scale by dividing by 2:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

The scaling factor is the **Rayleigh quotient estimate** $\lambda^{(1)} = 2$.

### Step 2: Iteration 2

$$\mathbf{y}^{(2)} = A \mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 0.5 \\ 1 + 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Largest component: 2.5. Scale:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{2.5} \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.8 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \lambda^{(2)} = 2.5$$

### Step 3: Iteration 3

$$\mathbf{y}^{(3)} = A \mathbf{x}^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 0.8 \\ 1 + 1.6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.8 \\ 2.6 \end{pmatrix}$$

Scale:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{2.8} \begin{pmatrix} 2.8 \\ 2.6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.9286 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \lambda^{(3)} \approx 2.8$$

### Step 4: Converged Answer
The sequence is converging to the *true* dominant eigenvalue of **3** and eigenvector $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (which can be confirmed by direct diagonalization: eigenvalues of A are **1** and **3**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Convergence Rate:** The Power Method converges *linearly* with rate $\left\vert \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} \right\vert$. The closer the gap, the slower the convergence. This is why **deflation** and **shifted inverse iteration** are used in production ML libraries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Eigen-Decomposition Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1: Full workflow from input matrix to diagonalized form.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Input Matrix A of order n cross n] --> B0[Subtract lambda I from A]
    B0 --> C0[Compute determinant of A minus lambda I]
    C0 --> D0[Form Characteristic Polynomial p of lambda]
    D0 --> E0[Solve p of lambda equals 0]
    E0 --> F0[Obtain Eigenvalues lambda 1 to lambda n]
    F0 --> G0{For each lambda i do}
    G0 --> H0[Form A minus lambda i I]
    H0 --> I0[Solve Homogeneous System equals zero]
    I0 --> J0[Null Space gives Eigenspace E lambda i]
    J0 --> K0[Extract basis vector as Eigenvector x i]
    K0 --> L0{More lambda values?}
    L0 -- Yes --> G0
    L0 -- No --> M0[Collect all x i into Modal Matrix P]
    M0 --> N0[Form Diagonal Matrix D with lambda i on diagonal]
    N0 --> O0[Verify A equals P times D times P inverse]
    O0 --> P0[End: Diagonalization Complete]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Relationships Between Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2: Concept map showing how eigenvalues interact with matrix properties.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Matrix A] --> B1[Characteristic Polynomial]
    A1 --> C1[Trace of A]
    A1 --> D1[Determinant of A]
    B1 --> E1[Eigenvalues lambda i]
    C1 --> F1[Sum of lambda i equals Trace]
    D1 --> G1[Product of lambda i equals Determinant]
    E1 --> H1[Eigenvectors x i]
    H1 --> I1[Linear Independence Check]
    I1 --> J1{Independent?}
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[Diagonalizable: A equals P D P inverse]
    J1 -- No --> L1[Not Diagonalizable]
    A1 --> M1[Is A Symmetric Real?]
    M1 -- Yes --> N1[Orthogonal Diagonalization]
    M1 -- No --> O1[Possible Complex Eigenvalues]
    E1 --> P1[Power Method Application]
    P1 --> Q1[Find Dominant Eigenvalue]
    E1 --> R1[Cayley Hamilton Theorem]
    R1 --> S1[Compute A inverse and A raised to k]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Power Method Convergence Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 3: Iterative structure of the Power Method for numerical eigen-analysis.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize random vector x 0] --> S1[Iteration k equals 0]
    S1 --> S2[Compute y equals A times x k]
    S2 --> S3[Find max absolute value mu k of y]
    S3 --> S4[Normalize x k plus 1 equals y divided by mu k]
    S4 --> S5[Store eigenvalue estimate lambda k equals mu k]
    S5 --> S6{Convergence reached: difference less than tolerance?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Increment k and loop back to step 2]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Output dominant eigenvalue and eigenvector]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The block diagrams above map the *exact* logical sequences expected in a 14-mark answer. A student who can reproduce any of these three diagrams (with their own labels) and then execute the corresponding math typically secures **12–14/14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define eigenvalues and eigenvectors. State any two properties of eigenvalues.*

#### Model Answer
An **eigenvalue** $\lambda$ of a square matrix $A$ is a scalar such that the equation $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ has a non-trivial solution. The corresponding non-zero vector $\mathbf{x}$ is called an **eigenvector**.

**Two Properties (any two of the following may be written):**
1. The sum of the eigenvalues equals the **trace** of the matrix: $\sum_{i} \lambda_i = \text{trace}(A)$.
2. The product of the eigenvalues equals the **determinant** of the matrix: $\prod_{i} \lambda_i = \det(A)$.
3. If $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $A$, then $\lambda^{k}$ is an eigenvalue of $A^{k}$.
4. If $A$ is symmetric and real, all its eigenvalues are **real**.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*What is the characteristic equation of a matrix? Why is the determinant set to zero?*

#### Model Answer
The **characteristic equation** of an $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is the polynomial equation:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

where $I$ is the identity matrix and $\lambda$ is a scalar. It is obtained by rewriting the eigen-equation $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ in the form $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ and noting that for a non-trivial solution $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ to exist, the matrix $A - \lambda I$ must be **singular** (non-invertible). A matrix is singular **if and only if** its determinant is zero, which is why the condition $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ is imposed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Students must answer **ONE** full question from each module. The two alternatives below are mutually exclusive choices for the same 14-mark slot.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

##### Model Solution

**Step 1: Form the characteristic equation.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \begin{vmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Expand and solve.**

$$12 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^{2} - 2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

Factoring: $(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0$. Thus $\lambda_1 = 5$ and $\lambda_2 = 2$.

**Step 3: Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From row 1: $-x_1 + x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = x_1$. Let $x_1 = 1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Find eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From row 1: $2x_1 + x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -2x_1$. Let $x_1 = 1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Final Answer:** Eigenvalues: $5, 2$. Eigenvectors: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Verify the **Cayley–Hamilton theorem** for the matrix $A$ in part (a) and hence compute $A^{-1}$.

##### Model Solution

**Step 1: Recall the characteristic polynomial from part (a).**

$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10$$

**Step 2: Compute $A^{2}$.**

$$A^{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 16 + 2 & 4 + 3 \\ 8 + 6 & 2 + 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 18 & 7 \\ 14 & 11 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $p(A) = A^{2} - 7A + 10I$.**

$$7A = \begin{pmatrix} 28 & 7 \\ 14 & 21 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad 10I = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$p(A) = \begin{pmatrix} 18 & 7 \\ 14 & 11 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 28 & 7 \\ 14 & 21 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Cayley–Hamilton **verified** ✔.

**Step 4: Compute $A^{-1}$ using rearrangement.** From $A^{2} - 7A + 10I = 0$, multiply by $A^{-1}$:

$$A - 7I + 10A^{-1} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10A^{-1} = 7I - A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A^{-1} = \frac{1}{10}(7I - A)$$

$$7I - A = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{10} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Diagonalize the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and hence compute $A^{5}$.

##### Model Solution

**Step 1: Find the eigenvalues.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (5 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - 4 = \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 6 = (\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1) = 0$$

So $\lambda_1 = 6$ and $\lambda_2 = 1$.

**Step 2: Find the eigenvectors.**

For $\lambda_1 = 6$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -x_1 + 4x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 4x_2$$

Let $x_2 = 1$: $\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

For $\lambda_2 = 1$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 + x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = -x_2$$

Let $x_2 = 1$: $\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 3: Form $P$ and $D$.**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad D = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $P^{-1}$.**

$$\det(P) = (4)(1) - (-1)(1) = 5$$

$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verify $A = PDP^{-1}$.** (Optional but earns full marks.)

$$PDP^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 24 & -1 \\ 6 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 25 & 20 \\ 5 & 10 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Compute $A^{5}$ using $A^{5} = PD^{5}P^{-1}$.**

$$D^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 6^{5} & 0 \\ 0 & 1^{5} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7776 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$PD^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7776 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 31104 & -1 \\ 7776 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^{5} = PD^{5}P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31104 & -1 \\ 7776 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31105 & 31100 \\ 7775 & 7780 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{A^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 6221 & 6220 \\ 1555 & 1556 \end{pmatrix}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Discuss the **Power Method** to find the dominant eigenvalue. Apply two iterations on $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ with $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

##### Model Solution

**Theory of Power Method:** The Power Method is an iterative numerical technique to find the *largest* (in magnitude) eigenvalue — called the **dominant eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ — of a matrix. Starting from an initial non-zero vector $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$, the method proceeds as:
1. Compute $\mathbf{y}^{(k+1)} = A\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$.
2. Scale the vector so that its largest component equals 1: $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{y}^{(k+1)} / \mu_{k+1}$, where $\mu_{k+1}$ is the largest absolute component.
3. The sequence $\mu_{k}$ converges to the dominant eigenvalue $\lambda_1$, and $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ converges to the corresponding eigenvector.

**Iteration 1 ($k = 0$):**

$$\mathbf{y}^{(1)} = A\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Largest component: $\mu_1 = 4$. Normalize: $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$. First estimate: $\lambda_1 \approx 4$.

**Iteration 2 ($k = 1$):**

$$\mathbf{y}^{(2)} = A\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 + 0.5 \\ 2 + 1.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 \\ 3.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

Largest component: $\mu_2 = 4.5$. Normalize: $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{4.5}\begin{pmatrix} 4.5 \\ 3.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0.7778 \end{pmatrix}$. Improved estimate: $\lambda_1 \approx 4.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> The true dominant eigenvalue of this matrix is **5** (from Question A above). The sequence $4 \to 4.5 \to \dots$ converges to 5, and the eigenvector converges to $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the characteristic equation.** A common error is to compute $\det(\lambda I - A)$ instead of $\det(A - \lambda I)$. While both yield the *same* polynomial (up to a sign), the eigenvalues come from setting it equal to zero — make the choice **once and stick with it**.
> 2. **Returning the zero vector as an eigenvector.** By definition, $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **NOT** an eigenvector. If your Gaussian elimination gives the trivial solution, you have made an arithmetic error — go back and check.
> 3. **Skipping the verification step.** KTU examiners often give 1 bonus mark for verifying that $A\mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ holds for the computed eigenvector. Always substitute back.
> 4. **In the Power Method, forgetting to normalize at every step.** This causes the vectors to overflow and makes the eigenvalue estimate numerically unstable. Use the *infinity norm* (max absolute component) consistently.
> 5. **Confusing "trace" with "determinant".** Trace = sum of eigenvalues (on the **diagonal** of the matrix). Determinant = product of eigenvalues. These are *very* different concepts.
> 6. **Missing the "n linearly independent eigenvectors" condition for diagonalization.** A matrix can have *fewer* than n distinct eigenvectors if it has repeated eigenvalues. Check linear independence before claiming diagonalizability.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before your KTU exam:**
> - [x] The eigen-equation is $A\mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
> - [x] The characteristic equation is $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$, which gives the **characteristic polynomial** of degree $n$.
> - [x] **Sum of eigenvalues = trace of A**; **Product of eigenvalues = det(A)**.
> - [x] Eigenvalues of $A^{k}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{k}$. Eigenvalues of $A^{-1}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$ (when $\det(A) \neq 0$).
> - [x] Eigenvalues of $A + cI$ are $\lambda_{i} + c$. Eigenvalues of $cA$ are $c\lambda_{i}$.
> - [x] A real symmetric matrix has all **real eigenvalues** and is orthogonally diagonalizable.
> - [x] A matrix is **diagonalizable** iff it has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
> - [x] The **Cayley–Hamilton theorem** states: every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial, $p(A) = 0$. This is *the* tool to compute $A^{-1}$ and $A^{k}$ efficiently.
> - [x] The **Power Method** iteratively finds the *dominant* eigenvalue by repeatedly multiplying a vector by $A$ and re-normalizing.
> - [x] **Eigenvalue multiplicity** matters: $AM \geq GM$ always, and the matrix is diagonalizable iff $AM = GM$ for every distinct eigenvalue.
> - [x] In ML, eigenvalues of the **covariance matrix** give the variance captured by each principal component in PCA.
> - [x] In control systems, real parts of eigenvalues of the system matrix determine **stability** (all real parts < 0 ⇒ stable).
> - [x] For a 2×2 matrix, the characteristic polynomial is always $\lambda^{2} - (\text{trace})\lambda + \det(A) = 0$ — a quick mental check.
> - [x] Always **verify** by substituting the eigenvector back: $A\mathbf{x}$ should equal $\lambda \mathbf{x}$ exactly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cholesky Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cholesky Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cholesky Decomposition** is a unique matrix factorization in which a **symmetric positive definite (SPD)** matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is decomposed into the product of a **lower triangular matrix** $\mathbf{L}$ and its **conjugate transpose** $\mathbf{L}^{T}$ such that:
> $$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L} \, \mathbf{L}^{T}$$

When the matrix entries are real, the transpose $\mathbf{L}^{T}$ is used; for complex Hermitian matrices, the conjugate transpose $\mathbf{L}^{H}$ is used. The matrix $\mathbf{L}$ is commonly called the **Cholesky factor** or the **square root matrix** of $\mathbf{A}$, since $\mathbf{L}$ acts as a multiplicative "square root" of $\mathbf{A}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence & Uniqueness Theorem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):**
> A symmetric positive definite matrix $\mathbf{A}$ admits a **unique** Cholesky decomposition $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ in which the diagonal entries of $\mathbf{L}$ are strictly positive. This is a fundamental result that distinguishes Cholesky from other factorizations like LU.

### Pre-Conditions for Cholesky Decomposition

The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ must satisfy **two** strict conditions:

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Practical Meaning |
|:---------|:----------------------|:------------------|
| **Symmetry** | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{T}$ | $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$ for all $i, j$ |
| **Positive Definiteness** | $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All leading principal minors $> 0$ and all eigenvalues $> 0$ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Perfect Origami" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine $\mathbf{A}$ as a flat, square sheet of stiff origami paper.**
> The Cholesky decomposition asks the question: *"Can I find a triangular piece of paper $\mathbf{L}$ that, when folded against itself along its mirror axis, exactly reproduces the original square sheet $\mathbf{A}$?"*

The triangular piece $\mathbf{L}$ is **half the information** of the square sheet, because the other half is just its mirror image. This is why Cholesky is so memory-efficient: it stores only the lower triangle, using **exactly $n(n+1)/2$ storage units** instead of the $n^2$ units required for the full matrix — a **~50% saving** for large matrices.

> **Real-World Analogy (Engineering Lens):**
> In **structural engineering**, the stiffness matrix of a stable truss is always SPD. Cholesky decomposition is akin to decomposing a complex coupled spring system into a single chain of independent springs — each spring in $\mathbf{L}$ represents one "uncoupled" mode, which makes solving for displacements and forces dramatically easier.

---

## 1.3 Why Cholesky Matters in Machine Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cholesky decomposition is the **workhorse** of probabilistic machine learning. It is the standard tool for:
> - Solving **linear least squares** regression problems (Normal equations: $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{y}$).
> - Generating **multivariate Gaussian samples** in Monte Carlo simulation and Bayesian inference.
> - Computing the **log-determinant** of a covariance matrix in Gaussian Process regression.
> - Acting as a **preconditioner** in iterative solvers like the Conjugate Gradient method.
> - Solving the **Riccati equation** in Kalman filters and optimal control.

### Key Complexity Statement (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> Cholesky decomposition requires approximately $\dfrac{n^3}{3}$ **floating point operations (flops)**, which is **half** the cost of general LU decomposition ($n^3/3 \times 2$ flops) because the matrix structure is symmetric. This makes it the **fastest exact linear solver** for SPD systems.

---

## 1.4 Terminology Glossary for Board Examinations

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|:-----|:-------|:--------|
| Cholesky Factor | $\mathbf{L}$ | Lower triangular matrix with positive diagonal entries |
| Symmetric Positive Definite | SPD | Matrix that satisfies $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{T}$ and $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} > 0$ |
| Principal Minor | $\Delta_k$ | Determinant of the $k \times k$ leading submatrix |
| Numerical Rank Deficiency | — | Condition when $\mathbf{A}$ is not positive definite; Cholesky fails |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Analysis of $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Cholesky decomposition works because $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric, meaning its information is fully contained in the **upper or lower triangle**. Thus $\mathbf{L}$ can store *all* the data needed to reconstruct $\mathbf{A}$.

Let us expand the product $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ entry-by-entry.

Consider a $3 \times 3$ SPD matrix and its Cholesky factor:
$$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11} & 0 & 0 \\ \ell_{21} & \ell_{22} & 0 \\ \ell_{31} & \ell_{32} & \ell_{33} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11} & \ell_{21} & \ell_{31} \\ 0 & \ell_{22} & \ell_{32} \\ 0 & 0 & \ell_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying these out:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11}^{2} & \ell_{21}\ell_{11} & \ell_{31}\ell_{11} \\ \ell_{21}\ell_{11} & \ell_{21}^{2}+\ell_{22}^{2} & \ell_{31}\ell_{21}+\ell_{32}\ell_{22} \\ \ell_{31}\ell_{11} & \ell_{31}\ell_{21}+\ell_{32}\ell_{22} & \ell_{31}^{2}+\ell_{32}^{2}+\ell_{33}^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

This expansion reveals the **6 independent scalar equations** that must be solved to obtain the **6 unknown entries** of $\mathbf{L}$ (since the upper triangle is zero). The pattern generalizes to give the algorithmic formulas below.

---

## 2.2 The Cholesky Algorithm — Derivation of the Recurrence Formulas

By equating the $(i, j)$-th entry of $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$, we get:

$$a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{\min(i,j)} \ell_{ik} \, \ell_{jk}$$

### Step 1 — Diagonal Entries ($i = j$)

When $i = j$, the equation becomes:
$$a_{ii} = \sum_{k=1}^{i} \ell_{ik}^{2} = \ell_{ii}^{2} + \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}$$

Solving for $\ell_{ii}$ (taking the positive root, since we want positive diagonal entries):

$$\boxed{\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each diagonal entry of $\mathbf{L}$ is the "remaining" length after subtracting the projections of all previously computed rows. The square root must yield a real, positive number — this is precisely the condition that enforces positive definiteness.

### Step 2 — Off-Diagonal Entries ($i > j$)

When $i > j$, the equation becomes:
$$a_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{j} \ell_{ik} \, \ell_{jk} = \ell_{ij}\ell_{jj} + \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk}$$

Solving for $\ell_{ij}$:

$$\boxed{\ell_{ij} = \frac{1}{\ell_{jj}} \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk} \right), \quad \text{for } i > j}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why divide by $\ell_{jj}$?** The diagonal element $\ell_{jj}$ acts as a "scaling factor" or pivot, similar to pivoting in Gaussian elimination. If $\ell_{jj}$ is very small, the off-diagonal entries become large, indicating the matrix is nearly singular.

### Step 3 — Order of Computation

The algorithm is computed **column-by-column from top to bottom** (or equivalently, row-by-row from left to right). The order is:

1. Compute $\ell_{11}$.
2. Compute $\ell_{21}, \ell_{22}$.
3. Compute $\ell_{31}, \ell_{32}, \ell_{33}$.
4. Continue until the full lower triangle is filled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This sequential dependence is the key reason Cholesky is **not easily parallelizable** in its pure form, although blocked and tiled variants exist for high-performance computing (e.g., on GPUs).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they appear in 80%+ of KTU board questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ | Core decomposition | $\mathbf{A}$ SPD |
| 2 | $\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}}$ | Diagonal formula | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| 3 | $\ell_{ij} = \frac{1}{\ell_{jj}}\left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk} \right)$ | Off-diagonal formula | $i > j$ |
| 4 | $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{ii}^{2}$ | Determinant via Cholesky | $\mathbf{A}$ SPD |
| 5 | $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = (\mathbf{L}^{-1})^{T} \mathbf{L}^{-1}$ | Inverse via Cholesky | $\mathbf{A}$ SPD |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{L}^{-1})^{T} (\mathbf{L}^{-1}\mathbf{b})$ | Linear system solver | $\mathbf{A}$ SPD |
| 7 | $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}) \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{z}, \; \mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ | Multivariate Gaussian sampling | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ |
| 8 | Cost: $\dfrac{n^3}{3}$ flops | Computational complexity | Compared to $2n^3/3$ for LU |
| 9 | Storage: $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Memory usage | Half of $n^2$ |
| 10 | $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \Vert \mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} \Vert_{2}^{2} > 0$ | Positivity check | Definition of SPD |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Table Symbol:** In KTU answer sheets, write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ and "such that" as $\mid$ to avoid markdown rendering issues. Use **bold** for the diagonal of $\mathbf{L}$ when writing it out manually.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

| Field | Application | Role of Cholesky |
|:------|:------------|:-----------------|
| **Machine Learning** | Gaussian Process regression, Bayesian inference | Inverting covariance matrices; computing log-determinants |
| **Computer Graphics** | Path tracing, light transport | Sampling from correlated noise distributions |
| **Control Systems** | Kalman filter, LQR control | Solving the continuous-time algebraic Riccati equation |
| **Geodesy / GPS** | Survey network adjustment | Solving large SPD normal equation systems |
| **Structural Mechanics** | Finite Element Analysis (FEA) | Solving $[K]\{u\} = \{F\}$ where $K$ is the global stiffness matrix |
| **Quantitative Finance** | Portfolio risk modelling (e.g., Monte Carlo VaR) | Sampling from correlated asset return distributions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The syllabus emphasizes the application of Cholesky in **solving linear regression normal equations** and **sampling from multivariate Gaussians** for ML applications. Be prepared to be asked these in Part A.

---

## 2.5 Geometric Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ transforms the **ellipsoid** defined by the quadratic form $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{x} = 1$ into a **unit sphere**.
>
> In other words, if $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x}$, then $\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_{2}^{2} = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$. The matrix $\mathbf{L}$ "whitens" or "spheres" the correlated space defined by $\mathbf{A}$.

This whitening transformation is the foundation of the **Mahalanobis distance**, **PCA preprocessing**, and **decorrelated noise generation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — $3 \times 3$ SPD Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** The symmetric positive definite matrix:
> $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 12 & -16 \\ 12 & 37 & -43 \\ -16 & -43 & 98 \end{bmatrix}$$
> **Required:** Find the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ such that $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$.

### Preliminary Check — Verifying Positive Definiteness

We compute the **leading principal minors** (Sylvester's criterion):

**Minor 1** ($1 \times 1$ leading submatrix):
$$\Delta_{1} = \vert 4 \vert = 4 > 0$$

**Minor 2** ($2 \times 2$ leading submatrix):
$$\Delta_{2} = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 12 \\ 12 & 37 \end{vmatrix} = (4)(37) - (12)(12) = 148 - 144 = 4 > 0$$

**Minor 3** (full determinant):
$$\Delta_{3} = \det(\mathbf{A})$$

Expanding along the first row:
$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 37 & -43 \\ -43 & 98 \end{vmatrix} - 12 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 12 & -43 \\ -16 & 98 \end{vmatrix} + (-16) \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 12 & 37 \\ -16 & -43 \end{vmatrix}$$

Computing each $2 \times 2$ determinant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{vmatrix} 37 & -43 \\ -43 & 98 \end{vmatrix} &= (37)(98) - (-43)(-43) = 3626 - 1849 = 1777 \\[4pt]
\begin{vmatrix} 12 & -43 \\ -16 & 98 \end{vmatrix} &= (12)(98) - (-43)(-16) = 1176 - 688 = 488 \\[4pt]
\begin{vmatrix} 12 & 37 \\ -16 & -43 \end{vmatrix} &= (12)(-43) - (37)(-16) = -516 + 592 = 76
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:
$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 4(1777) - 12(488) + (-16)(76) = 7108 - 5856 - 1216 = 36 > 0$$

All three leading principal minors are strictly positive ($\Delta_1 = 4$, $\Delta_2 = 4$, $\Delta_3 = 36$), so $\mathbf{A}$ is **positive definite** by Sylvester's criterion. Cholesky decomposition exists and is unique.

---

### Step 1 — Compute $\ell_{11}$

Using the diagonal formula with $i = 1$ (the sum is empty since the upper limit $i-1 = 0$):

$$\ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11} - \sum_{k=1}^{0} \ell_{1k}^{2}} = \sqrt{a_{11} - 0} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute $\ell_{21}$ and $\ell_{22}$

**For $\ell_{21}$** (off-diagonal, $i = 2, j = 1$):
$$\ell_{21} = \frac{1}{\ell_{11}} \left( a_{21} - \sum_{k=1}^{0} \ell_{2k}\ell_{1k} \right) = \frac{1}{2}(a_{21} - 0) = \frac{12}{2} = 6$$

**For $\ell_{22}$** (diagonal, $i = 2$):
$$\ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \sum_{k=1}^{1} \ell_{2k}^{2}} = \sqrt{37 - \ell_{21}^{2}} = \sqrt{37 - 36} = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute $\ell_{31}, \ell_{32}, \ell_{33}$

**For $\ell_{31}$** (off-diagonal, $i = 3, j = 1$):
$$\ell_{31} = \frac{1}{\ell_{11}} \left( a_{31} - \sum_{k=1}^{0} \ell_{3k}\ell_{1k} \right) = \frac{-16}{2} = -8$$

**For $\ell_{32}$** (off-diagonal, $i = 3, j = 2$):
$$\ell_{32} = \frac{1}{\ell_{22}} \left( a_{32} - \sum_{k=1}^{1} \ell_{3k}\ell_{2k} \right) = \frac{1}{1}\left( a_{32} - \ell_{31}\ell_{21} \right)$$
$$\ell_{32} = -43 - (-8)(6) = -43 + 48 = 5$$

**For $\ell_{33}$** (diagonal, $i = 3$):
$$\ell_{33} = \sqrt{a_{33} - \sum_{k=1}^{2} \ell_{3k}^{2}} = \sqrt{98 - (\ell_{31}^{2} + \ell_{32}^{2})} = \sqrt{98 - (64 + 25)} = \sqrt{98 - 89} = \sqrt{9} = 3$$

---

### Final Cholesky Factor

$$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 6 & 1 & 0 \\ -8 & 5 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.2 Verification — Confirming $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$

We compute $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ and verify it equals $\mathbf{A}$.

$$\mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 & -8 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying row-by-column:

**Entry $(1,1)$:** $(2)(2) + (0)(0) + (0)(0) = 4$ ✓

**Entry $(1,2)$:** $(2)(6) + (0)(1) + (0)(0) = 12$ ✓

**Entry $(1,3)$:** $(2)(-8) + (0)(5) + (0)(3) = -16$ ✓

**Entry $(2,2)$:** $(6)(6) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) = 36 + 1 = 37$ ✓

**Entry $(2,3)$:** $(6)(-8) + (1)(5) + (0)(3) = -48 + 5 = -43$ ✓

**Entry $(3,3)$:** $(-8)(-8) + (5)(5) + (3)(3) = 64 + 25 + 9 = 98$ ✓

By symmetry, all other entries match. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 12 & -16 \\ 12 & 37 & -43 \\ -16 & -43 & 98 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Generalization to $n \times n$

> [!TIP]
> The Cholesky algorithm generalizes elegantly. Below is the formal pseudocode-style description for an arbitrary $n \times n$ SPD matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

| Step | Operation | Formula | Dependencies |
|:----:|:----------|:--------|:-------------|
| 1 | Initialize | $\mathbf{L} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}_{n \times n}$ | — |
| 2 | For $i = 1$ to $n$ | Loop over rows/columns | Sequential |
| 3 | For $j = 1$ to $i - 1$ | Compute off-diagonal | Requires $\ell_{i,k}, \ell_{j,k}$ for $k < j$ |
| 4 | Compute $\ell_{ij}$ | $\ell_{ij} = \frac{1}{\ell_{jj}}\left(a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk}\right)$ | Step 3 |
| 5 | Compute $\ell_{ii}$ | $\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}}$ | Step 3 (for row $i$) |
| 6 | Check | $\ell_{ii} > 0$ (else: matrix not SPD) | Step 5 |
| 7 | End | Return $\mathbf{L}$ | — |

---

## 3.4 Derivations of Key Properties

### Property 1 — Determinant via Cholesky

The determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal entries:

$$\det(\mathbf{L}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{ii}$$

Using the multiplicative property of determinants:
$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = \det(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}) = \det(\mathbf{L}) \cdot \det(\mathbf{L}^{T}) = [\det(\mathbf{L})]^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{ii}^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** This is the most efficient way to compute $\log\det(\mathbf{A})$ in machine learning:
> $$\log\det(\mathbf{A}) = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log(\ell_{ii})$$
> This expression appears in the **log-likelihood** of Gaussian distributions and the **evidence lower bound (ELBO)** in variational inference.

### Property 2 — Inverse via Cholesky

If $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$, then:

$$\mathbf{A}^{-1} = (\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T})^{-1} = (\mathbf{L}^{T})^{-1} \mathbf{L}^{-1} = (\mathbf{L}^{-1})^{T} \mathbf{L}^{-1}$$

To compute $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$:
1. Solve $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{I}$ by **forward substitution** to get $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{L}^{-1}$.
2. Compute $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \mathbf{Y}^{T} \mathbf{Y}$.

> [!TIP]
> This is more numerically stable and **twice as fast** as computing $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ via Gaussian elimination, especially for large sparse systems.

### Property 3 — Solving $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ via Cholesky

The system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ becomes $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, which we solve in two triangular stages:

**Stage 1 — Forward substitution:** Solve $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$ for $\mathbf{y}$:

$$y_{i} = \frac{1}{\ell_{ii}} \left( b_{i} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik} y_{k} \right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Stage 2 — Backward substitution:** Solve $\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$ for $\mathbf{x}$:

$$x_{i} = \frac{1}{\ell_{ii}} \left( y_{i} - \sum_{k=i+1}^{n} \ell_{ki} x_{k} \right), \quad i = n, n-1, \ldots, 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total cost:** $\dfrac{n^3}{3} + \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ flops, which is **half** the cost of full LU decomposition followed by two substitutions. This is why Cholesky is preferred whenever $\mathbf{A}$ is SPD.

---

## 3.5 Solution of a Linear System — Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Using the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and its Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ from §3.1, solve $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 50 \\ -30 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Stage 1 — Solve $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 6 & 1 & 0 \\ -8 & 5 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} y_{1} \\ y_{2} \\ y_{3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 50 \\ -30 \end{bmatrix}$$

From row 1: $2y_1 = 20 \Rightarrow y_1 = 10$

From row 2: $6y_1 + y_2 = 50 \Rightarrow 60 + y_2 = 50 \Rightarrow y_2 = -10$

From row 3: $-8y_1 + 5y_2 + 3y_3 = -30 \Rightarrow -80 - 50 + 3y_3 = -30 \Rightarrow 3y_3 = 100 \Rightarrow y_3 = \dfrac{100}{3}$

**Stage 2 — Solve $\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 & -8 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \\ x_{3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ -10 \\ \dfrac{100}{3} \end{bmatrix}$$

From row 3: $3x_3 = \dfrac{100}{3} \Rightarrow x_3 = \dfrac{100}{9}$

From row 2: $x_2 + 5x_3 = -10 \Rightarrow x_2 = -10 - \dfrac{500}{9} = \dfrac{-90 - 500}{9} = \dfrac{-590}{9}$

From row 1: $2x_1 + 6x_2 - 8x_3 = 10$

$$2x_1 = 10 - 6 \left( \dfrac{-590}{9} \right) + 8 \left( \dfrac{100}{9} \right) = 10 + \dfrac{3540}{9} + \dfrac{800}{9} = 10 + \dfrac{4340}{9} = \dfrac{90 + 4340}{9} = \dfrac{4430}{9}$$

$$x_1 = \dfrac{4430}{18} = \dfrac{2215}{9}$$

**Final Solution Vector:**

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 2215/9 \\ -590/9 \\ 100/9 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{9}\begin{bmatrix} 2215 \\ -590 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification (Quick Check):** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ at row 1:

$$4x_1 + 12x_2 - 16x_3 = 4 \cdot \dfrac{2215}{9} + 12 \cdot \dfrac{-590}{9} - 16 \cdot \dfrac{100}{9} = \dfrac{8860 - 7080 - 1600}{9} = \dfrac{180}{9} = 20 \quad \checkmark$$

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Cholesky Algorithm Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram maps the **complete computational workflow** of the Cholesky algorithm, including precondition checks, the recursive computation of $\mathbf{L}$, and the numerical validity test.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Symmetric Matrix A (n x n)"] --> B["Verify Symmetry: A = A transpose"]
    B --> C{"Is A Symmetric?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["HALT: Cholesky Requires Symmetry"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Verify Positive Definiteness via Leading Principal Minors"]
    E --> F{"All Minors > 0?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["HALT: Cholesky Requires Positive Definiteness"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H["Initialize L as Zero Matrix (n x n)"]
    H --> I["Set i = 1"]
    I --> J["Compute Diagonal Entry L ii"]
    J --> K["Compute Off-Diagonal Entries L ij for j < i"]
    K --> L{"i = n?"}
    L -- "No" --> M["Increment i by 1"]
    M --> J
    L -- "Yes" --> N["Output: Lower Triangular Matrix L"]
    N --> O["Verification Step: Compute L times L transpose"]
    O --> P{"L L transpose equals A?"}
    P -- "Yes" --> Q["SUCCESS: Cholesky Decomposition Complete"]
    P -- "No" --> R["Check for Rounding Errors or Matrix Ill-Conditioning"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Numerical Stability Considerations

> [!WARNING]
> This state diagram alerts students to the **three failure modes** of Cholesky decomposition that lead to loss of marks in numerical computing questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InputSPD
    InputSPD --> ComputeL: Matrix is Symmetric and Positive Definite
    ComputeL --> OutputL: All Diagonal Entries Real and Positive
    ComputeL --> NegativeUnderRoot: Encountered Negative Value Under Square Root
    ComputeL --> DivisionByZero: Diagonal Entry Equals Zero
    ComputeL --> NumericalInstability: Small Diagonal Entries cause Overflow

    NegativeUnderRoot --> [*]: Matrix is NOT Positive Definite
    DivisionByZero --> [*]: Matrix is Singular
    NumericalInstability --> [*]: Matrix is Ill-Conditioned

    OutputL --> [*]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A questions are direct, formulaic, and test definitional clarity. Aim for crisp, single-paragraph answers.**

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`

> **State and explain the Cholesky decomposition theorem. What are the necessary and sufficient conditions for its existence?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Cholesky decomposition theorem states that **every symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix** $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ can be uniquely factorized as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$, where $\mathbf{L}$ is a **lower triangular matrix with strictly positive diagonal entries**.
>
> The necessary and sufficient conditions are:
> 1. **Symmetry:** $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{T}$ (i.e., $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$ for all $i, j$).
> 2. **Positive Definiteness:** $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all non-zero $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, which by **Sylvester's criterion** is equivalent to all **leading principal minors** of $\mathbf{A}$ being strictly positive.
>
> Uniqueness is guaranteed by enforcing the convention that $\ell_{ii} > 0$. The Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ contains exactly $n(n+1)/2$ independent entries, which equals the number of independent entries in the symmetric matrix $\mathbf{A}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

> **Compare Cholesky decomposition with LU decomposition. Mention any two advantages of Cholesky.**

**Model Answer:**

> Both Cholesky and LU decompose a matrix into a product of triangular factors, but they differ in scope and efficiency:
>
> | Aspect | LU Decomposition | Cholesky Decomposition |
> |:-------|:-----------------|:----------------------|
> | Applicable to | Any square, non-singular matrix | Only symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices |
> | Form | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{U}$ | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ |
> | Flops | $\approx 2n^{3}/3$ | $\approx n^{3}/3$ (half of LU) |
> | Storage | $n^{2}$ | $n(n+1)/2$ (half of LU) |
> | Existence | Guaranteed for non-singular $\mathbf{A}$ | Only for SPD matrices |
>
> **Two Advantages of Cholesky over LU:**
> 1. **Half the computational cost** ($\approx n^{3}/3$ flops vs. $2n^{3}/3$ flops) due to exploiting symmetry.
> 2. **Half the storage requirement** ($n(n+1)/2$ vs. $n^{2}$) and **guaranteed numerical stability** without the need for row pivoting. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part B questions involve multi-part problems requiring derivation, computation, and interpretation. KTU ESE provides an internal choice between two questions — both are presented below.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, End-Semester]`

> **(a)** Derive the formulas for computing the diagonal and off-diagonal entries of the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ for a $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive definite matrix $\mathbf{A} = [a_{ij}]$. Clearly state the order of computation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the SPD matrix:
> $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & -3 & 6 \\ -3 & 2 & -2 \\ 6 & -2 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$
> compute its Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ and verify your answer. Use $\mathbf{L}$ to compute $\det(\mathbf{A})$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part A(a):**

> **Step 1 — Set up the matrix equation:**
>
> We write $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ where:
> $$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11} & 0 & 0 \\ \ell_{21} & \ell_{22} & 0 \\ \ell_{31} & \ell_{32} & \ell_{33} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11} & \ell_{21} & \ell_{31} \\ 0 & \ell_{22} & \ell_{32} \\ 0 & 0 & \ell_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Multiply to get $\mathbf{A}$:**
