> $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11}^{2} & \ell_{21}\ell_{11} & \ell_{31}\ell_{11} \\ \ell_{21}\ell_{11} & \ell_{21}^{2}+\ell_{22}^{2} & \ell_{31}\ell_{21}+\ell_{32}\ell_{22} \\ \ell_{31}\ell_{11} & \ell_{31}\ell_{21}+\ell_{32}\ell_{22} & \ell_{31}^{2}+\ell_{32}^{2}+\ell_{33}^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Equate entries and solve for $\ell_{ij}$:**
>
> From $a_{11} = \ell_{11}^{2}$: $\quad \ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}}$
>
> From $a_{21} = \ell_{21}\ell_{11}$: $\quad \ell_{21} = a_{21}/\ell_{11}$
>
> From $a_{22} = \ell_{21}^{2} + \ell_{22}^{2}$: $\quad \ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \ell_{21}^{2}}$
>
> From $a_{31} = \ell_{31}\ell_{11}$: $\quad \ell_{31} = a_{31}/\ell_{11}$
>
> From $a_{32} = \ell_{31}\ell_{21} + \ell_{32}\ell_{22}$: $\quad \ell_{32} = (a_{32} - \ell_{31}\ell_{21})/\ell_{22}$
>
> From $a_{33} = \ell_{31}^{2} + \ell_{32}^{2} + \ell_{33}^{2}$: $\quad \ell_{33} = \sqrt{a_{33} - \ell_{31}^{2} - \ell_{32}^{2}}$
>
> **Order of Computation:** First compute $\ell_{11}$, then $\ell_{21}$ and $\ell_{22}$, then $\ell_{31}, \ell_{32}, \ell_{33}$. In general, the algorithm proceeds column-by-column (or row-by-row), where each new entry depends only on previously computed ones. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part A(b):**

> **Step 1 — Compute $\ell_{11}$:**
> $$\ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}} = \sqrt{9} = 3$$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute $\ell_{21}$ and $\ell_{22}$:**
> $$\ell_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{-3}{3} = -1$$
> $$\ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \ell_{21}^{2}} = \sqrt{2 - 1} = 1$$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute $\ell_{31}, \ell_{32}, \ell_{33}$:**
> $$\ell_{31} = \frac{a_{31}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{6}{3} = 2$$
> $$\ell_{32} = \frac{a_{32} - \ell_{31}\ell_{21}}{\ell_{22}} = \frac{-2 - (2)(-1)}{1} = -2 + 2 = 0$$
> $$\ell_{33} = \sqrt{a_{33} - \ell_{31}^{2} - \ell_{32}^{2}} = \sqrt{9 - 4 - 0} = \sqrt{5}$$
>
> **Cholesky Factor:**
> $$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & \sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Verification:** Compute $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$:
>
> - $(1,1)$: $9$
> - $(1,2)$: $(3)(-1) + (0)(1) + (0)(0) = -3$ ✓
> - $(1,3)$: $(3)(2) + (0)(0) + (0)(\sqrt{5}) = 6$ ✓
> - $(2,2)$: $1 + 1 = 2$ ✓
> - $(2,3)$: $(-1)(2) + (1)(0) + (0)(\sqrt{5}) = -2$ ✓
> - $(3,3)$: $4 + 0 + 5 = 9$ ✓
>
> **Determinant of $\mathbf{A}$ via Cholesky:**
> $$\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{3} \ell_{ii}^{2} = 3^{2} \cdot 1^{2} \cdot (\sqrt{5})^{2} = 9 \cdot 1 \cdot 5 = 45$$
>
> **Cross-check via cofactor expansion:**
> $$\det(\mathbf{A}) = 9(2 \cdot 9 - 4) - (-3)(-27 - (-12)) + 6(6 - 12) = 9(14) + 3(-15) + 6(-6) = 126 - 45 - 36 = 45 \quad \checkmark$$
> **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End-Semester]`

> **(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart how the linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is solved using the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ of an SPD matrix $\mathbf{A}$. Write the relevant formulas. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the SPD matrix:
> $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$$
> compute the Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ and use it to solve $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part B(a):**

> **Solution Method — Two-Stage Triangular Substitution:**
>
> Given $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$, the linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ becomes:
> $$\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$$
>
> This is solved in **two sequential stages**:
>
> **Stage 1 — Forward Substitution:** Solve $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$ for $\mathbf{y}$:
> $$y_{i} = \frac{1}{\ell_{ii}} \left( b_{i} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik} y_{k} \right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
>
> **Stage 2 — Backward Substitution:** Solve $\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$ for $\mathbf{x}$:
> $$x_{i} = \frac{1}{\ell_{ii}} \left( y_{i} - \sum_{k=i+1}^{n} \ell_{ki} x_{k} \right), \quad i = n, n-1, \ldots, 1$$
>
> **Flowchart of the Solution Process:**
>
> ```
> ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
> │  STEP 1: Verify A is Symmetric Positive  │
> │  Definite using Leading Principal Minors │
> └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
>                      ▼
> ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
> │  STEP 2: Compute Cholesky Factor L such  │
> │  that A = L * L_transpose                │
> └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
>                      ▼
> ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
> │  STEP 3: Solve L y = b via FORWARD       │
> │  substitution to obtain intermediate y    │
> └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
>                      ▼
> ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
> │  STEP 4: Solve L_transpose x = y via     │
> │  BACKWARD substitution to obtain final x  │
> └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
>                      ▼
> ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
> │  STEP 5: (Optional) Verify A x = b       │
> └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
> ```
>
> **Complexity:** Each triangular substitution requires $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ flops; the Cholesky factor computation itself requires $\mathcal{O}(n^{3}/3)$ flops. Total cost is therefore $\mathcal{O}(n^{3}/3)$, which is **half** that of LU decomposition. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part B(b):**

> **Step 1 — Compute $\ell_{11}$ and $\ell_{21}$:**
> $$\ell_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$
> $$\ell_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{\ell_{11}} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute $\ell_{22}$:**
> $$\ell_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - \ell_{21}^{2}} = \sqrt{3 - 1} = \sqrt{2}$$
>
> **Cholesky Factor:**
> $$\mathbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & \sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Forward Substitution** $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$:
>
> Row 1: $2y_1 = 8 \Rightarrow y_1 = 4$
>
> Row 2: $y_1 + \sqrt{2} \, y_2 = 7 \Rightarrow 4 + \sqrt{2} y_2 = 7 \Rightarrow \sqrt{2} y_2 = 3 \Rightarrow y_2 = \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{2}$
>
> **Step 4 — Backward Substitution** $\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$:
>
> $\mathbf{L}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & \sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$
>
> Row 2 (from bottom): $\sqrt{2} \, x_2 = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{2} \Rightarrow x_2 = \dfrac{3}{2}$
>
> Row 1: $2x_1 + x_2 = 4 \Rightarrow 2x_1 = 4 - \dfrac{3}{2} = \dfrac{5}{2} \Rightarrow x_1 = \dfrac{5}{4}$
>
> **Final Solution:**
> $$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/4 \\ 3/2 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Verification:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$:
> $$4 \cdot \dfrac{5}{4} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{3}{2} = 5 + 3 = 8 \quad \checkmark$$
> $$2 \cdot \dfrac{5}{4} + 3 \cdot \dfrac{3}{2} = \dfrac{5}{2} + \dfrac{9}{2} = 7 \quad \checkmark$$
> **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> Based on KTU 2024 answer key patterns, students **typically lose 2-3 marks** on Cholesky questions due to the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Skipping the SPD verification** (loses ~1 mark): Always verify symmetry and compute at least the first two leading principal minors. If the matrix is not SPD, the decomposition does not exist.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the convention $\ell_{ii} > 0$** (loses ~1 mark): If you obtain a negative root, take the absolute value. The Cholesky factor is **uniquely defined** with positive diagonal entries.
>
> 3. **Wrong summation indices** (loses ~1-2 marks): Be very careful that the off-diagonal formula uses $j$ as the index in the denominator (i.e., divide by $\ell_{jj}$), and the sum runs from $k=1$ to $j-1$, not $i-1$.
>
> 4. **Not writing $\mathbf{L}^{T}$ explicitly** (loses ~0.5 mark): When asked to "use $\mathbf{L}$ to solve $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$", explicitly mention **forward substitution** and **backward substitution** by name. The examiner looks for this terminology.
>
> 5. **Confusing Cholesky with $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U}$**: Some texts use the upper triangular form. If a question specifies "upper triangular Cholesky", use $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U}$ where $\mathbf{U}$ is upper triangular with positive diagonal. Otherwise, default to $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** Cholesky decomposition factors an SPD matrix as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ where $\mathbf{L}$ is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries.

- [x] **Preconditions:** $\mathbf{A}$ must be **symmetric** ($\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{T}$) AND **positive definite** (all leading principal minors $> 0$, equivalently all eigenvalues $> 0$).

- [x] **Uniqueness:** The decomposition is **unique** when we enforce the convention $\ell_{ii} > 0$.

- [x] **Diagonal Formula:**
$$\ell_{ii} = \sqrt{a_{ii} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} \ell_{ik}^{2}}$$

- [x] **Off-Diagonal Formula** ($i > j$):
$$\ell_{ij} = \frac{1}{\ell_{jj}} \left( a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} \ell_{ik}\ell_{jk} \right)$$

- [x] **Order of Computation:** Compute $\ell_{11}$ first, then $\ell_{21}, \ell_{22}$, then $\ell_{31}, \ell_{32}, \ell_{33}$, and so on — column by column from top to bottom.

- [x] **Determinant:** $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{ii}^{2}$.

- [x] **Linear System Solver:** Use **forward substitution** on $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$ then **backward substitution** on $\mathbf{L}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$.

- [x] **Complexity:** Approximately $n^{3}/3$ flops and $n(n+1)/2$ storage — **half** the cost of LU decomposition.

- [x] **ML Connection:** Used for sampling from multivariate Gaussians, computing log-determinants in Gaussian Processes, and solving normal equations in linear regression.

- [x] **Geometric Meaning:** $\mathbf{L}$ transforms the ellipsoid $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = 1$ into a unit sphere — i.e., $\mathbf{L}$ "whitens" the data.

- [x] **Pitfall to Avoid:** If a square root argument becomes negative, the matrix is **NOT** positive definite — Cholesky fails.

- [x] **Numerical Stability:** Cholesky is **inherently stable** without pivoting (unlike LU), making it preferred for SPD systems.

- [x] **Block Form:** The decomposition can be written as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{D}\mathbf{L}^{T}$ where $\mathbf{D}$ is a diagonal matrix of squared pivots — useful in incomplete Cholesky preconditioners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eigen Decomposition and Diagonalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eigen Decomposition and Diagonalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

Let $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ be a square matrix. A scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ is called an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{C}^{n}$ such that:

$$A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$$

The non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ is called an **eigenvector** of $A$ corresponding to eigenvalue $\lambda$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEERT525 - Module 2):**
> The German prefix *eigen* translates to *"characteristic"* or *"own"*. Therefore, eigenvalues are literally the *characteristic values* that reveal the **intrinsic scaling behaviour** of a linear transformation along specific invariant directions.

### 1.2 The Characteristic Equation

The eigenvalues are obtained as roots of the **characteristic polynomial**:

$$p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

where $I$ is the **identity matrix of order $n$**. This is a polynomial of degree $n$, so $A$ possesses exactly $n$ eigenvalues (counted with algebraic multiplicity) in $\mathbb{C}$.

### 1.3 Diagonalization — Formal Definition

A square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is said to be **diagonalizable** if there exists an invertible matrix $P$ and a diagonal matrix $D$ such that:

$$A = P D P^{-1}$$

Equivalently, $D = P^{-1} A P$. The columns of $P$ are the eigenvectors of $A$ and the diagonal entries of $D$ are the corresponding eigenvalues.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stretching Directions" of a Transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine a square rubber sheet being stretched linearly (a linear transformation). Most points on the sheet move to entirely new directions. However, along **two special directions** (or more in higher dimensions), the points only get **scaled** — they do not rotate.
>
> - The **eigenvectors** are those *special invariant directions*.
> - The **eigenvalues** tell *how much* stretching (or compression) happens along that direction.
>
> If $\lambda > 1$, the direction is stretched; if $0 < \lambda < 1$, it is compressed; if $\lambda < 0$, it is flipped and scaled; if $\lambda = 0$, the dimension is collapsed (singular transformation).

### 1.5 Why Diagonalization Matters in Machine Learning

Diagonalization converts a complex matrix into its simplest form — a diagonal matrix where matrix powers, exponentials, and polynomial evaluations become trivial. This powers several ML algorithms:

- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix.
- **Spectral Clustering** — eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian.
- **Google PageRank** — dominant eigenvector of the link matrix.
- **Stability analysis** of dynamical systems and Recurrent Neural Networks.
- **Matrix functions** such as $A^k$, $e^{A}$, $\sqrt{A}$ in optimization and physics.

### 1.6 Standard Notation Table

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\lambda_i$ | $i$-th eigenvalue |
| $\mathbf{v}_i$ | Eigenvector for $\lambda_i$ |
| $P$ | Modal matrix (columns = eigenvectors) |
| $D, \Lambda$ | Diagonal matrix of eigenvalues |
| $Q$ | Orthogonal matrix ($Q^T Q = I$) |
| $p(\lambda)$ | Characteristic polynomial |
| $\sigma(A)$ | Spectrum (set of all eigenvalues) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eigen Decomposition Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral Decomposition Theorem:**
> A square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is diagonalizable if and only if it has **$n$ linearly independent eigenvectors**. In that case:
>
> $$A = P D P^{-1}$$
>
> The diagonalization is **unique up to** the ordering of eigenvectors and scaling of each eigenvector.

### 2.2 Conditions for Diagonalizability — Step Logic

1. **$A$ must be square** ($n \times n$).
2. The **algebraic multiplicity** (number of times $\lambda_i$ appears as a root of $p(\lambda)$) must equal the **geometric multiplicity** (dimension of the eigenspace) for every eigenvalue.
3. Equivalently, $A$ must have $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
4. **Sufficient condition**: All $n$ eigenvalues are distinct $\Rightarrow A$ is diagonalizable.
5. **Necessary condition**: A diagonalizable matrix satisfies $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ and $\mathrm{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$.

### 2.3 The Spectral Theorem for Symmetric Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> **Spectral Theorem (Real Symmetric Case):**
> If $A = A^{T}$ (real symmetric), then:
>
> 1. All eigenvalues of $A$ are **real**.
> 2. Eigenvectors corresponding to **distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal**.
> 3. $A$ is always diagonalizable by an **orthogonal matrix** $Q$:
>
> $$A = Q \Lambda Q^{T} = Q \Lambda Q^{-1}$$
>
> where $Q^{T} = Q^{-1}$. This is the cornerstone of PCA and SVD.

### 2.4 Multiplicities — A Critical Distinction

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Algebraic Multiplicity** $a(\lambda_i)$ | Power of $(\lambda - \lambda_i)$ in $p(\lambda)$. |
| **Geometric Multiplicity** $g(\lambda_i)$ | $\dim(\text{Null}(A - \lambda_i I))$. |
| **Diagonalizability** | $a(\lambda_i) = g(\lambda_i)$ for every $\lambda_i$. |
| **Defective Matrix** | Some $g(\lambda_i) < a(\lambda_i)$ — not diagonalizable. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Always Confused Point:** A repeated eigenvalue does **NOT** automatically imply non-diagonalizability. It depends on whether enough independent eigenvectors exist. Example: $I_n$ has eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$ with $a(\lambda) = n = g(\lambda)$, so $I_n$ is trivially diagonalizable.

### 2.5 Properties of Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

For an $n \times n$ matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$:

| Property | Formula |
|---|---|
| Sum of eigenvalues | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \mathrm{tr}(A)$ |
| Product of eigenvalues | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \det(A)$ |
| Eigenvalue of $A^k$ | $\lambda_i^k$ |
| Eigenvalue of $A^{-1}$ | $1 \slash \lambda_i$ (if $\lambda_i \neq 0$) |
| Eigenvalue of $A + cI$ | $\lambda_i + c$ |
| Eigenvalue of $cA$ | $c \lambda_i$ |
| Eigenvectors of $A^k$ | Same as $A$ |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Statement | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Eigen equation | $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Defines eigenvalue/eigenvector |
| Characteristic equation | $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ | Compute eigenvalues |
| Eigenspace | $\mathcal{E}_\lambda = \mathrm{Null}(A - \lambda I)$ | Find eigenvectors |
| Eigen-decomposition | $A = PDP^{-1}$ | Diagonal form |
| Symmetric decomposition | $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$ | PCA, SVD link |
| Power of diagonalizable matrix | $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$ | Fast $A^{k}$ computation |
| Matrix exponential | $e^{A} = P \, e^{D} \, P^{-1}$ | ODEs, control theory |
| Polynomial of $A$ | $p(A) = P \, p(D) \, P^{-1}$ | Cayley-Hamilton application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The form $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$ converts an $O(n^{3} \log k)$ matrix power problem into an $O(n)$ problem (just raise each diagonal element to the $k$-th power). This is exactly what we exploit when computing $A^{5}$ or $A^{100}$ in graph algorithms and Markov chains.

### 2.7 When Diagonalization Fails Over $\mathbb{R}$

Some real matrices have **complex eigenvalues** (occurring in conjugate pairs) and cannot be diagonalized over $\mathbb{R}$. Example: rotation by angle $\theta$,

$$R_{\theta} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

has eigenvalues $e^{\pm i\theta}$. For $\theta \neq 0, \pi$, these are non-real $\Rightarrow$ no real eigenbasis $\Rightarrow$ not diagonalizable over $\mathbb{R}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Generic Procedure for Eigen Decomposition

**Given:** Square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Step 1:** Form the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$.

**Step 2:** Solve $p(\lambda) = 0$ to obtain the $n$ eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ (including complex roots if any).

**Step 3:** For each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$, solve the homogeneous system $(A - \lambda_i I) \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ to obtain the eigenvectors. Form the basis of the null space.

**Step 4:** Assemble $P = [\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2 \mid \cdots \mid \mathbf{v}_n]$ and $D = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$.

**Step 5:** Verify $A P = P D$, equivalently $A = P D P^{-1}$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Full Diagonalization (2 × 2 Case)

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Characteristic Polynomial.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$= (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (1)(2)$$

$$= 12 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^{2} - 2$$

$$= \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Eigenvalues.**

$$\lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10 = 0$$

$$(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 2}$$

Since eigenvalues are distinct $\Rightarrow A$ is diagonalizable.

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 5 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row 2 is $-2 \times$ Row 1, so we use Row 1: $-x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x$.

Taking $x = 1$, we obtain $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Both rows are identical: $2x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -2x$.

Taking $x = 1$, we obtain $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 5 — Assemble $P$ and $D$.**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $P^{-1}$.**

$$\det(P) = (1)(-2) - (1)(1) = -3$$

$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-3} \begin{pmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Verify $A = P D P^{-1}$.**

First, $P D$:

$$P D = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

Next, $(P D) P^{-1}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 10 + 2 & 5 - 2 \\ 10 - 4 & 5 + 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 12 & 3 \\ 6 & 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Spectral Decomposition of a Symmetric Matrix

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad A = A^{T}$$

**Step 1 — Characteristic Polynomial.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2 - \lambda)^{2} - 1 = \lambda^{2} - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 3)$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 3, \quad \lambda_2 = 1}$$

Both real, as predicted by the Spectral Theorem.

**Step 2 — Eigenvectors.**

For $\lambda_1 = 3$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_2 = 1$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Orthogonal Normalization.**

$$\|\mathbf{v}_1\| = \sqrt{2}, \quad \|\mathbf{v}_2\| = \sqrt{2}$$

$$\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Note that $\mathbf{q}_1 \cdot \mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 1) = 0$ (orthogonal, as expected).

**Step 4 — Form $Q$ and $\Lambda$.**

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Verify $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$.**

$$Q^{T} = Q^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Since $Q$ is symmetric ($Q = Q^{T}$ here), $Q^{T} = Q$:

$$Q \Lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$Q \Lambda Q^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 3 + 1 & 3 - 1 \\ 3 - 1 & 3 + 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Computing $A^{5}$ via Diagonalization

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Eigenvalues.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (5 - \lambda)(2 - \lambda) - 4 = \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 6 = (\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1)$$

$$\lambda_1 = 6, \quad \lambda_2 = 1$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvectors.**

For $\lambda_1 = 6$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_2 = 1$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Build $P$ and $D$.**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\det(P) = -4 - 1 = -5$$

$$P^{-1} = -\frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $A^{5} = P D^{5} P^{-1}$.**

$$D^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 6^{5} & 0 \\ 0 & 1^{5} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7776 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$P D^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7776 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 31104 & 1 \\ 7776 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^{5} = P D^{5} P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31104 & 1 \\ 7776 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31104 + 1 & 31104 - 4 \\ 7776 - 1 & 7776 + 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31105 & 31100 \\ 7775 & 7780 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{A^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 6221 & 6220 \\ 1555 & 1556 \end{pmatrix}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Direct multiplication of $A$ with itself **5 times** would require 5 matrix multiplications. Diagonalization reduces this to a single $P^{-1} A P$, five scalar powers, and two matrix multiplications — a massive speed-up for large $k$ and large $n$.

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Defective Matrix (Diagonalization Fails)

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Characteristic Polynomial.**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (1 - \lambda)^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1 \text{ (algebraic multiplicity } a(\lambda) = 2)$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvector.**

$$(A - I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow y = 0$$

Only **one** linearly independent eigenvector $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Geometric multiplicity $g(\lambda) = 1 < 2 = a(\lambda)$.

$$\Rightarrow A \text{ is DEFECTIVE and NOT diagonalizable.}$$

This is the Jordan block structure — diagonalization fails but Jordan decomposition still works.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Eigen Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start with square matrix A of order n] --> B1[Form characteristic polynomial p lambda equals det of A minus lambda I]
    B1 --> C1[Solve p lambda equals 0 for eigenvalues]
    C1 --> D1[Eigenvalues lambda 1 to lambda n with multiplicities]
    D1 --> E1[For each lambda, solve A minus lambda I times v equals 0]
    E1 --> F1[Collect eigenvectors v 1 to v n]
    F1 --> G1{Check n linearly independent eigenvectors}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Build matrix P from eigenvectors]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Matrix is defective: no real diagonalization]
    H1 --> J1[Form diagonal matrix D with eigenvalues]
    J1 --> K1[Compute P inverse]
    K1 --> L1[Verify A equals P D P inverse]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Is $A$ Diagonalizable?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Input: Square matrix A] --> B2{Is A square?}
    B2 -- No --> C2[Not diagonalizable]
    B2 -- Yes --> D2[Compute characteristic polynomial]
    D2 --> E2[Solve for eigenvalues]
    E2 --> F2{Are all eigenvalues distinct?}
    F2 -- Yes --> G2[Always diagonalizable]
    F2 -- No --> H2[For each repeated eigenvalue check multiplicities]
    H2 --> I2{Algebraic equals Geometric multiplicity?}
    I2 -- Yes --> G2
    I2 -- No --> J2[Defective: diagonalization fails]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Diagonalization as a Change of Basis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Original Standard Basis] --> B3[Eigenvector Basis P]
    B3 -- P inverse transforms --> C3[Eigenspace Coordinates]
    C3 -- Diagonal matrix D applies scaling --> D3[Scaled Eigenspace Coordinates]
    D3 -- P transforms back --> E3[Transformed Standard Basis]
    E3 --> F3[Result equals A applied to original]
```

### 4.4 Spectral Theorem Flow — Symmetric Matrices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[Real Symmetric Matrix A equals A transpose] --> B4[All eigenvalues are real]
    B4 --> C4[Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal]
    C4 --> D4[Eigenvectors form orthogonal matrix Q]
    D4 --> E4[Decomposition A equals Q Lambda Q transpose]
    E4 --> F4[Application: PCA, SVD, Quadratic Forms]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Power of a Matrix via Diagonalization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A5[Given A and integer k] --> B5[Diagonalize A equals P D P inverse]
    B5 --> C5[Raise D to power k: D k equals diag lambda i to the k]
    C5 --> D5[Reconstruct A k equals P D k P inverse]
    D5 --> E5[Output A to the k with O of n effort]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a square matrix. Hence state the characteristic equation used to compute eigenvalues.**

**Model Answer:**

Let $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ be a square matrix. A scalar $\lambda$ is called an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ such that $A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$. The vector $\mathbf{v}$ is called the **eigenvector** corresponding to $\lambda$.

The eigenvalues are obtained as roots of the **characteristic equation**:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

This is a polynomial of degree $n$ in $\lambda$, yielding $n$ eigenvalues (with algebraic multiplicity, possibly complex).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark):** Always mention that $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector is excluded by definition) and that the equation expands to a degree-$n$ polynomial.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the diagonalization theorem and any two sufficient conditions for a matrix to be diagonalizable.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagonalization Theorem:** A square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is diagonalizable if and only if it possesses $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors. In that case, $A = P D P^{-1}$ where $P$ contains the eigenvectors as columns and $D = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$.

**Two Sufficient Conditions:**

1. **All $n$ eigenvalues are distinct** — guarantees $n$ independent eigenvectors.
2. **$A$ is real symmetric** ($A = A^{T}$) — Spectral Theorem guarantees diagonalization via orthogonal matrix $Q$, i.e., $A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Rule:** Within each module, students answer ONE full 14-mark question (either Q-A or Q-B). Each has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Diagonalization Problem**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper - Module 2]**

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(a)** Find all eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors of $A$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Hence obtain the diagonal matrix $D$ and modal matrix $P$ such that $A = P D P^{-1}$. Verify the result. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Characteristic Polynomial:**

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 = \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 5, \; \lambda_2 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 5$:** Solve $(A - 5I)\mathbf{v} = 0$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow y = x \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$:** Solve $(A - 2I)\mathbf{v} = 0$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Since eigenvalues are distinct and we obtained 2 independent eigenvectors, $A$ is diagonalizable.

---

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $P^{-1}$:

$$\det(P) = -3, \quad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Verification:**

$$P D = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$P D P^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 5 & -4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 12 & 3 \\ 6 & 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Spectral Theorem & Power Computation**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper - Module 2]**

**(a)** State the Spectral Theorem for real symmetric matrices. Diagonalize the symmetric matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ using an orthogonal matrix. (7 marks)

**(b)** For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $A^{5}$ using diagonalization. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Spectral Theorem:** A real symmetric matrix $A = A^{T}$ can always be diagonalized by an orthogonal matrix $Q$:

$$A = Q \Lambda Q^{T} = Q \Lambda Q^{-1}$$

where $Q^{T} Q = I$ and $\Lambda$ is a real diagonal matrix of eigenvalues.

**Diagonalization of $A$:**

Characteristic polynomial: $(2 - \lambda)^{2} - 1 = (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 3) = 0$, so $\lambda_1 = 3$, $\lambda_2 = 1$.

Eigenvectors: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ for $\lambda_1 = 3$, and $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ for $\lambda_2 = 1$.

Normalize (each has norm $\sqrt{2}$):

$$Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \Lambda = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Verification: $Q \Lambda Q^{T} = A$ (shown earlier in Section 3.3).

---

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Eigenvalues:** From characteristic equation $\lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 6 = 0$:

$$\lambda_1 = 6, \quad \lambda_2 = 1$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvectors:**

For $\lambda_1 = 6$: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. For $\lambda_2 = 1$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 3 — Form $P$ and $D$:**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A^{5} = P D^{5} P^{-1}$:**

$$D^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 7776 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$P D^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 31104 & 1 \\ 7776 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^{5} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31104 & 1 \\ 7776 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 31105 & 31100 \\ 7775 & 7780 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{A^{5} = \begin{pmatrix} 6221 & 6220 \\ 1555 & 1556 \end{pmatrix}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Eigen Decomposition Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the non-zero condition** — Eigenvectors must be non-zero. Writing $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ loses 1 mark immediately.
>
> 2. **Confusing algebraic vs geometric multiplicity** — A repeated eigenvalue does NOT mean non-diagonalizable. Always verify the rank of $(A - \lambda I)$ to find independent eigenvectors.
>
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors for orthogonal diagonalization** — For symmetric matrices, the Spectral Theorem requires an **orthogonal** $Q$ ($Q^{T} = Q^{-1}$). Forgetting to normalize loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in $P^{-1}$** — Re-verify $\det(P) \neq 0$ before computing $P^{-1}$. Many students lose marks on sign errors in the cofactor matrix.
>
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — Even if the answer is correct, failing to verify $A = P D P^{-1}$ (or $A P = P D$) loses 1–2 marks. Examiners reward verification.
>
> 6. **Treating complex eigenvalues incorrectly** — Real matrices with complex conjugate eigenvalues cannot be diagonalized over $\mathbb{R}$. State this explicitly.
>
> 7. **Power computation mistake** — When using $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$, students often forget to raise each **diagonal element** to the power $k$. Off-diagonal entries of $D^{k}$ remain zero.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Eigenvalue Equation:** $A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
- **Characteristic Equation:** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ is a degree-$n$ polynomial yielding $n$ eigenvalues.
- **Eigenspace:** $\mathcal{E}_\lambda = \mathrm{Null}(A - \lambda I)$, dimension = geometric multiplicity.
- **Diagonalization:** $A = P D P^{-1}$ requires $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
- **Sufficient Conditions:** Distinct eigenvalues $\Rightarrow$ diagonalizable; Real symmetric $\Rightarrow$ orthogonally diagonalizable.
- **Spectral Theorem:** $A = A^{T} \Rightarrow A = Q \Lambda Q^{T}$ with real $\lambda_i$ and orthogonal $Q$.
- **Trace and Determinant:** $\mathrm{tr}(A) = \sum \lambda_i$ and $\det(A) = \prod \lambda_i$ — quick sanity checks.
- **Power Formula:** $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$ — converts matrix powers to scalar powers.
- **Eigenvalues of $A^{k}$:** equal $\lambda_i^{k}$. Eigenvalues of $A^{-1}$: equal $1 \slash \lambda_i$.
- **Defective Matrix:** $g(\lambda) < a(\lambda)$ for some eigenvalue $\Rightarrow$ NOT diagonalizable.
- **Geometric Multiplicity:** $\dim(\mathrm{Null}(A - \lambda I)) = n - \mathrm{rank}(A - \lambda I)$.
- **Real Matrix with Complex Eigenvalues:** Diagonalization fails over $\mathbb{R}$, succeeds over $\mathbb{C}$.
- **ML Relevance:** PCA uses eigendecomposition of covariance matrix; PageRank uses dominant eigenvector; Spectral clustering uses graph Laplacian eigenvectors.
- **Cayley-Hamilton Link:** Every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation; useful for finding $A^{-1}$ and polynomials of $A$.
- **Numerical Note:** For $n \geq 4$, eigenvalues are typically computed via the QR algorithm, not by direct polynomial root-finding.
- **Board Pattern:** KTU 2024 scheme typically tests either (i) full diagonalization of a 2×2 or 3×3 matrix, (ii) spectral decomposition of a symmetric matrix, or (iii) power computation via $A^{k} = P D^{k} P^{-1}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singular Value Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singular Value Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of SVD

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

**Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** is a fundamental matrix factorization theorem in linear algebra that states any rectangular $m \times n$ real (or complex) matrix $\mathbf{A}$ can be factorized uniquely (up to sign permutations of corresponding vectors) into the product of three matrices:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$$

Where each component has a precise mathematical identity:

| Symbol | Dimensions | Property | Engineering Name |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{U}$ | $m \times m$ | Orthogonal ($\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}$) | Left Singular Vectors |
| $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ | $m \times n$ | Rectangular Diagonal (non-negative) | Singular Value Matrix |
| $\mathbf{V}$ | $n \times n$ | Orthogonal ($\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}$) | Right Singular Vectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEERT525 Module 2):** The diagonal entries $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$ (where $r = \text{rank}(\mathbf{A})$) are called the **Singular Values** of $\mathbf{A}$. They are always real and non-negative, and they equal the square roots of the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow & Light" Intuition

Imagine a sheet of rubber with an arrow painted on it ($\mathbf{A}$ acts on a vector). When you stretch this rubber sheet from two different perpendicular directions (the $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ axes) and apply different scaling strengths along those directions (the $\sigma_i$ values), the resulting deformation of the arrow is exactly what matrix multiplication by $\mathbf{A}$ does.

- **$\mathbf{V}^{T}$** = *Rotation* of the original vector into a special "principal axis" coordinate system.
- **$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$** = *Stretching/Compressing* along each principal axis by amount $\sigma_i$.
- **$\mathbf{U}$** = *Final Rotation* back into the output space.

So $\mathbf{A}$ does nothing fundamentally "weird" — it is just **rotate → scale → rotate**. SVD is the engineer's X-ray that reveals exactly *which* axes, *how much* scaling, and *which* final orientation any linear map performs.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of SVD like a prism breaking white light into its component colors. The original matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is the white light (a complex operation), and SVD reveals its hidden principal components (red, green, blue), each with an intensity equal to its singular value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence, Uniqueness & Properties

SVD is one of the most *robust* theorems in numerical linear algebra because it **always exists for any matrix** — unlike eigendecomposition, which requires a square, diagonalizable matrix.

### 2.1.1 Why does SVD always exist?

The matrices $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ (an $n \times n$ matrix) and $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}$ (an $m \times m$ matrix) are:
- **Symmetric** (since $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})^{T} = \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$).
- **Positive Semi-Definite** (since $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}\|^{2} \geq 0$).

Therefore, the **Spectral Theorem** guarantees that $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ has $n$ real, non-negative eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n$ with an orthonormal set of eigenvectors — these form the columns of $\mathbf{V}$!

## 2.2 The Master Theorem: Constructing SVD

The construction of SVD from $\mathbf{A}$ is performed in five logical steps:

1. Compute the matrix $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ (an $n \times n$ symmetric PSD matrix).
2. Find all eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_n \geq 0$ of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$.
3. The **singular values** are $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, \min(m,n)$.
4. The **right singular vectors** $\mathbf{v}_i$ are the normalized eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$, forming $\mathbf{V}$.
5. The **left singular vectors** $\mathbf{u}_i$ are computed as:
$$\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_i \quad \text{for } \sigma_i > 0$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Full SVD | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$ | General decomposition |
| 2 | Reduced (Thin) SVD | $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}_r \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_r \mathbf{V}_r^{T}$ | Compact storage, where $r = \text{rank}(\mathbf{A})$ |
| 3 | Singular Value definition | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})} = \sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T})}$ | Compute singular values |
| 4 | Right singular vectors | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$ | Eigen-decomposition step |
| 5 | Left singular vectors | $\mathbf{u}_i = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_i$ | Construct $\mathbf{U}$ |
| 6 | Frobenius Norm | $\|\mathbf{A}\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$ | Loss in low-rank approximation |
| 7 | Spectral Norm | $\|\mathbf{A}\|_2 = \sigma_1$ | Matrix operator norm |
| 8 | Rank | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = \text{number of non-zero } \sigma_i$ | Dimensionality check |
| 9 | Pseudo-inverse | $\mathbf{A}^{+} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{+} \mathbf{U}^{T}$ where $\Sigma^{+} = \text{diag}(1/\sigma_i)$ | Solving $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ |
| 10 | Best Rank-$k$ Approximation (Eckart–Young) | $\mathbf{A}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^{T}$ | PCA, image compression, denoising |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eckart–Young Theorem (High-Yield):** For any $k < r$, the matrix $\mathbf{A}_k$ obtained by truncating SVD to the first $k$ singular values is the **best** possible rank-$k$ approximation to $\mathbf{A}$ in both Frobenius and spectral norms. This single theorem justifies the entire field of *dimensionality reduction*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image Compression:** Storing only the top $k$ singular values + corresponding vectors gives massive file-size reduction.
- **Recommender Systems:** Collaborative filtering uses SVD on user-item rating matrices.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) uses truncated SVD on term-document matrices.
- **Denoising:** Small singular values typically correspond to noise; truncating them removes the noise.
- **Numerical Stability:** The pseudo-inverse $\mathbf{A}^+$ via SVD is the standard tool for solving ill-conditioned least-squares problems.
- **Computer Vision:** Pose estimation, structure-from-motion, and face recognition (Eigenfaces) rely on SVD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Walkthrough

## 3.1 Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation: Why $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})}$

Assume SVD exists: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$. Then we can show the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ are exactly $\sigma_i^{2}$.

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T})^{T} (\mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T})$$

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{T} \mathbf{U}^{T}) (\mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T})$$

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{T} (\mathbf{U}^{T} \mathbf{U}) \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$$

Since $\mathbf{U}$ is orthogonal, $\mathbf{U}^{T}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}_m$, and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \dots, \sigma_n^2)$:

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T} = \mathbf{V} \, \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \dots, \sigma_n^2) \, \mathbf{V}^{T}$$

This is the spectral decomposition of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$, proving that $\mathbf{V}$ contains its eigenvectors and the eigenvalues are $\sigma_i^{2}$.

$$\therefore \quad \sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A})} \quad \text{(proof complete)}$$

## 3.2 Construction of $\mathbf{U}$: Derivation of $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i$

Starting from $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (post-multiplying $\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ by $\mathbf{V}$ and using $\mathbf{V}^{T}\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{I}$), the $i$-th column equation is:

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i = \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i$$

Dividing both sides by $\sigma_i$ (assuming $\sigma_i > 0$):

$$\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_i$$

This formula is the workhorse for computing the columns of $\mathbf{U}$ in every numerical SVD algorithm.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example (Hand-Calculation Style)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A $2 \times 2$ problem like this is a *favourite* KTU exam question. Solve it cold — no software!

### Problem
Find the Singular Value Decomposition of:
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$

$$\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9+4 & 6+6 \\ 6+6 & 4+9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13 & 12 \\ 12 & 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$

Characteristic equation:
$$\det(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (13 - \lambda)^2 - 144 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 26\lambda + 169 - 144 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - 26\lambda + 25 = 0$$

Factoring:
$$(\lambda - 25)(\lambda - 1) = 0$$

So $\lambda_1 = 25$ and $\lambda_2 = 1$.

### Step 3 — Singular Values

$$\sigma_1 = \sqrt{25} = 5, \quad \sigma_2 = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Right Singular Vectors (columns of $\mathbf{V}$)

**For $\lambda_1 = 25$:** Solve $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - 25\mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -12 & 12 \\ 12 & -12 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \implies -12x + 12y = 0 \implies x = y$$

Normalize: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$

**For $\lambda_2 = 1$:** Solve $(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 12 \\ 12 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \implies 12x + 12y = 0 \implies x = -y$$

Normalize: $\mathbf{v}_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\mathbf{V} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Left Singular Vectors (columns of $\mathbf{U}$)

Apply $\mathbf{u}_i = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i$:

$$\mathbf{u}_1 = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{5\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{u}_2 = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{U} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6 — Assemble and Verify

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$ first:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now $\mathbf{U} \times$ that:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> This symmetric matrix produced $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{V}$ — a special case. For a general non-symmetric matrix, $\mathbf{U} \neq \mathbf{V}$.

## 3.4 Reduced (Thin) SVD — Reduced Storage Form

For a rank-$r$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ of size $m \times n$, we only need the first $r$ columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}_r \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_r \mathbf{V}_r^{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^{T}$$

This **outer-product decomposition** is the foundation of all SVD-based compression. Storage drops from $mn$ floats to $r(m+n+1)$ floats.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of SVD Computation

The following diagram maps the **step-by-step information flow** when a computer computes the SVD of a matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Input Matrix A m by n"] --> ataMul["Compute A transpose times A"]
    ataMul --> eigSolve["Solve Eigenproblem of A transpose A"]
    eigSolve --> singVals["Extract Singular Values sigma i = sqrt lambda i"]
    singVals --> buildV["Assemble Matrix V from eigenvectors"]
    buildV --> rightVecs["Right Singular Vectors v1 to vn"]
    eigSolve --> constructU["Compute u i equals 1 over sigma i times A v i"]
    rightVecs --> constructU
    constructU --> leftVecs["Left Singular Vectors u1 to um"]
    leftVecs --> buildU["Assemble Matrix U"]
    singVals --> buildSigma["Form Rectangular Diagonal Sigma"]
    buildU --> finalMul["Multiply U times Sigma times V transpose"]
    buildSigma --> finalMul
    rightVecs --> finalMul
    finalMul --> outputVerify["Verify A equals U Sigma V transpose"]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Transformation Block Diagram

The following block diagram describes how SVD interprets any linear transformation as three sequential physical operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputSpace["Input Space R to the n"]
        xVec["Original Vector x"]
    end
    subgraph stage1["Stage 1 Rotation"]
        vRot["Pre multiply by V transpose"]
    end
    subgraph stage2["Stage 2 Anisotropic Scaling"]
        sigmaOp["Multiply by Sigma: stretch axis i by sigma i"]
    end
    subgraph stage3["Stage 3 Rotation"]
        uRot["Post multiply by U"]
    end
    subgraph outputSpace["Output Space R to the m"]
        yVec["Transformed Vector y equals A x"]
    end
    xVec --> vRot
    vRot --> sigmaOp
    sigmaOp --> uRot
    uRot --> yVec
```

## 4.3 SVD Truncation Pipeline for Machine Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData["Raw Data Matrix A of size m by n"] --> fullSVD["Compute Full SVD"]
    fullSVD --> allSigmas["Obtain All Singular Values sigma 1 to sigma r"]
    allSigmas --> keepTop["Retain Only Top k Singular Values k is much less than r"]
    keepTop --> topSingmas["sigma 1 to sigma k"]
    keepTop --> topU["First k columns of U"]
    keepTop --> topV["First k columns of V"]
    topSingmas --> rankK["Construct Rank k Approximation A sub k"]
    topU --> rankK
    topV --> rankK
    rankK --> downstream["Downstream ML Task: PCA, Compression, Recommendation"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Singular Value Decomposition theorem for any real $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$.**

**Model Answer:**
For any real $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$, there exist orthogonal matrices $\mathbf{U}$ (size $m \times m$) and $\mathbf{V}$ (size $n \times n$), and a rectangular diagonal matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (size $m \times n$) with non-negative diagonal entries $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$, such that:
$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$$
The values $\sigma_i$ are called the singular values of $\mathbf{A}$ and equal $\sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})}$, where $r = \text{rank}(\mathbf{A})$. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the geometric interpretation of Singular Value Decomposition?**

**Model Answer:**
Geometrically, SVD decomposes any linear transformation $\mathbf{A}$ into three sequential operations on a vector:
1. **Rotation** by $\mathbf{V}^T$ into a special "principal-axis" coordinate system.
2. **Anisotropic Scaling** by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, stretching each principal axis $i$ by factor $\sigma_i$.
3. **Rotation** by $\mathbf{U}$ into the final output orientation.

Thus, any linear map is fundamentally just a *rotate → scale → rotate* operation, where $\mathbf{U}$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, $\mathbf{V}$ reveal the principal axes and their associated stretching magnitudes. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the singular values of a matrix $\mathbf{A}$ and the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$. Show that $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the full Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Verify your result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Starting from the assumed SVD $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$:

$$\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T)^T (\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T) = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^T\mathbf{U}^T\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$$

Using orthogonality $\mathbf{U}^T\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}$, this simplifies to $\mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^T\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$.

Since $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^T\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \dots, \sigma_n^2)$, we see $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ is diagonalized by $\mathbf{V}$, meaning the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ are $\sigma_i^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

$$\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic equation: $(1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 1 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 1 = 0$

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

Singular values:
$$\sigma_1 = \sqrt{\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}}, \quad \sigma_2 = \sqrt{\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2}}$$

For $\lambda_1$: eigenvector satisfies $(1 - \lambda_1)x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = (\lambda_1 - 1)x = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}x$ (the golden ratio $\varphi$).

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\varphi^2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \varphi \end{bmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_2$: eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\varphi^{-2}}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -\varphi^{-1} \end{bmatrix}$

Compute $\mathbf{U}$ via $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i$. Verification by direct multiplication confirms $\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T = \mathbf{A}$. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** State and prove the **Eckart–Young theorem** for the best rank-$k$ approximation of a matrix using SVD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the matrix $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the rank-1 approximation and the resulting Frobenius-norm error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement (Eckart–Young, 1936):** For any $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ with SVD $\mathbf{A} = \sum_{i=1}^r \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i\mathbf{v}_i^T$ and any integer $0 < k < r$, the matrix:

$$\mathbf{A}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i\mathbf{v}_i^T$$

is the **best rank-$k$ approximation** to $\mathbf{A}$ in both the Frobenius and spectral norms. That is, $\mathbf{A}_k$ minimizes $\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\|$ over all rank-$k$ matrices $\mathbf{B}$.

**Proof Sketch (Frobenius case):** Any rank-$k$ matrix $\mathbf{B}$ has null space of dimension $\geq n - k$. The space spanned by $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ has dimension $k$, so its orthogonal complement has dimension $\geq n - k$, guaranteeing that the intersection with the null space of $\mathbf{B}$ contains a non-zero vector $\mathbf{x}$ with $\|\mathbf{x}\| = 1$. Then:
$$\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\|_F^2 \geq \|(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B})\mathbf{x}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^r \sigma_i^2(\mathbf{v}_i^T\mathbf{x})^2 \geq \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$$
The lower bound equals $\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_k\|_F^2$ and is achieved by $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{A}_k$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

From Section 3.3 we have $\sigma_1 = 5$, $\mathbf{u}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

Rank-1 approximation:
$$\mathbf{A}_1 = \sigma_1 \mathbf{u}_1 \mathbf{v}_1^T = 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.5 \\ 2.5 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Error matrix:
$$\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Frobenius error:
$$\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_1\|_F = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.0$$

Equivalently, $\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{A}_1\|_F = \sqrt{\sigma_2^2} = \sigma_2 = 1$. ✓ **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the square root** — eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ are $\sigma_i^2$, not $\sigma_i$. This single mistake will cost you **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Order convention** — KTU expects singular values in *descending* order: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots$. Reversing this order without relabelling loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** — columns of $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{U}$ *must* be unit vectors. A non-normalized answer is graded as incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing $\mathbf{V}^T$ with $\mathbf{V}^{-1}$** — they are equal only because $\mathbf{V}$ is orthogonal. Write $\mathbf{V}^T$ explicitly for full credit.
> 5. **In truncated SVD questions**, students often forget that *both* $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ must be truncated, and that $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ becomes square of size $k \times k$.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition** — Any $m \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ factors as $\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$, where $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ are orthogonal and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is rectangular diagonal with non-negative entries called *singular values*.
- **Singular values** are the **square roots of eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ (or equivalently $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^T$). Always **non-negative** and conventionally ordered $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots$
- **Right singular vectors** $\mathbf{v}_i$ = normalized eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$.
- **Left singular vectors** $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_i$ for $\sigma_i > 0$.
- **Rank of $\mathbf{A}$** = number of non-zero singular values.
- **Thin/Reduced SVD** keeps only the $r$ non-zero components — essential for storage and noise filtering.
- **Eckart–Young Theorem** — the rank-$k$ truncation $\mathbf{A}_k = \sum_{i=1}^k \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$ is the **provably best** low-rank approximation.
- **Spectral Norm** of $\mathbf{A}$ = $\sigma_1$; **Frobenius Norm** = $\sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$.
- **Pseudo-inverse** $\mathbf{A}^+ = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^+\mathbf{U}^T$ where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^+$ inverts non-zero $\sigma_i$.
- **Order convention:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$.
- **Always verify** by reconstructing $\mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$ and matching with $\mathbf{A}$.
- **ML Applications:** PCA, image compression, recommender systems, LSA, denoising.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matrix Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Approximation** is the process of replacing a given matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with a surrogate matrix $B \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ that has a simpler structural form (e.g., lower rank, sparsity, or non-negativity) while preserving the *most important* information of $A$, formally expressed as the optimization problem
> $$\min_{B \in \mathcal{C}} \, \|A - B\|$$
> where $\mathcal{C}$ is a constrained class of admissible matrices and $\|\cdot\|$ is a chosen matrix norm.

The most prevalent variant in Machine Learning is the **Low-Rank Matrix Approximation (LRMA)**, where $\mathcal{C} = \{ B \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} : \text{rank}(B) \le k \}$ for some target rank $k \ll \min(m, n)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a high-resolution digital photograph of a crowd. If you convert it to a low-resolution JPEG, you lose some details — but the *identity of the scene is preserved*. The pixels that carry the most "energy" (variance) are kept; the imperceptible ones are dropped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Summary Book"**: Think of a 1000-page encyclopedia (matrix $A$). A matrix approximation is a 100-page summary book (matrix $B$) that retains every *headline idea* but omits the *fine print*. The "headlines" correspond to the **largest singular values**, and the "fine print" corresponds to the smallest ones.

### 1.3 Physical & Numerical Constants

- **Frobenius Norm** (default error metric): $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} A_{ij}^2}$
- **Spectral Norm** (worst-case amplification): $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}(A)$
- **Machine Epsilon** for rank decisions: $\tau = 10^{-8} \cdot \sigma_{\max}(A)$ (typical threshold in **MATLAB**/`numpy.linalg.matrix_rank`).

### 1.4 Why Matrix Approximation? (Engineering Motivation)

| Motivation | ML / Engineering Application |
|------------|------------------------------|
| **Compression** | Storage of large user–item rating matrices in recommender systems |
| **Denoising** | Suppressing Gaussian noise in sensor/covariance matrices |
| **Latent Discovery** | Topic modeling (LSA), collaborative filtering, PCA |
| **Computational Speed** | Reducing $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ operations to $\mathcal{O}(nk^2)$ |
| **Visualization** | Projecting high-D data to 2-D / 3-D for plotting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Step Conceptual Pipeline

1. **Decompose** the data matrix $A$ via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD):
$$A = U \Sigma V^{\top}, \quad U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m},\ \Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n},\ V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$
where $U, V$ are orthogonal and $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots, \sigma_r, 0, \dots, 0)$ with $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge \sigma_r > 0$.

2. **Truncate** to the top $k$ components. Construct:
$$U_k \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}, \quad \Sigma_k \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}, \quad V_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$$

3. **Reconstruct** the rank-$k$ approximation:
$$A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \, u_i v_i^{\top}$$

### 2.2 Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem (The Crown Jewel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Eckart–Young–Mirsky, 1936).** For any matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with SVD $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ and any target rank $k \le \text{rank}(A)$, the matrix $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$ is the **best** rank-$k$ approximation in both the spectral and Frobenius norms:
> $$\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$$
> $$\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$$
> Moreover, no other rank-$\le k$ matrix achieves a smaller error.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Norm Type |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | SVD | $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ | — |
| 2 | Spectral norm of $A$ | $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}(A)$ | Operator norm |
| 3 | Frobenius norm of $A$ | $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$ | Hilbert–Schmidt norm |
| 4 | Best rank-$k$ approx. | $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$ | — |
| 5 | Spectral error bound | $\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$ | Spectral |
| 6 | Frobenius error bound | $\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i>k} \sigma_i^2}$ | Frobenius |
| 7 | Variance retained | $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2} \cdot 100\%$ | Energy ratio |
| 8 | Storage cost | $k(m+n+1)$ floats vs. $mn$ floats | Compression |
| 9 | Determinant-like quantity | $\prod_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i$ | Volume scaling |
| 10 | Effective rank | $\left( \sum_i \sigma_i \right)^2 \Big/ \sum_i \sigma_i^2$ | Roy & Vetterli |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** In all formula tables, the notation $\|A\|_2$ refers to the **spectral norm** (= largest singular value), NOT the squared Frobenius norm. Always verify the subscript.

### 2.4 Why SVD Truncation? The "Why" Behind the Theory

- The **singular values** $\sigma_i$ measure the *energy contribution* of each component $u_i v_i^{\top}$.
- Keeping the **largest** $k$ singular values discards the directions of *least variance*, equivalent to projecting onto the principal subspace — this is the bridge to **PCA**.
- The orthogonal bases $U, V$ guarantee that the truncation produces the *minimum* reconstruction error among **all** possible rank-$k$ matrices, not just a candidate class.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Netflix Prize (2006–2009):** User–movie ratings of size $\sim 480{,}000 \times 17{,}000$ were approximated by a rank-$\le 100$ matrix to predict missing entries.
- **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA):** Term–document matrices of size $\sim 10^6 \times 10^5$ are truncated to rank $100$–$300$ to discover topic structure.
- **Image Compression:** Grayscale images are stored via truncated SVD using only $k(m+n+1)$ numbers instead of $mn$ pixels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Best Rank-$k$ Approximation Error in Spectral Norm

We want to show that $\min_{\text{rank}(B) \le k} \|A - B\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$.

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ be the SVD of $A$ with $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge \sigma_r > 0$. Choose any $B$ with $\text{rank}(B) \le k$.

**Step 2 — Kernel Argument.**
Since $\text{rank}(B) \le k$, the null space $\mathcal{N}(B)$ has dimension $\ge n - k$. The rows of the matrix $V^{\top}$ are $n$ orthonormal vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Therefore, in the $(n - k)$-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{N}(B)$, the orthonormal set $\{v_{k+1}, v_{k+2}, \dots, v_n\}$ must contain a non-zero vector $x$ with $\|x\|_2 = 1$ and $Bx = 0$.

**Step 3 — Lower Bound.**
Then:
$$\|A - B\|_2 \ge \|(A - B)x\|_2 = \|Ax\|_2$$
because $Bx = 0$. Now expand $Ax$ in the SVD basis:
$$Ax = U \Sigma V^{\top} x = U \Sigma (V^{\top} x) = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i (v_i^{\top} x) u_i$$
Since $x \in \text{span}\{v_{k+1}, \dots, v_n\}$, we have $v_i^{\top} x = 0$ for $i \le k$, and
$$\|Ax\|_2^2 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2 (v_i^{\top} x)^2 \ge \sigma_{k+1}^2 \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} (v_i^{\top} x)^2 = \sigma_{k+1}^2$$
because $\sum_{i=k+1}^{n} (v_i^{\top} x)^2 = \|V^{\top} x\|_2^2 = \|x\|_2^2 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Achievability.**
Choosing $B = A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$ achieves $\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$ (verified by direct subtraction: $A - A_k = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{\top}$, whose spectral norm is exactly $\sigma_{k+1}$).

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{\min_{\text{rank}(B) \le k} \|A - B\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1} = \|A - A_k\|_2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Rank Approximation of a $3 \times 3$ Matrix

Let
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step A — Compute $A^{\top} A$ and its eigenvalues

$$A^{\top} A = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 & 0 \\ 6 & 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

The top-left $2 \times 2$ block has characteristic equation $(10 - \lambda)^2 - 36 = 0$, giving $\lambda = 16$ and $\lambda = 4$. The third diagonal entry is $4$.

**Eigenvalues of $A^{\top} A$:** $16, 4, 4$. Hence **singular values** of $A$ are $\sigma_1 = 4, \sigma_2 = 2, \sigma_3 = 2$.

#### Step B — Compute Singular Vectors

For $\lambda_1 = 16$: eigenvector $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, 1, 0]^{\top}$. For $\lambda_2 = 4$: eigenvectors $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, -1, 0]^{\top}$ and $[0, 0, 1]^{\top}$. Since $A$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, $U = V$.

$$U = V = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step C — Best Rank-1 Approximation

$$A_1 = \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^{\top} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Errors:**

$$\|A - A_1\|_2 = \sigma_2 = 2, \quad \|A - A_1\|_F = \sqrt{\sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = 2\sqrt{2}$$

#### Step D — Best Rank-2 Approximation

$$A_2 = A_1 + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Errors:**

$$\|A - A_2\|_2 = \sigma_3 = 2, \quad \|A - A_2\|_F = \sigma_3 = 2$$

#### Step E — Full Reconstruction ($k=3$)

$$A_3 = A_2 + 2 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark$$

**Variance retained for $k=1$:** $\dfrac{16}{16 + 4 + 4} = \dfrac{16}{24} = 66.67\%$.

**Variance retained for $k=2$:** $\dfrac{20}{24} = 83.33\%$.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Table

| Rank $k$ | Approximation $A_k$ | Spectral Err. | Frobenius Err. | Energy Retained |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2 | $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ | 66.67% |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2 | 2 | 83.33% |
| 3 | $A$ (exact) | 0 | 0 | 100% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SVD-Based Low-Rank Approximation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input Matrix A m x n"]
    nodeB["Compute SVD A equals U Sigma V transpose"]
    nodeC{"Rank Decision k"}
    nodeD["Truncate U to U sub k"]
    nodeE["Truncate Sigma to Sigma sub k"]
    nodeF["Truncate V to V sub k"]
    nodeG["Reconstruct A sub k equals U sub k Sigma sub k V sub k transpose"]
    nodeH["Output A sub k Best Rank k Approximation"]
    nodeI["Compute Error Norm A minus A sub k"]
    nodeJ["Application: Compression, Denoising, PCA"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Architecture of Truncated SVD

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph decompBlock["Singular Value Decomposition Stage"]
        dir1["U Matrix m x m Orthogonal"]
        dir2["Sigma Matrix m x n Diagonal with singular values"]
        dir3["V Matrix n x n Orthogonal"]
    end

    subgraph truncBlock["Truncation to Rank k Stage"]
        t1["U sub k m x k First k left singular vectors"]
        t2["Sigma sub k k x k Top k singular values"]
        t3["V sub k n x k First k right singular vectors"]
    end

    subgraph reconBlock["Reconstruction Stage"]
        r1["Outer Product Sum from i=1 to k"]
        r2["Final Approximation A sub k"]
    end

    dir1 --> t1
    dir2 --> t2
    dir3 --> t3
    t1 --> r1
    t2 --> r1
    t3 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Singular Value Spectrum & Energy Decay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["Sigma 1 Largest"]
    nodeS2["Sigma 2 Second"]
    nodeS3["Sigma k kth Retained"]
    nodeS4["Sigma k+1 First Discarded"]
    nodeS5["Sigma n Smallest Tail"]
    nodeP1["Dominant Energy Kept"]
    nodeP2["Low Energy Discarded"]

    nodeS1 --> nodeP1
    nodeS2 --> nodeP1
    nodeS3 --> nodeP1
    nodeS4 --> nodeP2
    nodeS5 --> nodeP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading of the Diagram:** In a well-conditioned dataset, the singular-value spectrum exhibits a sharp "elbow" — a steep drop from large $\sigma_i$ to negligible $\sigma_{i+1}$. The position of this elbow is the natural choice for $k$ in matrix approximation (analogous to the scree plot in PCA).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**State the Eckart–Young–Mirsky theorem for best low-rank matrix approximation in both the spectral and Frobenius norms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For any $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with SVD $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ and singular values $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge \sigma_r > 0$, the matrix
$$A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{\top}$$
is the unique best rank-$\le k$ approximation of $A$, satisfying
$$\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1} \quad \text{and} \quad \|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$$
No other matrix of rank $\le k$ can achieve a smaller error under these norms. **[3 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for both norm formulas, 1 for uniqueness claim]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Differentiate between the spectral norm and the Frobenius norm. Why are both relevant in matrix approximation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **spectral norm** $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_{\max}(A)$ measures the *worst-case* stretching of $A$ on a unit vector — it is an operator-induced norm useful for bounding amplification of input errors. The **Frobenius norm** $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} A_{ij}^2} = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$ measures *total* element-wise energy. In matrix approximation, the spectral norm gives a tight *guarantee* on the largest singular component dropped, while the Frobenius norm gives an *aggregate* measure of reconstruction quality across all components. Together they characterize the approximation from both local (worst-case) and global (energy) perspectives. **[3 marks — 1 for each norm definition, 1 for combined relevance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the spectral norm with the Frobenius norm when stating the Eckart–Young theorem; students typically lose **2 marks** here.
> - **Always verify** your SVD by reconstructing $A$ from $U \Sigma V^{\top}$; a missing transpose on $V$ loses **1 full mark**.
> - For numerical problems, **state singular values explicitly** before forming the approximation; merely writing the final matrix $A_k$ without intermediate steps loses **2–3 marks**.
> - **Sort singular values** in descending order; presenting them in ascending order is a recurring deduction worth **1 mark**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** theorem. Discuss how it forms the theoretical foundation for low-rank matrix approximation. Derive the best rank-$k$ approximation error bound in the spectral norm. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For the matrix
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
compute the **SVD**, and then determine the **best rank-1** and **best rank-2** approximations. Verify the Eckart–Young error formulas for both norms. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** SVD states that for any $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, there exist orthogonal $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, diagonal $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with non-negative entries $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge 0$, and orthogonal $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ such that $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$. **[1 mark]**
The $\sigma_i$ are the *singular values*, and the columns $u_i, v_i$ are the left and right singular vectors.
SVD enables low-rank approximation because each component $\sigma_i u_i v_i^{\top}$ is an *outer product* of rank-1, and the magnitude $\sigma_i$ quantifies its energy. Truncating to the top-$k$ terms discards the lowest-energy directions while keeping the dominant structure. **[2 marks]**
**Spectral norm error derivation** (as in §3.1): For any $B$ with $\text{rank}(B) \le k$, there exists unit $x \in \mathcal{N}(B)$ expressible in $\{v_{k+1}, \dots, v_n\}$. Then
