$$\|A - B\|_2 \ge \|Ax\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i>k} \sigma_i^2 (v_i^{\top} x)^2} \ge \sigma_{k+1}$$
The bound is achieved by $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$. **[4 marks]**

**(b)** From the worked example in §3.2: $\sigma_1 = 4, \sigma_2 = 2, \sigma_3 = 2$, and
$$U = V = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \text{diag}(4, 2, 2)$$
**Best rank-1 approximation:** $A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ **[1.5 marks]**
**Best rank-2 approximation:** $A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ **[1.5 marks]**
**Verification:**
- $\|A - A_1\|_2 = \sigma_2 = 2$ ✓ **[1 mark]**
- $\|A - A_1\|_F = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2}$ ✓ **[1 mark]**
- $\|A - A_2\|_2 = \sigma_3 = 2$ ✓ **[1 mark]**
- $\|A - A_2\|_F = \sqrt{2^2} = 2$ ✓ **[1 mark]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Eckart–Young–Mirsky theorem** for low-rank matrix approximation. State the result for both the spectral and Frobenius norms and provide the geometric intuition for why the truncated SVD is optimal. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **engineering and ML applications** of matrix approximation. Explain its role in **(i)** collaborative filtering (recommender systems), **(ii)** image compression, and **(iii)** Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA). Quantify the storage reduction in each case with a formula. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Theorem statement** (Eckart–Young–Mirsky, 1936): The best rank-$\le k$ approximation of $A$ in spectral norm is
$$A_k = \arg\min_{\text{rank}(B) \le k} \|A - B\|_2 = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$$
with $\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$. The same $A_k$ also minimizes the Frobenius norm, with $\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$. **[3 marks]**
**Geometric intuition:** The column space of any rank-$\le k$ matrix lies in some $k$-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{S}$. The residual $A - B$ must therefore have non-trivial component in the *orthogonal complement* of $\mathcal{S}$. The SVD identifies the unique $k$-dimensional subspace (spanned by $\{u_1, \dots, u_k\}$) that minimizes this orthogonal residual, because the largest $k$ singular values represent the directions of maximum variance. Any other rank-$k$ subspace will necessarily include a smaller singular value, increasing the residual. **[4 marks]**

**(b) (i) Collaborative Filtering:** A user–item rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is approximated by $R \approx P Q^{\top}$ where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ encode $k$ latent user/item factors. Storage: from $mn$ to $k(m+n)$. The Netflix dataset reduces from $\sim 8 \times 10^9$ entries to $\sim 10^7$ for $k = 100$. **[2.5 marks]**
**(ii) Image Compression:** A grayscale image $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is stored via truncated SVD $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top}$. Storage: $k(m + n + 1)$ scalars. Compression ratio: $\frac{mn}{k(m+n+1)}$. For $512 \times 512$ at $k = 50$: $\frac{262144}{25650} \approx 10.2\times$ compression. **[2.5 marks]**
**(iii) Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA):** Term–document matrix $T \in \mathbb{R}^{t \times d}$ (e.g., $10^5$ terms $\times$ $10^6$ documents) is truncated to rank $k = 100\text{–}300$. The reduced $U_k$ matrix gives a low-dimensional *semantic* representation of terms, enabling similarity search and topic discovery. Storage: from $td$ to $k(t + d)$, often $> 1000 \times$ reduction. **[2 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Matrix Approximation**

- **Definition:** Replace $A$ by $A_k$ of lower rank, minimizing $\|A - A_k\|$.
- **The Engine:** SVD — $A = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ with $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \cdots \ge \sigma_r > 0$.
- **Best Approximation:** $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{\top} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{\top}$.
- **Spectral Error:** $\|A - A_k\|_2 = \sigma_{k+1}$.
- **Frobenius Error:** $\|A - A_k\|_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$.
- **Energy Retained:** $\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2}$.
- **Storage Cost:** $k(m + n + 1)$ vs. $mn$ — choose $k$ at the "scree elbow".
- **Eckart–Young–Mirsky (1936):** SVD truncation is *provably optimal* in both spectral and Frobenius norms.
- **Algorithm Cost:** Full SVD is $\mathcal{O}(\min(m^2 n, mn^2))$; randomized SVD is $\mathcal{O}(mn \log k)$.
- **ML Bridges:** Truncated SVD = PCA (on centered data) = LSA (on TF-IDF) = collaborative filtering (on ratings).
- **Common Pitfalls:**
  1. Confusing spectral vs. Frobenius errors in answers.
  2. Forgetting to sort singular values in descending order.
  3. Forgetting $V^{\top}$ (not $V$) in the SVD reconstruction.
  4. Using $k \ge r$ where $r = \text{rank}(A)$ — gives $A$ back exactly, not useful.
  5. Reporting only $A_k$ without verifying reconstruction error.
- **Extensions Beyond SVD (Awareness Level):**
  - **CUR Decomposition:** Uses actual rows/columns of $A$ for interpretability.
  - **Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF):** Constrains factors $\ge 0$ for parts-based representations.
  - **Randomized Projections:** Johnson–Lindenstrauss lemma for ultra-fast approximation.
- **Key Theorem to Memorize:** *Eckart–Young–Mirsky* — the SVD truncation is the unique minimizer of both $\|A - B\|_2$ and $\|A - B\|_F$ over all $B$ with $\text{rank}(B) \le k$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differentiation of Univariate Functions - Partial Differentiation and Gradients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differentiation of Univariate Functions - Partial Differentiation and Gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Univariate Differentiation — Formal Definition

A **univariate function** $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ accepts exactly one real input and produces one real output. The **derivative** of such a function at a point $x_0$ quantifies the **instantaneous rate of change** of the function's output with respect to its input.

Formally, the derivative is defined as the two-sided limit of the difference quotient:

$$f'(x_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}$$

provided this limit exists and is finite. The derivative $f'(x_0)$ is also denoted $\dfrac{df}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=x_0}$ in Leibniz notation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Computational Fundamentals for Machine Learning*, differentiation is not studied as an abstract calculus exercise. It is studied because **gradient-based optimization** (the engine behind training neural networks, logistic regression, support vector machines, and almost every ML model) is built entirely on derivatives. You cannot understand *backpropagation*, *gradient descent*, or *Jacobian/Hessian analysis* without mastering the material in this module.

### 1.2 Partial Differentiation — Formal Definition

A **multivariate function** $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ accepts $n$ real inputs $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and produces one real output. Its derivative is no longer a single number — it becomes a vector of partial derivatives called the **gradient**.

The **partial derivative of $f$ with respect to $x_i$** at a point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (x_{0,1}, x_{0,2}, \ldots, x_{0,n})$ is defined as:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x_{0,1}, \ldots, x_{0,i} + h, \ldots, x_{0,n}) - f(x_{0,1}, \ldots, x_{0,i}, \ldots, x_{0,n})}{h}$$

Conceptually, the partial derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ is the **ordinary derivative** of $f$ taken while **temporarily freezing every input except the $i$-th one** at its current value. This single idea — *freeze all other variables and differentiate* — is the conceptual heart of the entire module.

### 1.3 The Gradient — Formal Definition

The **gradient** of a scalar-valued multivariate function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the vector formed by stacking all $n$ partial derivatives:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)^{\top}$$

Geometrically, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is a vector at the point $\mathbf{x}_0$ that:
- **Points in the direction of steepest ascent** of $f$.
- Has a **magnitude equal to the rate of change** in that direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Machine Learning:** During training, a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ measures how "wrong" a model's predictions are. Gradient descent updates parameters as $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \eta \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$. **You cannot train a model without computing gradients.** This is why Module 3 is foundational.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Hiking Mountain

Imagine you are standing somewhere on a foggy mountain. You cannot see the summit, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet.

- The **height** of the mountain at any point is your function $f(x, y)$.
- The **slope of the ground under your feet** in the east-west direction is $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$.
- The **slope of the ground under your feet** in the north-south direction is $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$.
- The **gradient $\nabla f$** is a compass arrow telling you **which way to walk to climb the fastest**.
- If you want to *descend* (find the valley), you walk in the **opposite direction** $-\nabla f$. This is gradient descent.

### 1.5 Notation Standards

| Symbol | Meaning | Context |
|---|---|---|
| $f'(x)$ | Ordinary derivative | Univariate functions |
| $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ | Leibniz derivative | Both univariate and multivariate (one variable) |
| $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ | Partial derivative | Multivariate functions |
| $\nabla f$ | Gradient vector | Multivariate scalar-valued functions |
| $\mathbf{J}$ | Jacobian matrix | Multivariate vector-valued functions |
| $\mathbf{H}$ | Hessian matrix | Second-order partial derivatives |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examination Tip:** Examiners will *always* deduct marks if you use $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ when you mean a *partial* derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ in a multivariate setting. Memorize the distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rules of Univariate Differentiation (Quick Recap)

Before extending to multiple variables, the standard derivative rules for univariate $f(x)$ must be firmly in place. These rules are the building blocks for partial differentiation.

**Power Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}[x^n] = n x^{n-1}$

**Constant Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}[c] = 0$ for any constant $c \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Sum Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}[f(x) + g(x)] = f'(x) + g'(x)$

**Product Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}[f(x) \cdot g(x)] = f'(x) g(x) + f(x) g'(x)$

**Quotient Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right] = \dfrac{f'(x) g(x) - f(x) g'(x)}{[g(x)]^{2}}$, provided $g(x) \neq 0$.

**Chain Rule:** If $y = f(g(x))$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$.

**Exponential & Logarithm:**
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[e^{x}] = e^{x}$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[a^{x}] = a^{x} \ln(a)$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\ln(x)] = \dfrac{1}{x}$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_{a}(x)] = \dfrac{1}{x \ln(a)}$

**Trigonometric:**
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\sin(x)] = \cos(x)$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\cos(x)] = -\sin(x)$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\tan(x)] = \sec^{2}(x)$

> [!NOTE]
> These rules are **not re-derived** when computing partial derivatives — they are applied **variable by variable**, treating every other variable as a constant.

### 2.2 Operational Steps for Computing Partial Derivatives

The procedure is mechanical and consistent:

1. **Identify** the variable of differentiation (say, $x_i$). All other variables are treated as **constants**.
2. **Apply** the standard univariate differentiation rules (power, product, quotient, chain) with respect to $x_i$ only.
3. **Simplify** the resulting expression.
4. **Repeat** for every variable $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ to build the gradient.

**Higher-Order Partial Derivatives:** Partial derivatives can themselves be differentiated again. For a function $f(x, y)$:

- $f_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}$ — differentiate twice w.r.t. $x$.
- $f_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y^{2}}$ — differentiate twice w.r.t. $y$.
- $f_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \, \partial x}$ — first w.r.t. $x$, then w.r.t. $y$.
- $f_{yx} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \, \partial y}$ — first w.r.t. $y$, then w.r.t. $x$.

**Clairaut's Theorem (Equality of Mixed Partials):** If $f$, $f_x$, $f_y$, $f_{xy}$, and $f_{yx}$ are all continuous on an open region, then $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$ at every point in that region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This theorem is the reason the **Hessian matrix** is symmetric — its $(i,j)$ entry equals $f_{x_i x_j}$ and equals $f_{x_j x_i}$, so $H = H^{\top}$. KTU frequently tests this property.

### 2.3 The Gradient — Geometry and Directional Derivatives

The **directional derivative** of $f$ at point $\mathbf{x}_0$ in the direction of a unit vector $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n)$ (so $\|\mathbf{u}\| = 1$) is:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_0 + h \mathbf{u}) - f(\mathbf{x}_0)}{h}$$

This is the rate of change of $f$ as we move from $\mathbf{x}_0$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$.

**Fundamental Theorem Connecting Gradient and Directional Derivative:**

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) \cdot \mathbf{u}$$

This single equation is **the most important result in the entire module**. It tells us that:
- The gradient is a linear operator that maps directions to rates of change.
- The **maximum** value of $D_{\mathbf{u}} f$ over all unit vectors $\mathbf{u}$ is $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)\|$, achieved when $\mathbf{u}$ is parallel to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)$.
- The **minimum** value is $-\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)\|$, achieved when $\mathbf{u}$ points opposite to the gradient.

### 2.4 Critical Points and the Gradient

A point $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ is called a **critical point** (or **stationary point**) of $f$ if:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$$

That is, every partial derivative equals zero. To classify a critical point, the second derivative test uses the **Hessian matrix**:

$$\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_1^{2}} & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\ \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_2^{2}} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_n^{2}} \end{bmatrix}$$

For a two-variable function $f(x, y)$, let $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ be the determinant of the $2 \times 2$ Hessian at a critical point $(x_0, y_0)$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Univariate derivative | $f'(x_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}$ | Limit must exist and be finite |
| Partial derivative w.r.t. $x_i$ | $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(\ldots, x_i + h, \ldots) - f(\ldots, x_i, \ldots)}{h}$ | All other variables held constant |
| Gradient vector | $\nabla f = \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right)^{\top}$ | Column vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| Directional derivative | $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{u}$ | Requires $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$ |
| Chain rule (multivariate) | If $z = f(\mathbf{x})$, $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{g}(t)$, then $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \dfrac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt}$ | Composite scalar function |
| Chain rule (general) | If $z = f(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))$, then $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x_i} = \sum_{k} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial g_k} \dfrac{\partial g_k}{\partial x_i}$ | Sum over intermediate variables |
| Critical point condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for local extremum |
| Hessian matrix entry | $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Symmetric under Clairaut's theorem |
| Second derivative test | $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ | $D > 0, f_{xx} > 0$: local min |
| Second derivative test | $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ | $D > 0, f_{xx} < 0$: local max |
| Second derivative test | $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ | $D < 0$: saddle point |
| Gradient descent update | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \eta \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$ | $\eta$ is the learning rate |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Learning Training:** The update rule $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is performed billions of times when training deep neural networks.
- **Computer Vision:** Image filters and edge detectors (Sobel, Prewitt) are discrete approximations to partial derivatives in $x$ and $y$ directions.
- **Robotics & Control:** The Jacobian $\mathbf{J} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{x}_{end\text{-}effector}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}$ relates joint angles to end-effector velocities.
- **Optimization:** Constrained and unconstrained optimization problems in operations research rely on gradient and Hessian information.
- **Physics Simulation:** Gradient fields model gravitational, electric, and magnetic potentials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Partial Derivatives of a Multivariate Polynomial

**Problem:** Given $f(x, y, z) = x^{2} y + 3 x z^{3} - 5 y^{2} z + 7$, compute all three partial derivatives and the gradient vector.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $x$ (treat $y$ and $z$ as constants):**

Term-by-term analysis:
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[x^{2} y] = 2x \cdot y = 2xy$ (power rule on $x$, $y$ is constant)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[3 x z^{3}] = 3 z^{3}$ (power rule on $x$, $z^{3}$ is constant)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[-5 y^{2} z] = 0$ (no $x$ appears)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}[7] = 0$ (constant)

Summing these:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2xy + 3z^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $y$ (treat $x$ and $z$ as constants):**

- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}[x^{2} y] = x^{2}$ (power rule on $y$)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}[3 x z^{3}] = 0$ (no $y$ appears)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}[-5 y^{2} z] = -10 y z$ (power rule: $2 \cdot (-5) y^{1} z = -10 y z$)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}[7] = 0$

Summing these:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = x^{2} - 10yz$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $z$ (treat $x$ and $y$ as constants):**

- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}[x^{2} y] = 0$ (no $z$ appears)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}[3 x z^{3}] = 9 x z^{2}$ (power rule: $3 \cdot 3 x z^{2} = 9 x z^{2}$)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}[-5 y^{2} z] = -5 y^{2}$ (power rule: coefficient $-5 y^{2}$ survives)
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}[7] = 0$

Summing these:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial z} = 9xz^{2} - 5y^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla f(x, y, z) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial z} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2xy + 3z^{3} \\[4pt] x^{2} - 10yz \\[4pt] 9xz^{2} - 5y^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Directional Derivative Computation

**Problem:** Let $f(x, y) = x^{2} y + 2 y^{2}$. At the point $P = (1, 2)$, find the directional derivative in the direction of the vector $\mathbf{v} = (3, 4)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient $\nabla f(x, y)$:**

$\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2xy$ and $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = x^{2} + 4y$.

So:

$$\nabla f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 2xy \\ x^{2} + 4y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the gradient at $P = (1, 2)$:**

$$\nabla f(1, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(1)(2) \\ (1)^{2} + 4(2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize the direction vector $\mathbf{v} = (3, 4)$ to a unit vector $\mathbf{u}$:**

$$\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{v}\|} = \left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the directional derivative via the dot product:**

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(1, 2) = \nabla f(1, 2) \cdot \mathbf{u} = (4)\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) + (9)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)$$

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(1, 2) = \frac{12}{5} + \frac{36}{5} = \frac{48}{5} = 9.6$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Interpretation:** The function $f$ increases at a rate of $9.6$ units per unit step when moving from $(1, 2)$ in the direction $(3/5, 4/5)$.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Critical Point Classification Using the Hessian

**Problem:** Find and classify all critical points of $f(x, y) = x^{3} - 3xy^{2} + y^{3}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute first-order partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3y^{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -6xy + 3y^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Set both partial derivatives to zero to find critical points:**

From $f_{x} = 0$: $3x^{2} - 3y^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x^{2} = y^{2} \Rightarrow y = \pm x$.

From $f_{y} = 0$: $-6xy + 3y^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow 3y(-2x + y) = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$ or $y = 2x$.

**Step 3 — Solve the system by considering all combinations:**

*Case A:* $y = 0$. Then $y = \pm x$ gives $x = 0$. Critical point: $(0, 0)$.

*Case B:* $y = 2x$ and $y = x$. Substituting: $2x = x \Rightarrow x = 0$, then $y = 0$. Same point.

*Case C:* $y = 2x$ and $y = -x$. Substituting: $2x = -x \Rightarrow 3x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$, $y = 0$. Same point.

So the only critical point is $(0, 0)$.

**Step 4 — Compute second-order partial derivatives (the Hessian entries):**

$$f_{xx} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^{2} - 3y^{2}) = 6x$$

$$f_{yy} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-6xy + 3y^{2}) = -6x + 6y$$

$$f_{xy} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^{2} - 3y^{2}) = -6y$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Hessian at $(0, 0)$ and compute the determinant:**

$$\mathbf{H}(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 6(0) & -6(0) \\ -6(0) & -6(0) + 6(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2} = (0)(0) - (0)^{2} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Inconclusive second-derivative test:** Since $D = 0$, the second derivative test is inconclusive. We must examine the function directly.

Note that $f(0,0) = 0$. Along the line $y = x$: $f(x, x) = x^{3} - 3x \cdot x^{2} + x^{3} = 2x^{3} - 3x^{3} = -x^{3}$. For small positive $x$, $f(x, x) = -x^{3} < 0$. For small negative $x$, $f(x, x) > 0$.

So $(0, 0)$ is a **saddle point** — neither a maximum nor a minimum.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Multivariable Chain Rule

**Problem:** Let $z = f(x, y) = x^{2} + xy + y^{2}$, and let $x = t^{2}$ and $y = \sin(t)$. Find $\dfrac{dz}{dt}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x + y$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = x + 2y$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 2t$$

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \cos(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the multivariable chain rule:**

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = (2x + y)(2t) + (x + 2y)\cos(t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $x = t^{2}$ and $y = \sin(t)$:**

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = (2t^{2} + \sin(t))(2t) + (t^{2} + 2\sin(t))\cos(t)$$

Expanding:

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = 4t^{3} + 2t \sin(t) + t^{2} \cos(t) + 2\sin(t)\cos(t)$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Partial Derivative Identification Table

| Function Form | Partial Derivative Type | Common Mistake | Correct Procedure |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x^{n} y^{m}$ (single term) | Mixed-power | Differentiating both variables at once | Differentiate the active variable only; keep the other as a constant coefficient |
| $\sin(x y)$ | Composite in two variables | Forgetting to apply chain rule for the inner variable | Apply product rule: $\cos(xy) \cdot \dfrac{\partial(xy)}{\partial x} = y \cos(xy)$ |
| $e^{x+y}$ | Exponential composite | Differentiating only $e^{x}$ and missing $e^{y}$ contribution | Use chain rule: $e^{x+y} \cdot \dfrac{\partial(x+y)}{\partial x} = e^{x+y}$ |
| $\ln(x^{2} + y^{2})$ | Log of composite | Dropping the inner derivative | $\dfrac{2x}{x^{2} + y^{2}}$ — quotient rule / chain rule needed |
| Constant term $c$ | Zero | Carrying the constant through | $\dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x_i} = 0$ — always discard constants after differentiation |
| Independent variable | Identity | Confusing partial and total derivatives | $\dfrac{\partial x}{\partial x} = 1$, $\dfrac{\partial x}{\partial y} = 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Univariate Derivative to Multivariate Gradient

The following block diagram traces how a single-variable derivative concept is extended to multiple variables, ultimately leading to the gradient — the central object in machine learning optimization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Univariate Function f of x"] --> nodeB["Derivative as Slope of Tangent Line"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Limit Definition f prime of x"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Multivariate Function f of x sub 1 to x sub n"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Partial Derivative: Freeze All Variables Except One"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute n Partial Derivatives Separately"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Stack as Column Vector"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Gradient Vector nabla f in R to the n"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Directional Derivative via Dot Product with Unit Vector"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Gradient Descent Update Rule theta new equals theta old minus eta nabla L"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Maximum Rate of Ascent equals Norm of nabla f"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Machine Learning Model Training Loop"]
```

### 4.2 Architecture of the Gradient Descent Algorithm

The following block diagram maps the functional architecture of how gradients are used in iterative optimization — the backbone of nearly every ML training pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Initialize Parameters theta sub 0 Randomly"] --> nodeB["Forward Pass: Compute Predictions y hat"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Loss Function L of theta measures Error"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Backward Pass: Compute nabla L of theta"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Update Step: theta sub t plus 1 equals theta sub t minus eta nabla L of theta sub t"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Convergence Check Loss below threshold"}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeB
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG["Trained Model Parameters theta asterisk"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Partial Derivatives dL by d theta sub i for each i"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Chain Rule Applied Layer by Layer Backpropagation"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Partial Derivative Computation Pipeline

This schematic represents the sequential logical steps executed by hand (or in symbolic computation engines like SymPy) when computing partial derivatives of a multivariate function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input Multivariate Function f of x sub 1 to x sub n"] --> nodeB["Identify Variable of Interest x sub i"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Treat All Other Variables as Constants"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Apply Standard Differentiation Rules Power, Product, Quotient, Chain"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Simplify the Expression"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Store Result as d f by d x sub i"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Have All n Variables Been Processed"}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeB
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH["Assemble Column Vector nabla f"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Optional Evaluate at Point x sub 0"]
```

### 4.4 Geometric Visualization — Gradient as Compass on a Contour Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Contour Map of f at Multiple Levels"] --> nodeB["Point x sub 0 on a Contour Line"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Gradient Vector nabla f at x sub 0"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Perpendicular to Contour Line"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Points Toward Higher f Values"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Magnitude equals Steepness"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

Define the **gradient** of a scalar-valued multivariate function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. State its geometric significance.

**Model Answer:**

The gradient of a scalar-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the vector of all first-order partial derivatives:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)^{\top}$$

**Geometric Significance:**
1. The gradient at any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** (greatest rate of increase) of $f$.
2. The magnitude $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0)\|$ equals the **maximum directional derivative** at that point.
3. The gradient is **perpendicular to the level curves** (or level surfaces) of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

State **Clairaut's Theorem** on the equality of mixed partial derivatives. Why is it important for the Hessian matrix?

**Model Answer:**

**Clairaut's Theorem (Schwarz's Theorem):** If $f(\mathbf{x})$ has continuous partial derivatives $f_{x_i}$, $f_{x_j}$, $f_{x_i x_j}$, and $f_{x_j x_i}$ in an open region containing $\mathbf{x}_0$, then:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \, \partial x_j} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_j \, \partial x_i} \quad \text{at } \mathbf{x}_0$$

**Importance for the Hessian:** Since the Hessian matrix has entries $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \, \partial x_j}$, Clairaut's theorem guarantees $H_{ij} = H_{ji}$, meaning the Hessian is **symmetric**: $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}^{\top}$. This symmetry is essential in optimization theory, ensures real eigenvalues, and guarantees the validity of the second-derivative test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Given $f(x, y) = x^{3} + 3x^{2}y - 3xy^{2} - y^{3} + 2x - 5y$, compute the gradient $\nabla f(x, y)$ and the directional derivative at the point $(1, -1)$ in the direction of the vector $\mathbf{v} = (4, -3)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Find and classify all critical points of $f(x, y) = x^{3} + y^{3} - 3xy$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} + 6xy - 3y^{2} + 2$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 3x^{2} - 6xy - 3y^{2} - 5$$

**Step 2 — Write the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 3x^{2} + 6xy - 3y^{2} + 2 \\ 3x^{2} - 6xy - 3y^{2} - 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\nabla f$ at $(1, -1)$:**

$f_x(1, -1) = 3(1)^{2} + 6(1)(-1) - 3(-1)^{2} + 2 = 3 - 6 - 3 + 2 = -4$

$f_y(1, -1) = 3(1)^{2} - 6(1)(-1) - 3(-1)^{2} - 5 = 3 + 6 - 3 - 5 = 1$

$$\nabla f(1, -1) = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize $\mathbf{v} = (4, -3)$:**

$$\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{4^{2} + (-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\mathbf{u} = \left(\frac{4}{5}, -\frac{3}{5}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the directional derivative:**

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(1, -1) = \nabla f(1, -1) \cdot \mathbf{u} = (-4)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) + (1)\left(-\frac{3}{5}\right)$$

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(1, -1) = -\frac{16}{5} - \frac{3}{5} = -\frac{19}{5} = -3.8$$

**Interpretation:** The function $f$ decreases at a rate of $3.8$ units per unit step when moving from $(1, -1)$ in the direction $(4, -3)$.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the first partial derivatives:**

$$f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3y$$

$$f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 3y^{2} - 3x$$

**Step 2 — Set up the system $f_x = 0$ and $f_y = 0$:**

$3x^{2} - 3y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x^{2}$

$3y^{2} - 3x = 0 \Rightarrow x = y^{2}$

**Step 3 — Substitute $y = x^{2}$ into $x = y^{2}$:**

$$x = (x^{2})^{2} = x^{4}$$

$$x^{4} - x = 0 \Rightarrow x(x^{3} - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x(x - 1)(x^{2} + x + 1) = 0$$

The quadratic $x^{2} + x + 1$ has discriminant $1 - 4 = -3 < 0$, so no real roots.

Real solutions: $x = 0$ or $x = 1$.

**Step 4 — Compute corresponding $y$ values:**

For $x = 0$: $y = 0^{2} = 0$. Critical point: $(0, 0)$.

For $x = 1$: $y = 1^{2} = 1$. Critical point: $(1, 1)$.

**Step 5 — Compute second partial derivatives (Hessian entries):**

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{yy} = 6y, \quad f_{xy} = -3$$

The Hessian determinant:

$$D(x, y) = f_{xx} \cdot f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2} = (6x)(6y) - (-3)^{2} = 36xy - 9$$

**Step 6 — Classify each critical point:**

**At $(0, 0)$:** $D(0, 0) = 36(0)(0) - 9 = -9 < 0$. Since $D < 0$, the point $(0, 0)$ is a **saddle point**.

**At $(1, 1)$:** $D(1, 1) = 36(1)(1) - 9 = 27 > 0$. Since $D > 0$ and $f_{xx}(1, 1) = 6 > 0$, the point $(1, 1)$ is a **local minimum**.

The minimum value is $f(1, 1) = (1)^{3} + (1)^{3} - 3(1)(1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compute all four second-order partial derivatives $f_{xx}$, $f_{yy}$, $f_{xy}$, $f_{yx}$ of $f(x, y) = x e^{y} + y \sin(x)$. Verify Clairaut's theorem. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Let $z = f(x, y) = x^{2} + 2xy + y^{2}$ with $x = u v$ and $y = u + v$. Using the multivariable chain rule, compute $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial u}$ and $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial v}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute first-order partial derivatives:**

$$f_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x e^{y} + y \sin(x)) = e^{y} + y \cos(x)$$

$$f_y = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x e^{y} + y \sin(x)) = x e^{y} + \sin(x)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f_{xx}$:**

$$f_{xx} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(e^{y} + y \cos(x)) = -y \sin(x)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $f_{yy}$:**

$$f_{yy} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x e^{y} + \sin(x)) = x e^{y}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f_{xy}$:**

$$f_{xy} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(e^{y} + y \cos(x)) = e^{y} + \cos(x)$$

**Step 5 — Compute $f_{yx}$:**

$$f_{yx} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x e^{y} + \sin(x)) = e^{y} + \cos(x)$$

**Step 6 — Verify Clairaut's Theorem:**

Comparing Step 4 and Step 5:

$$f_{xy} = e^{y} + \cos(x) = f_{yx}$$

Therefore $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$, confirming Clairaut's theorem for this function.

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x + 2y$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2x + 2y$$

**Step 2 — Compute partials of the inner variables:**

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial u} = v, \quad \frac{\partial x}{\partial v} = u$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial u} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial v} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial u}$:**

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial u} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{\partial x}{\partial u} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial u}$$

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial u} = (2x + 2y)(v) + (2x + 2y)(1) = (2x + 2y)(v + 1)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $x = uv$, $y = u + v$:**

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial u} = \bigl(2uv + 2(u + v)\bigr)(v + 1) = (2uv + 2u + 2v)(v + 1)$$

Expanding:

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial u} = (2u + 2v)(v + 1)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the chain rule for $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial v}$:**

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial v} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{\partial x}{\partial v} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial v}$$

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial v} = (2x + 2y)(u) + (2x + 2y)(1) = (2x + 2y)(u + 1)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial v} = (2uv + 2u + 2v)(u + 1) = (2u + 2v)(u + 1)(v + 1)$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Normalize the Direction Vector:** When asked for the directional derivative, students frequently compute $\nabla f \cdot \mathbf{v}$ using the *non-unit* vector $\mathbf{v}$. This is wrong by a factor of $\|\mathbf{v}\|$. Always normalize: $\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{v}\|}$.
>
> 2. **Using $df$ instead of $\partial f$:** When the function has multiple variables and you differentiate with respect to only one, you **must** write $\partial f$, not $df$. Examiners explicitly check this notation. Marks are deducted for the wrong differential symbol.
>
> 3. **Treating All Variables as Independent during Chain Rule:** When $x = x(t)$ and $y = y(t)$ are functions of a common parameter, students sometimes write $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ *only*, omitting the $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dt}$ term. **Both terms must appear.**
>
> 4. **Misclassifying Critical Points:** When $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2} = 0$, the second-derivative test is **inconclusive** — not automatically a maximum or minimum. You must check the sign of $f$ along nearby directions.
>
> 5. **Confusing the Gradient with the Jacobian:** The gradient $\nabla f$ is a column vector defined only for scalar-valued functions. For vector-valued functions, the analogous object is the **Jacobian matrix**. Examiners will deduct marks if these are conflated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**
>
> - **Univariate derivative definition:** $f'(x_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}$.
> - **Partial derivative operational rule:** Treat all other variables as constants; differentiate using standard rules.
> - **Gradient notation:** $\nabla f = \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right)^{\top}$ — column vector.
> - **Directional derivative formula:** $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{u}$, requires $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$.
> - **Steepest ascent direction** is $\dfrac{\nabla f}{\Vert \nabla f \Vert}$.
> - **Steepest descent direction** is $-\dfrac{\nabla f}{\Vert \nabla f \Vert}$.
> - **Critical point:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$.
> - **Second derivative test (2D):** Compute $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}$ at the critical point.
>   - $D > 0$, $f_{xx} > 0$: Local **minimum**.
>   - $D > 0$, $f_{xx} < 0$: Local **maximum**.
>   - $D < 0$: **Saddle point**.
>   - $D = 0$: **Inconclusive** — analyze directly.
> - **Clairaut's theorem:** Under continuity conditions, $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$. This makes the **Hessian symmetric**.
> - **Multivariable chain rule:** $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial t} = \sum_{k} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_k} \cdot \dfrac{\partial x_k}{\partial t}$.
> - **Gradient descent update (the ML connection):** $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \eta \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$.
> - **Geometric interpretation:** Gradient is perpendicular to level curves / level surfaces.
> - **Distinguish clearly:** $\nabla f$ (scalar-to-vector), $\mathbf{J}$ Jacobian (vector-to-vector matrix), $\mathbf{H}$ Hessian (matrix of second partials).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradients of Vector-Valued Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradients of Vector-Valued Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In multivariable calculus, the **gradient operator** $\nabla$ is canonically defined only for **scalar-valued functions** $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$, producing the familiar gradient vector

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\ \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}.$$

For a **vector-valued function** $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$, defined as

$$\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(\mathbf{x}) \\ f_2(\mathbf{x}) \\ \vdots \\ f_m(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix},$$

the natural, mathematically rigorous generalisation of "the gradient" is the **Jacobian matrix** (denoted $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x})$, $D\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, or $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$), an $m \times n$ matrix of all first-order partial derivatives:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_n} \\[6pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}.$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key terminological clarity (KTU Board favourite):** When $m = 1$ (i.e., a scalar function), the Jacobian matrix collapses into a single column — exactly the gradient vector. Hence, the **Jacobian is the matrix-form generalisation of the gradient** to vector-valued functions. Many ML textbooks loosely write "gradient of a vector function" to mean the Jacobian.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Weather Station Network.**
> Imagine you are standing in a 3-D landscape (coordinates $x, y, z$). A **scalar function** is like a single thermometer at your location — it reports one number (say, temperature). The **gradient** is a tiny arrow that points in the direction of fastest temperature increase, with magnitude equal to that rate.
> A **vector-valued function** is like a **weather station with $m$ different sensors** at every point (temperature, humidity, wind-speed, pressure, …). The "gradient" of this vector field must now describe, for **each of the $m$ sensors**, how it changes in **each of the $n$ directions**. That is a table — a matrix — of size $m \times n$. This table is the **Jacobian matrix**.
> In **machine learning**, the loss $\mathcal{L}$ is scalar, but the network's intermediate activations are vectors. To push gradients all the way back to the weights, we need to compose Jacobians — which is exactly the **back-propagation algorithm**.

### 1.3 Important Special Cases (Highlighted for KTU)

- **Scalar-to-scalar ($m = n = 1$):** Jacobian is a $1 \times 1$ matrix equal to $f'(x)$.
- **Scalar-valued ($m = 1$):** Jacobian is $1 \times n$, identical to $\nabla f^{\top}$ (a row vector) or $\nabla f$ (column vector) depending on convention.
- **Vector-valued, square ($m = n$):** Jacobian is square, and its **determinant** is the local volume-scaling factor of the mapping (used in change-of-variables, e.g., normalizing flows).
- **Linear map $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$:** The Jacobian is the constant matrix $\mathbf{A}$ itself.

### 1.4 The Directional Derivative Interpretation

For any unit direction vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, the **directional derivative** of $\mathbf{f}$ at $\mathbf{x}$ along $\mathbf{v}$ is the $m$-vector

$$D_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x})\, \mathbf{v},$$

i.e., a **matrix-vector product** of the Jacobian with the direction. The Jacobian therefore completely characterises all first-order local behaviour of $\mathbf{f}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Row-wise and Column-wise Interpretation of the Jacobian

The Jacobian admits two equally important structural views, both heavily tested in KTU examinations:

**(A) Row-wise (each row is a gradient):** The $i$-th row of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ is the gradient of the $i$-th scalar component, transposed:

$$\text{Row}_i\bigl(\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\bigr) \;=\; \nabla f_i(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}.$$

**(B) Column-wise (each column is a partial-derivative vector):** The $j$-th column of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ describes how the *entire* output vector $\mathbf{f}$ changes when the *single* input $x_j$ varies:

$$\text{Col}_j\bigl(\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\bigr) \;=\; \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial x_j} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}.$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Test:** Given $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{3} \to \mathbb{R}^{2}$, the Jacobian is $2 \times 3$. Row 1 = $\nabla f_1^{\top}$, Column 2 = $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial y$. If you confuse these, expect 1–2 mark deductions in Part A.

### 2.2 Why a Matrix and Not a Vector? — The Linearisation Argument

The "gradient" of any differentiable function must, by definition, encode the **best linear approximation** of that function at a point. The linearisation of $\mathbf{f}$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$ is

$$\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \;\approx\; \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0) + \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}_0)\, \Delta \mathbf{x}.$$

Since the linear map $\Delta \mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}_0) \Delta \mathbf{x}$ must take an $n$-vector to an $m$-vector, it **must be** an $m \times n$ matrix. The Jacobian is precisely that matrix.

### 2.3 The Chain Rule (Most Important Theorem in ML)

If $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$, and $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$ (so $\mathbf{h} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$), then the Jacobians multiply:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}.$$

In index notation:

$$\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_k} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial g_j}\, \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k}.$$

This is the **backbone of the back-propagation algorithm** in deep learning.

### 2.4 Relationship with the Gradient (Scalar Case)

When $m = 1$, denoting $L = f_1$ (e.g., a loss function), the Jacobian $1 \times n$ matrix is

$$\mathbf{J}_{L}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \nabla L(\mathbf{x})^{\top}.$$

Gradient descent update therefore reads

$$\mathbf{x}^{(t+1)} \;=\; \mathbf{x}^{(t)} - \eta\, \mathbf{J}_{L}\!\bigl(\mathbf{x}^{(t)}\bigr)^{\top},$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (bolded as it is a standard ML hyperparameter).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Dimensions |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | Scalar gradient | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \bigl[\partial f/\partial x_j\bigr]^{\top}$ | $n \times 1$ |
| 2 | Jacobian definition | $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) = \bigl[\partial f_i/\partial x_j\bigr]$ | $m \times n$ |
| 3 | Row = gradient of component | $\text{Row}_i \mathbf{J} = \nabla f_i^{\top}$ | $1 \times n$ |
| 4 | Column = partial wrt one input | $\text{Col}_j \mathbf{J} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial x_j$ | $m \times 1$ |
| 5 | Linearisation | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0) + \mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{x}$ | $m$-vector |
| 6 | Directional derivative | $D_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{v}$ | $m \times 1$ |
| 7 | Chain rule | $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}} = \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})) \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})$ | $(m \times n)(n \times p)$ |
| 8 | Gradient descent | $\mathbf{x}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla L(\mathbf{x}^{(t)})$ | $n \times 1$ |
| 9 | Linear map Jacobian | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \Rightarrow \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{A}$ | $m \times n$ |
| 10 | Element-wise $\sigma$ Jacobian | $\dfrac{\partial (\sigma(\mathbf{z}))}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \text{diag}\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr)$ | $n \times n$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Training neural networks:** Back-propagation is *literally* repeated application of the Jacobian chain rule, with a final scalar loss giving $\nabla L$.
- **Robotics / control theory:** The Jacobian maps joint-space velocities to end-effector Cartesian velocities — foundational for manipulator kinematics.
- **Computer graphics / vision:** Image-jacobians underpin optical flow, image registration, and differentiable rendering.
- **Probability & information theory:** The Jacobian determinant $\vert \det \mathbf{J} \vert$ is the change-of-variables term in probability densities — used in **normalizing flows** and **VAEs**.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** Jacobians quantify how sensitive each output is to each input — critical in control and optimisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus highlight (KTU PEERT525 Module 3):** This topic is the bridge from calculus to **automatic differentiation / gradient-based learning**. A student who masters the chain rule for Jacobians has essentially mastered the mathematical foundation of all of deep learning.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Jacobian from First Principles

We motivate the Jacobian by demanding the best **linear approximation** of $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ at $\mathbf{x}_0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the residual.**
Define the error between $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta \mathbf{x})$ and a candidate linear map $\mathbf{A}\Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}$:

$$\mathbf{r}(\Delta \mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta \mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0) - \mathbf{A}\, \Delta \mathbf{x}.$$

**Step 2 — Demand the error to be little-o of the input.**
For differentiability, we require

$$\lim_{\lVert \Delta \mathbf{x} \rVert \to 0} \dfrac{\lVert \mathbf{r}(\Delta \mathbf{x}) \rVert}{\lVert \Delta \mathbf{x} \rVert} \;=\; 0.$$

**Step 3 — Restrict to single-coordinate perturbations.**
Take $\Delta \mathbf{x} = h \mathbf{e}_j$ (perturb only the $j$-th coordinate by an amount $h$). The $i$-th component of the residual is

$$r_i(h\mathbf{e}_j) \;=\; f_i(\mathbf{x}_0 + h\mathbf{e}_j) - f_i(\mathbf{x}_0) - a_{ij} h.$$

**Step 4 — Apply the single-variable derivative definition.**
The little-o condition forces, in the limit $h \to 0$,

$$a_{ij} \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f_i(\mathbf{x}_0 + h\mathbf{e}_j) - f_i(\mathbf{x}_0)}{h} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}(\mathbf{x}_0).$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the matrix.**
Collecting every $a_{ij}$ into an $m \times n$ matrix yields, by definition, the Jacobian

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}_0) \;=\; \bigl[\,a_{ij}\,\bigr]_{i=1..m,\; j=1..n} \;=\; \left[\,\dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}\,\right]. \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — A Scalar-to-Vector Function

Let $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}^{3}$ with $\mathbf{f}(x, y) = \bigl( x^2 y,\; \sin(x) + \cos(y),\; e^{xy} \bigr)$. Compute the Jacobian at the point $(1, \pi)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the components.**
$f_1 = x^2 y$, $\;f_2 = \sin x + \cos y$, $\;f_3 = e^{xy}$.

**Step 2 — Compute each partial derivative symbolically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} &= 2xy, \quad & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} &= x^2, \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} &= \cos x, \quad & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} &= -\sin y, \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} &= y\,e^{xy}, \quad & \dfrac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} &= x\,e^{xy}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the symbolic Jacobian matrix.**

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(x, y) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2xy & x^2 \\[4pt] \cos x & -\sin y \\[4pt] y\,e^{xy} & x\,e^{xy} \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $(1, \pi)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2xy \big|_{(1,\pi)} &= 2\pi, & x^2 \big|_{(1,\pi)} &= 1, \\
\cos x \big|_{(1,\pi)} &= \cos 1, & -\sin y \big|_{(1,\pi)} &= -\sin \pi = 0, \\
y\,e^{xy}\big|_{(1,\pi)} &= \pi e^{\pi}, & x\,e^{xy}\big|_{(1,\pi)} &= e^{\pi}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical Jacobian.**

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(1, \pi) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2\pi & 1 \\[4pt] \cos 1 & 0 \\[4pt] \pi e^{\pi} & e^{\pi} \end{bmatrix} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}.$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Jacobian Chain Rule

**Statement:** If $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ are both differentiable at $\mathbf{x}_0$ and $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_0)$ respectively, then $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$ is differentiable at $\mathbf{x}_0$ and

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}_0) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_0)\bigr)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x}_0).$$

**Proof — Component-wise argument:**

**Step 1 — Write the $i$-th component of $\mathbf{h}$.**

$$h_i(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; f_i\!\bigl(g_1(\mathbf{x}), g_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, g_n(\mathbf{x})\bigr).$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multivariable chain rule (scalar version) to $f_i$.**

$$\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_k} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_j}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr) \cdot \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k}(\mathbf{x}).$$

**Step 3 — Identify the two factors as matrix entries.**

By the definition of the Jacobian:

$$\dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_j}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr) \;=\; \Bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\Bigr]_{ij}, \qquad \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \Bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})\Bigr]_{jk}.$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the matrix product.**
The right-hand side of Step 2 is exactly the $(i, k)$-th entry of the matrix product $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})$:

$$\bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr]_{ik} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\bigr]_{ij}\, \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr]_{jk}.$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.** Therefore

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x}). \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Applying the Chain Rule

Let $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}^{2}$, $\mathbf{g}(u, v) = (u^2 + v,\; uv)$, and $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}^{2}$, $\mathbf{f}(x, y) = (x + y^2,\; e^{x-y})$. Compute $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}}$ at $(1, 1)$ **two ways** — by direct composition and via the chain rule — to verify equivalence.

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}$.**
Components: $g_1 = u^2 + v$, $g_2 = uv$.

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(u, v) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2u & 1 \\ v & u \end{bmatrix}.$$

At $(1, 1)$:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{g}(1, 1)$.**

$$\mathbf{g}(1, 1) \;=\; (1^2 + 1,\; 1 \cdot 1) \;=\; (2, 1).$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ at $(2, 1)$.**
Components: $f_1 = x + y^2$, $f_2 = e^{x-y}$.

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(x, y) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2y \\ e^{x-y} & -e^{x-y} \end{bmatrix}.$$

At $(2, 1)$:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(2, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ e^{1} & -e^{1} \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 4 — Multiply via the chain rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(2, 1)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ e & -e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each entry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1):&\;\; 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 = 4, \\
(1,2):&\;\; 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3, \\
(2,1):&\;\; e \cdot 2 + (-e) \cdot 1 = e, \\
(2,2):&\;\; e \cdot 1 + (-e) \cdot 1 = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore\;\;\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ e & 0 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 5 — Verify by direct composition.**
$\mathbf{h}(u, v) = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{g}(u, v)) = \mathbf{f}(u^2 + v,\; uv) = \bigl( (u^2+v) + (uv)^2,\; e^{(u^2+v) - uv} \bigr)$.

Therefore $h_1 = u^2 + v + u^2 v^2$ and $h_2 = e^{u^2 + v - uv}$.

Compute partials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial u} &= 2u + 2u v^2, & \dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial v} &= 1 + 2u^2 v, \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial u} &= (2u - v)\, e^{u^2 + v - uv}, & \dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial v} &= (1 - u)\, e^{u^2 + v - uv}.
\end{aligned}
$$

At $(u, v) = (1, 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial u}\bigg|_{(1,1)} &= 2 + 2 = 4, & \dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial v}\bigg|_{(1,1)} &= 1 + 2 = 3, \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial u}\bigg|_{(1,1)} &= (2 - 1)\, e^{1} = e, & \dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial v}\bigg|_{(1,1)} &= (1 - 1)\, e^{1} = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ e & 0 \end{bmatrix}. \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods give the **identical** Jacobian, confirming the chain rule.

### 3.5 Application: Back-Propagation via Jacobians

Consider a 1-hidden-layer regression network:

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}, \qquad \mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}), \qquad \hat{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + b^{(2)},$$

with scalar loss $L = \tfrac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$, where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation applied element-wise.

**Step 1 — Loss to output.**

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \;=\; \hat{y} - y \;\;(\text{scalar}).$$

**Step 2 — Output to $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$.**
Since $\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + b^{(2)}$, we have

$$\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} \;=\; \mathbf{h}^{\top}.$$

Hence (treating the gradient as a row for clean presentation)

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\, \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{h}^{\top}.$$

**Step 3 — Output to hidden activation $\mathbf{h}$.**

$$\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial \mathbf{h}} \;=\; \mathbf{W}^{(2)}, \qquad \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}.$$

**Step 4 — Hidden activation to pre-activation $\mathbf{z}$.**
The element-wise sigmoid yields a **diagonal Jacobian**:

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{z}} \;=\; \text{diag}\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr).$$

So

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \, \text{diag}\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr).$$

**Step 5 — Pre-activation to $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$.**

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}}\, \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{z}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \;=\; \left[(\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \, \text{diag}\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr)\right] \mathbf{x}^{\top}.$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The four-step computation above is exactly the back-propagation algorithm**, derived systematically by repeated application of the Jacobian chain rule. This is why mastery of the chain rule is *non-negotiable* in ML.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Conceptual Map: Scalar Gradient → Jacobian → Hessian

This diagram shows the hierarchy of derivative objects as the codomain and the order of differentiation increase.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Scalar function f : R to R"]:::base --> B["Vector function f : R to R^m"]
    B --> C["Vector function f : R^n to R^m"]
    A --> D["Gradient grad f : R^n to R^n"]
    C --> E["Jacobian J_f : R^n to R^{m x n}"]
    E --> F["Hessian H_f : R^n to R^{n x n}"]
    D --> F
    E --> G["Backprop Chain Rule J_{f o g} = J_f J_g"]
    G --> H["ML Training Pipeline"]

    classDef base fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Moving rightward, the *codomain* grows from a single number to a vector, forcing the derivative object to grow from a scalar, to a vector (gradient), to a matrix (Jacobian), to a second-order tensor (Hessian). All four objects are unified by the same linearisation principle.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Functional Flow of Back-Propagation

A block diagram of how Jacobians are *chained* during the backward pass of a 2-layer neural network.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input vector x"]:::input --> L1["Linear W1 x + b1"]
    L1 --> Z["Pre-activation z"]
    Z --> S["Sigmoid sigma z"]
    S --> H["Hidden h"]
    H --> L2["Linear W2 h + b2"]
    L2 --> Y["Output y_hat"]
    Y --> LOSS["Loss L = 0.5 y_hat - y squared"]
    LOSS --> GY["dL_dyhat = y_hat - y"]
    GY --> GW2["dL_dW2 = dL_dyhat times h transpose"]
    GW2 --> GH["dL_dh = dL_dyhat times W2"]
    GH --> GZ["dL_dz = dL_dh elementwise sigma prime of z"]
    GZ --> GW1["dL_dW1 = dL_dz times x transpose"]
    LOSS -.jacobian chain.-> GW2
    GW2 -.jacobian chain.-> GH
    GH -.jacobian chain.-> GZ
    GZ -.jacobian chain.-> GW1

    classDef input fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **forward pass** (top row, left → right) builds the computation graph. The **backward pass** (bottom, right → left) propagates the scalar gradient by multiplying successive Jacobians — exactly the chain rule applied at the matrix level.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Row/Column Anatomy of the Jacobian

A block diagram visualising the two structural interpretations of the $m \times n$ Jacobian for $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J["Jacobian J_f of size m by n"]:::head --> R["ROW VIEW: Row i equals grad f_i transpose"]
    J --> C["COLUMN VIEW: Column j equals partial f by partial x_j"]
    R --> R1["Row 1: gradient of f1"]
    R --> R2["Row 2: gradient of f2"]
    R --> R3["Row m: gradient of fm"]
    C --> C1["Col 1: sensitivity to x1"]
    C --> C2["Col 2: sensitivity to x2"]
    C --> C3["Col n: sensitivity to xn"]
    R1 --> USES["Used in: directional derivative, optimisation"]
    R2 --> USES
    R3 --> USES
    C1 --> USES
    C2 --> USES
    C3 --> USES

    classDef head fill:#E2F0D9,stroke:#548235,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each **row** is the gradient of one scalar output component; each **column** is the partial-derivative vector with respect to one scalar input. Both views are mathematically equivalent and useful in different contexts.
