## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
Define the **Jacobian matrix** of a vector-valued function $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$. Show that it is the natural matrix-form generalisation of the gradient of a scalar function.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobian matrix of $\mathbf{f} = (f_1, \ldots, f_m)$ at $\mathbf{x}$ is the $m \times n$ matrix of all first-order partial derivatives:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \left[\,\dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}\,\right]_{i=1..m,\; j=1..n}.$$

When $m = 1$, this reduces to a $1 \times n$ row matrix whose only row is $\bigl[\partial f/\partial x_1, \ldots, \partial f/\partial x_n\bigr]$, which is the transpose of the gradient vector. Hence the Jacobian generalises the gradient to vector-valued outputs. **(3 marks)** — *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: dimensions; 1 mark: reduction to gradient for $m=1$.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
State the **chain rule for Jacobians**. Explain in one sentence its significance in training deep neural networks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

If $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ are differentiable, then for $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$,

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}.$$

**Significance:** Back-propagation in deep neural networks is *exactly* the repeated matrix-product application of this chain rule — composing Jacobians of successive layers to compute the gradient of the scalar loss with respect to every weight. **(3 marks)** — *(1 mark: statement; 1 mark: matrix dimensions; 1 mark: ML significance.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Chosen by student as Q11a or Q11b alternative)*

**(a)** Compute the Jacobian of the function
$$\mathbf{f}(x, y, z) \;=\; \bigl( x^2 + yz,\; xyz,\; e^{x+y} \sin z \bigr)$$
at the point $(1, 1, 0)$. Also state the dimension of the Jacobian and identify its rows and columns. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Let $\mathbf{g}(u, v) = (u^2 - v^2,\; 2uv)$ and $\mathbf{f}(x, y) = (x^2 - y^2,\; 2xy)$. Compute the Jacobian of $\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$ at $(1, 1)$ using the **chain rule**. Verify one entry of the resulting matrix by direct composition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Identify components and dimension.**
$f_1 = x^2 + yz,\; f_2 = xyz,\; f_3 = e^{x+y}\sin z$. Since $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{3} \to \mathbb{R}^{3}$, the Jacobian is a **$3 \times 3$** matrix.

**Step 2 — Compute all nine partial derivatives.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\partial f_1/\partial x &= 2x, \quad & \partial f_1/\partial y &= z, \quad & \partial f_1/\partial z &= y, \\
\partial f_2/\partial x &= yz, \quad & \partial f_2/\partial y &= xz, \quad & \partial f_2/\partial z &= xy, \\
\partial f_3/\partial x &= e^{x+y}\sin z, \quad & \partial f_3/\partial y &= e^{x+y}\sin z, \quad & \partial f_3/\partial z &= e^{x+y}\cos z.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbolic Jacobian.**

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(x, y, z) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2x & z & y \\ yz & xz & xy \\ e^{x+y}\sin z & e^{x+y}\sin z & e^{x+y}\cos z \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $(1, 1, 0)$.**
Note: $e^{1+0} = e$, $\sin 0 = 0$, $\cos 0 = 1$.

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(1, 1, 0) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & e \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 5 — Identify rows and columns.**
- **Row 1** $= \nabla f_1^{\top}$ (gradient of $x^2 + yz$ at the point),
- **Row 2** $= \nabla f_2^{\top}$,
- **Row 3** $= \nabla f_3^{\top}$.
- **Column 1** $= \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial x$,
- **Column 2** $= \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial y$,
- **Column 3** $= \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial z$.

*(Mark split: 1 mark dimension, 3 marks partial derivatives, 2 marks substitution, 1 mark row/column interpretation = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}$.**
$g_1 = u^2 - v^2$, $\;g_2 = 2uv$.

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(u, v) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2u & -2v \\ 2v & 2u \end{bmatrix}.$$

At $(1, 1)$:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $\mathbf{g}(1, 1)$.**

$$\mathbf{g}(1, 1) \;=\; (1 - 1,\; 2) \;=\; (0, 2).$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ at $(0, 2)$.**
$f_1 = x^2 - y^2$, $\;f_2 = 2xy$.

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(x, y) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2x & -2y \\ 2y & 2x \end{bmatrix}.$$

At $(0, 2)$:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(0, 2) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -4 \\ 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 4 — Multiply (chain rule).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(0, 2)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -4 \\ 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Entry-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1):&\; 0\cdot 2 + (-4)\cdot 2 = -8, \\
(1,2):&\; 0\cdot(-2) + (-4)\cdot 2 = -8, \\
(2,1):&\; 4\cdot 2 + 0\cdot 2 = 8, \\
(2,2):&\; 4\cdot(-2) + 0\cdot 2 = -8.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}}(1, 1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -8 & -8 \\ 8 & -8 \end{bmatrix}.$$

**Step 5 — Verify the $(1, 1)$-entry by direct composition.**
$\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$ has components
$h_1 = g_1^2 - g_2^2$ and $h_2 = 2 g_1 g_2$.
Therefore

$$\dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial u} \;=\; 2 g_1\, \dfrac{\partial g_1}{\partial u} - 2 g_2\, \dfrac{\partial g_2}{\partial u} \;=\; 2 g_1 \cdot 2u - 2 g_2 \cdot 2v.$$

At $(1, 1)$: $g_1 = 0$, $g_2 = 2$, $u = 1$, $v = 1$, so

$$\dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial u}\bigg|_{(1,1)} \;=\; 2(0)(2) - 2(2)(2) \;=\; -8. \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the $(1, 1)$-entry of the chain-rule result. *(Mark split: 1 mark each for the two Jacobians, 2 marks matrix product, 2 marks verification = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Derive the **Jacobian chain rule** from first principles: if $\mathbf{g} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ are differentiable, prove that the Jacobian of $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}$ is the product of the two Jacobians. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple 1-hidden-layer regression network is defined by
$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}, \quad \mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}), \quad \hat{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + b^{(2)},$$
with scalar loss $L = \tfrac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$ and element-wise sigmoid $\sigma$. Using the **Jacobian chain rule**, derive an explicit expression for the gradient $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Write the $i$-th scalar component of $\mathbf{h}$.**

$$h_i(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; f_i\bigl(g_1(\mathbf{x}), g_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, g_n(\mathbf{x})\bigr).$$

**Step 2 — Apply the scalar multivariable chain rule.**
Since $f_i$ is a function of the intermediate vector $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}) = (g_1, \ldots, g_n)$,

$$\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_k} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_j}\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr) \cdot \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k}(\mathbf{x}).$$

**Step 3 — Identify the partials as matrix entries.**

$$\dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_j}\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr) \;=\; \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\bigr]_{ij}, \qquad \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr]_{jk}.$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the matrix product.**

The $(i, k)$-entry of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})$ is

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\bigr]_{ij}\, \bigl[\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr]_{jk} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_j} \cdot \dfrac{\partial g_j}{\partial x_k} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial x_k}.$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\!\bigl(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x}).\;} \qquad \blacksquare$$

Dimensions: $(m \times n)(n \times p) = m \times p$, which matches $\mathbf{h} : \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$. *(Mark split: 1 mark writing $h_i$, 2 marks scalar chain rule, 2 marks matrix identification, 1 mark conclusion, 1 mark dimensions = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Loss to output.**

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \;=\; \hat{y} - y \;\;(\text{scalar}).$$

**Step 2 — Output to $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$.**
$\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + b^{(2)}$, so $\partial \hat{y}/\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \mathbf{h}^{\top}$. Hence

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{h}^{\top}.$$

**Step 3 — Output to $\mathbf{h}$.**
$\partial \hat{y}/\partial \mathbf{h} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}$, so

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}.$$

**Step 4 — Hidden $\mathbf{h}$ to pre-activation $\mathbf{z}$.**
Since $\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{z})$ element-wise, the Jacobian is diagonal: $\partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{z} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{z}))$. Therefore

$$\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} \;=\; (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\, \text{diag}\!\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr).$$

**Step 5 — Pre-activation to $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$.**
$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$, so $\partial \mathbf{z}/\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \mathbf{x}^{\top}$. Finally,

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \;=\; \left[(\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\, \text{diag}\!\bigl(\sigma'(\mathbf{z})\bigr)\right] \mathbf{x}^{\top}.\;}$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for Steps 1–4, 2 marks for the final assembly in Step 5 = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the order of multiplication in the chain rule.** The matrix product is $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}} \cdot \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}$, **not** $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}} \cdot \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$. Order matters because matrix multiplication is non-commutative. *(Typical 2-mark loss in Part B.)*
> 2. **Using $\nabla \mathbf{f}$ to mean a vector.** When $m > 1$, there is no single "gradient vector." Always write the Jacobian as a matrix. Examiners dock 1 mark for this loose notation.
> 3. **Confusing rows and columns.** A common slip: writing $\partial f_i / \partial x_j$ in the wrong cell. Memorise: *row index = output index $i$, column index = input index $j$.*
> 4. **Skipping substitution / evaluation step.** Even if the symbolic Jacobian is correct, marks are awarded only after the *specific point* is substituted. Always finish the numerical evaluation.
> 5. **In back-prop derivations, forgetting the diagonal Jacobian** for element-wise activations. This single omission can cost the entire 7 marks of the sub-part.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The "gradient" of a vector-valued function $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, **not** a single vector.
- **Jacobian entry convention:** $(i, j)$-entry is $\partial f_i / \partial x_j$ — *output index first, input index second*.
- **Row view:** Row $i$ of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ equals $\nabla f_i^{\top}$.
- **Column view:** Column $j$ of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}$ equals $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial x_j$.
- **Linearisation:** $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_0) + \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{x}_0)\, \Delta \mathbf{x}$.
- **Directional derivative:** $D_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}\, \mathbf{v}$.
- **Chain rule (most important):** $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f} \circ \mathbf{g}} = \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{f}}(\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}))\, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{g}}(\mathbf{x})$ — dimensions $(m \times n)(n \times p) = m \times p$.
- **Scalar special case ($m = 1$):** $\mathbf{J}_L = \nabla L^{\top}$; gradient descent becomes $\mathbf{x}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(t)} - \eta\, \mathbf{J}_L^{\top}$.
- **Linear map:** $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \Rightarrow \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{A}$ (constant).
- **Element-wise activations:** Jacobian is diagonal: $\partial \sigma(\mathbf{z})/\partial \mathbf{z} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{z}))$.
- **ML link:** Back-propagation is the chain rule applied layer by layer, with a final scalar loss.
- **Robotics link:** The manipulator Jacobian maps joint velocities to end-effector Cartesian velocities.
- **Probability link:** $\vert \det \mathbf{J} \vert$ is the change-of-variables term for probability densities under a transformation.
- **Examination mantra:** *Compute partials → assemble matrix → evaluate at the point → (if required) chain-multiply.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradients of Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradients of Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Gradient (Numerator/Denominator Layout)**
> Let $f : \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued (scalar) function of a matrix variable $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. The **gradient of $f$ with respect to $\mathbf{X}$** is the matrix of partial derivatives:
> $$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f \;\triangleq\; \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial X_{ij}}\right]_{m \times n}$$
> where $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_{ij}}$ denotes the ordinary partial derivative of $f$ with respect to the $(i,j)$-th entry of $\mathbf{X}$.

In the more general case where $\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{p \times q}$ is matrix-valued, the derivative is a **fourth-order tensor** $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{F}}{\partial \mathbf{X}}$, but for the KTU scope we focus almost exclusively on the scalar case, which is the workhorse of every loss-function optimization in ML.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slope Map" of a Mountain Range

Imagine you are standing on a vast **mountain range** shaped like an $m \times n$ grid. Each grid cell $(i, j)$ has a height given by the scalar $f(\mathbf{X})$ when you plug in $X_{ij}$ as the local "altitude dial." A regular gradient (in 1-D or 2-D) tells you the *single steepest direction* of ascent.

A **matrix gradient** is different — it is a *whole map* of steepest ascents, one slope for every entry of $\mathbf{X}$. Think of it as giving every cell of the grid its own little arrow. If the arrow points "up" (positive derivative), raising that entry increases $f$; if it points "down", raising that entry decreases $f$. Optimization then becomes: *walk on the map in the direction all arrows are pointing* — this is exactly what **gradient descent** does in linear regression, neural-net backprop, and PCA.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Machine Learning

| ML Setting | Scalar Loss $f$ | Matrix Variable $\mathbf{X}$ | Why the Gradient Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | MSE loss | Weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ | Closed-form normal equation $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} = 0$ |
| Logistic Regression | Log-loss | Weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ | Used in gradient-descent updates |
| Neural Networks | Cross-entropy | Weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}$ | Core of the backpropagation algorithm |
| Gaussian Models | NLL | Covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | Drives Fisher information, MLE updates |
| Low-Rank Models | Reconstruction error | Factor $\mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V}$ | Used in matrix factorization, PCA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The ability to *differentiate traces, determinants, norms, and quadratic forms* w.r.t. a matrix is the single most-tested skill in Module 3. Master the identities in Section 2 and you master 80% of the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Layout Conventions (Must Be Understood First)

There are two competing traditions in matrix calculus. KTU follows the **denominator layout** (also called the *Hessian* or *gradient* layout), where $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f$ has the *same shape* as $\mathbf{X}$:

$$\left(\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f\right)_{ij} \;=\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_{ij}}$$

In the *numerator layout* (Jacobian), you would write the transpose. We will use **denominator layout** throughout, which is the standard in machine-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop, Murphy) and matches the matrix cookbook of Petersen & Pedersen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Sanity Check Rule:** If $f$ is scalar, $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f$ has the **same shape as $\mathbf{X}$**. Period. This single rule eliminates 90% of student errors in valuation.

### 2.2 Operational Concept — How a Matrix Gradient Is Computed

The procedure has three disciplined steps:

1. **Rewrite using trace identities.** A scalar expression is rewritten in the equivalent form $\text{tr}(\dots)$ because $\text{tr}(\cdot)$ plays nicely with differentials.
2. **Take the differential $df$.** Use the rules: $d(\text{tr}(\mathbf{A})) = \text{tr}(d\mathbf{A})$, $d(\mathbf{AB}) = (d\mathbf{A})\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{A}(d\mathbf{B})$, $d(\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = -\mathbf{A}^{-1}(d\mathbf{A})\mathbf{A}^{-1}$.
3. **Identify** the matrix multiplying $d\mathbf{X}$. That matrix is $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *core identities* the KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce. **Memorize them by category, not by rote.**

| # | Scalar Function $f(\mathbf{X})$ | $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f$ (denominator layout) | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ constant |
| 2 | $\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^{\top}$ | — |
| 3 | $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{a}$ | $\mathbf{a}$ | — |
| 4 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$ | $\mathbf{A}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{A}$ constant |
| 5 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}^{\top})$ | $\mathbf{A}$ | — |
| 6 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B})$ | $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}$ constant |
| 7 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B})$ | $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}$ | — |
| 8 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$ | $(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{X}$ | — |
| 9 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}) = \Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2}$ | $2\mathbf{X}$ | — |
| 10 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top})$ | $2\mathbf{X}^{\top}$ | — |
| 11 | $\log \det(\mathbf{X})$ | $(\mathbf{X}^{-1})^{\top} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top})^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ square, non-singular |
| 12 | $\det(\mathbf{X})$ | $\det(\mathbf{X})\,(\mathbf{X}^{-1})^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{X}$ square, non-singular |
| 13 | $\mathbf{X}^{-1}$ (matrix-by-matrix) | $-\mathbf{X}^{-1}\,\mathbf{E}_{ij}\,\mathbf{X}^{-1}$ | per-entry formula |
| 14 | $\Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2}$ | $2\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B})$ | least-squares gradient |
| 15 | $\text{tr}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{B})$ | $\mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}^{\top} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}$ | cyclic property applied |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These identities are the **backbone of every ML derivation**:

- **Identity #8** underpins the derivation of the **normal equation** in linear regression and the **update rule in Ridge regression**.
- **Identity #11** is critical in **Gaussian MLE**: the gradient of $\log\det\mathbf{\Sigma}$ appears when fitting covariance matrices.
- **Identity #9** is the Frobenius norm gradient used in **matrix completion, recommender systems (SVD), and PCA reconstruction loss**.
- **Identity #14** is the canonical form of the **least-squares loss**, the most-frequently-asked KTU 14-marker.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout:** All $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}$ are *constant* (not functions of $\mathbf{X}$). The variable of differentiation is **always $\mathbf{X}$**.

---

### 3.1 Derivation A — Linear Form $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})$

This is the simplest non-trivial case and the building block for all others.

**Step 1.** Write the scalar explicitly as a double sum:
$$\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i \, X_{ij} \, b_j$$

**Step 2.** Apply ordinary calculus to the scalar summand:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial X_{ij}}\bigl(\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}\bigr) \;=\; a_i \, b_j$$

This is because all other terms in the sum are constants w.r.t. $X_{ij}$.

**Step 3.** Assemble the matrix of partials. The $(i,j)$-th entry of $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})$ is $a_i b_j$, which is exactly the $(i,j)$-th entry of the outer product $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^{\top}$:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) \;=\; \mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^{\top}$$

**Numerical check** with $\mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\2\end{bmatrix}, \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}3\\4\end{bmatrix}$:
$$\mathbf{a}\mathbf{b}^{\top} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{a}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Quadratic Form $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$

This is the **most-asked KTU derivation** and the source of the famous $(A + A^{\top})$ factor.

**Step 1.** Convert to trace form using cyclic property:
$$f \;=\; \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Take the differential using product rule:
$$df \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((d\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\right) \;+\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}(d\mathbf{X})\right)$$

**Step 3.** Reorganize. In the first term, the differential $d\mathbf{X}$ is on the *left* of the transpose; in the second term, on the *right*. Use the identity $\text{tr}(\mathbf{M}^{\top}\mathbf{N}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{N}\mathbf{M}^{\top}) = \sum_{ij} M_{ij} N_{ij}$ to write the first term as:
$$\text{tr}\!\left((d\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Combine the two terms:
$$df \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\bigl[\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\bigr]^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\bigl[(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{X}\bigr]^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Read off the gradient from $df = \text{tr}((\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f)^{\top} d\mathbf{X})$:
$$\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}) \;=\; (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\,\mathbf{X}\;}$$

**Important Corollaries (valuation gold):**
- If $\mathbf{A}$ is **symmetric** ($\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}$), the gradient **simplifies to $2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$**.
- If $\mathbf{A}$ is **skew-symmetric** ($\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{A}^{\top}$), the gradient is **$\mathbf{0}$** (extremum!).
- If $\mathbf{X}$ is a *column vector* ($\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$), this reduces to the famous vector identity $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{x}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Log-Determinant $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \log\det(\mathbf{X})$

This identity is essential for Gaussian likelihoods, KL-divergence, and information geometry.

**Step 1.** Use the spectral decomposition. For a square, non-singular $\mathbf{X}$:
$$\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \log\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \lambda_i$$

**Step 2.** Use the *differential* route (cleaner and coordinate-free). We invoke the standard identity:
$$d\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; \det(\mathbf{X}) \,\text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{-1}\,d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Apply the chain rule $d(\log u) = du/u$:
$$d\log\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{X})} \cdot \det(\mathbf{X}) \,\text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{-1}\,d\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{-1}\,d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Convert to denominator layout using $\text{tr}(\mathbf{M}\,d\mathbf{X}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{M}^{\top} d\mathbf{X})$:
$$d\log\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((\mathbf{X}^{-1})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right) \;\equiv\; \text{tr}\!\left((\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\log\det\mathbf{X})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\log\det(\mathbf{X}) \;=\; (\mathbf{X}^{-1})^{\top} \;=\; (\mathbf{X}^{\top})^{-1}\;}$$

**Crucial symmetry case:** If $\mathbf{X}$ is symmetric, $(\mathbf{X}^{\top})^{-1} = \mathbf{X}^{-1}$, so the gradient is just $\mathbf{X}^{-1}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation D — Frobenius Norm $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2}$

**Step 1.** Expand the squared Frobenius norm:
$$\Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2} \;=\; \sum_{i,j} X_{ij}^{2} \;=\; \text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})$$

**Step 2.** Take the differential:
$$d\Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2} \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((d\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{X}\right) \;+\; \text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; 2\,\text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{X}^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

(The two terms are equal by cyclic property of the trace.)

**Step 3.** Identify the gradient:
$$\boxed{\;\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2} \;=\; 2\mathbf{X}\;}$$

**Engineering tie-in:** Setting this to zero gives the *trivial* solution $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}$. The *interesting* problem is $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2} = 2\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B}) = \mathbf{0}$, which yields the **normal equation** $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation E — The Matrix Chain Rule

**Statement:** If $\mathbf{Y} = g(\mathbf{X})$ and $f = f(\mathbf{Y})$, then for the scalar case:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{l=1}^{q} \frac{\partial f}{\partial Y_{kl}}\,\frac{\partial Y_{kl}}{\partial \mathbf{X}}$$

In trace form, this becomes the elegant identity:
$$df \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f)^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((\nabla_{\mathbf{Y}} f)^{\top} d\mathbf{Y}\right) \;=\; \text{tr}\!\left((\nabla_{\mathbf{Y}} f)^{\top}\,\frac{\partial \mathbf{Y}}{\partial \mathbf{X}}\,d\mathbf{X}\right)$$

**Worked example:** Let $f(\mathbf{Y}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{Y}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y})$ with $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{C}$.
- From (B) and the product rule: $\nabla_{\mathbf{Y}} f = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{Y}$.
- The chain rule gives:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f \;=\; \mathbf{B}^{\top}(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\,\mathbf{Y}\,\mathbf{C}^{\top} \;=\; \mathbf{B}^{\top}(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\,\mathbf{B}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{C}^{\top}$$

This is the gradient used in **convolutional layer backpropagation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Taxonomy of Matrix Gradients

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *complete decision path* a student takes when computing $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f$, exactly as the KTU examiner expects:

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start: Scalar f of Matrix X] --> classA{What is the shape of f?}
    classA -->|Scalar output| pathB[Most common case]
    classA -->|Matrix output| pathC[Fourth-order tensor - rare in KTU]
    pathB --> step1[Step 1: Rewrite f using trace and cyclic identities]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Take differential df]
    step2 --> step3{Does f involve inverse or determinant?}
    step3 -->|Yes, inverse| branchInv[Use dXinv = -Xinv dX Xinv]
    step3 -->|Yes, determinant| branchDet[Use d det X = det X tr Xinv dX]
    step3 -->|No| branchSimple[Standard product rule on differentials]
    branchInv --> step4
    branchDet --> step4
    branchSimple --> step4[Step 3: Identify matrix multiplying dX in df]
    step4 --> step5[Step 4: That matrix is the gradient in denominator layout]
    step5 --> step6[Step 5: Verify shape matches X]
    step6 --> done[Final Answer grad X f]
    pathC --> noteOut[Out of KTU Module 3 scope]
```

### 4.2 Relationship Map — Identity Clusters

This second diagram shows how the master identities cluster into the *four function families* you must know:

```mermaid
graph LR
    familyA[LINEAR FAMILY] --> id1[aT X b yields a bT]
    familyA --> id2[tr AX yields AT]
    familyA --> id3[tr AXB yields AT BT]
    familyB[QUADRATIC FAMILY] --> id4[XT A X yields A plus AT times X]
    familyB --> id5[Frobenius norm squared yields 2X]
    familyB --> id6[Least squares yields 2 AT times AX minus B]
    familyC[DET FAMILY] --> id7[log det X yields Xinv transposed]
    familyC --> id8[det X yields det X times Xinv transposed]
    familyD[INVERSE FAMILY] --> id9[Scalar inverse 1 over xT Ax yields minus 2 A x over xT Ax squared]
    familyD --> id10[Matrix inverse per entry yields minus Xinv Eij Xinv]
    familyA --> coreRule[Master Trick: Use trace and cyclic property]
    familyB --> coreRule
    familyC --> coreRule
    familyD --> coreRule
    coreRule --> exam[KTU 14-mark ready]
```

### 4.3 Computational Pipeline Block Diagram

This diagram is the *step-by-step recipe* the KTU board examiners expect in any derivation answer:

```mermaid
graph TD
    blkA[Block A: Recognize scalar f of matrix X] --> blkB[Block B: Express f as trace of product]
    blkB --> blkC[Block C: Apply d to all factors using product rule]
    blkC --> blkD[Block D: Collect all terms containing dX]
    blkD --> blkE[Block E: Use cyclic property to bring dX to the right end]
    blkE --> blkF[Block F: Factor out dX as tr of G transposed dX]
    blkF --> blkG[Block G: The matrix G is the gradient grad X f]
    blkG --> blkH[Block H: Verify shape of G equals shape of X]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A full-marks derivation in KTU 2024 follows Blocks A through H. Skipping any block (especially the "verify shape" step) costs at least **1 mark**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State the gradient of the scalar function $f(\mathbf{X}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B})$ with respect to $\mathbf{X}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are constant matrices of compatible dimensions. Justify your answer using the trace property.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
By the cyclic property of the trace, $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$. Treating $f$ as a linear function of $\mathbf{X}$ (entry-by-entry), we have $f = \sum_{i,j,k} A_{ik} X_{kj} B_{ji}$. The partial derivative is:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial X_{ab}} \;=\; \sum_{i,k} A_{ik}\,\delta_{kj=a}\,B_{ji}\big\vert_{j=a} \;=\; \sum_{i} A_{ib}\,B_{ai} \;=\; (\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A})_{ab} \;=\; (\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top})_{ab}$$
Hence, $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\, \text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}) = \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
For a symmetric positive definite matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, write the gradient of $f(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = \log\det(\mathbf{\Sigma})$ with respect to $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. Mention one real-world ML scenario where this gradient is used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a symmetric matrix, $(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1})^{\top} = \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}$, therefore:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}} \log\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}) \;=\; \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}$$
This gradient appears in the **MLE of a multivariate Gaussian**, where the log-likelihood contains the term $-\tfrac{n}{2}\log\det\mathbf{\Sigma}$ and is differentiated w.r.t. the covariance matrix. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive, with full steps, the gradient of the quadratic form $f(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$ with respect to $\mathbf{X}$. Clearly state the conditions under which this gradient reduces to $2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Hence, find the matrix $\mathbf{X}$ that minimizes $f(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - 2\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric positive definite and $\mathbf{b}$ is a constant vector. State the name of the result. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert to trace form:
$$f(\mathbf{X}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Differential using product rule:
$$df = \text{tr}((d\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}) + \text{tr}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\,d\mathbf{X})$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply $\text{tr}(\mathbf{M}^{\top}\mathbf{N}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{N}\mathbf{M}^{\top})$ to first term:
$$df = \text{tr}((\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}) + \text{tr}((\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}) = \text{tr}\bigl(((\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{X})^{\top} d\mathbf{X}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Read off:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})\mathbf{X}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** When $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}$ (symmetric), $\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top} = 2\mathbf{A}$, so $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$.

---

##### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the total gradient. The gradient of $-2\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ is $-2\mathbf{b}$ (a column vector case, equivalent to $\mathbf{b}\mathbf{I}$ with rank-1 form, but here $\mathbf{X}$ is a vector, so $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(-2\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{X}) = -2\mathbf{b}$):
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} f = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - 2\mathbf{b}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Set the gradient to zero for optimality (using symmetry, $\mathbf{A}^{\top} = \mathbf{A}$):
$$2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}^{*} - 2\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}^{*} = \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Since $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric positive definite, it is invertible, hence:
$$\mathbf{X}^{*} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** This is the celebrated **Normal Equation** of linear regression (or, equivalently, the **Ritz variational** form).

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the gradients of the following scalar functions w.r.t. $\mathbf{X}$:
    (i) $f(\mathbf{X}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B})$
    (ii) $f(\mathbf{X}) = \Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2}$
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** A multivariate Gaussian has log-likelihood (ignoring constants):
$$\ell(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = -\tfrac{n}{2}\log\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}) - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S})$$
where $\mathbf{S} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})^{\top}$ is the scatter matrix. Derive the MLE estimate of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ by setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\ell = \mathbf{0}$. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**(i) — 3 marks:**
Using cyclic property: $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$. Differentiating the linear scalar w.r.t. $\mathbf{X}$ gives $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}$. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) — 4 marks:**
Expand: $\Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2} = \text{tr}\bigl((\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B})^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{B})\bigr)$.
Differential:
$$d(\cdot) = \text{tr}\bigl((d\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B})\bigr) + \text{tr}\bigl((\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B})^{\top}\mathbf{A}\,d\mathbf{X}\bigr)$$
$$= 2\,\text{tr}\bigl(((\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}))^{\top}\mathbf{A}\,d\mathbf{X}\bigr)\big/\text{(cyclic; using }\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}\text{ real)}$$
Wait, careful: the first term simplifies to $\text{tr}((\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}))^{\top} d\mathbf{X})$; the second term is $\text{tr}((\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}))^{\top} d\mathbf{X})$ because $(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B})^{\top}\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}))^{\top}$. Thus:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2} = 2\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}) \quad (4 \text{ marks})$$

---

##### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute each gradient:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\bigl(-\tfrac{n}{2}\log\det\mathbf{\Sigma}\bigr) = -\tfrac{n}{2}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1})^{\top} = -\tfrac{n}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}$$
(since $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is symmetric). For the second term, use the identity $\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S}) = -(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1})^{\top}\mathbf{S}^{\top}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1})^{\top}$ (chain rule + inverse derivative):
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S})\bigr) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The full gradient is:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\ell = -\tfrac{n}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Set to zero and multiply from both sides by $2\mathbf{\Sigma}$:
$$-n\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{S}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} = \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{S}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} = n\mathbf{I}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Hence:
$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_{\text{MLE}} = \tfrac{1}{n}\mathbf{S} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{x}_i-\bar{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x}_i-\bar{\mathbf{x}})^{\top}}$$
This is the **sample covariance matrix** (up to the $1/n$ factor).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks on "Gradients of Matrices" problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetric reduction** — when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric, you MUST say $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$ and *state* the symmetry assumption. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Wrong transpose in trace identity** — students often write $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{A}$ instead of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}$. The mnemonic is "$\mathbf{A}$ is *outside* $\mathbf{X}$, so it transposes." **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Skipping the shape-check** — KTU examiners explicitly look for "since $\mathbf{X}$ is $m \times n$, the gradient must also be $m \times n$." Skipping it costs a mark.
> 4. **Cyclic-property abuse** — $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{C}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})$ is *valid*, but you cannot reorder non-conformable matrices or move $\mathbf{X}$ past a non-commuting factor blindly.
> 5. **Log-det identity sign error** — the gradient of $\log\det(\mathbf{X})$ is $\mathbf{X}^{-1}$ (for symmetric $\mathbf{X}$), *not* $\mathbf{X}$. A sign-correct derivation earns 2 marks automatically.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **gradient of a scalar w.r.t. a matrix** is a matrix of the *same shape* (denominator layout).
- ✅ Always **rewrite scalars as traces** before differentiating; this turns "magic" matrix identities into routine scalar calculus.
- ✅ The differential method (Steps 1–5 in Section 3) is the **only rigorous, examinable method** — component-by-component brute force is acceptable but slower.
- ✅ The **$(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{\top})$ factor** in $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$ is the single most-tested formula. Special case: symmetric $\mathbf{A} \Rightarrow 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$.
- ✅ **Trace gradients** (Identity #4 of Section 2): the gradient of $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X})$ is $\mathbf{A}^{\top}$; of $\text{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{B})$ is $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}$.
- ✅ **Log-determinant**: $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\log\det(\mathbf{X}) = (\mathbf{X}^{\top})^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Frobenius norm squared**: $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\Vert\mathbf{X}\Vert_{F}^{2} = 2\mathbf{X}$.
- ✅ **Least-squares gradient**: $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}\Vert\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B}\Vert_{F}^{2} = 2\mathbf{A}^{\top}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{B})$, leading to the **normal equation** $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}$.
- ✅ **Matrix chain rule** is required for backpropagation through linear layers: $\nabla_{\mathbf{X}}f(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}^{\top}\nabla_{\mathbf{Y}}f\cdot\mathbf{C}^{\top}$.
- ✅ Always **state validity conditions** (square, non-singular, symmetric, positive definite) before applying the determinant or inverse identities.
- ✅ Use the **mnemonic "OUTSIDE transposes"** for trace gradients: any matrix *outside* $\mathbf{X}$ in a trace gets transposed.
- ✅ Master the **differential table** in Section 2.3 — it is the fastest way to compute any KTU 14-mark problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Useful Identities for Computing Gradients

### Formal Definition
In the context of deep learning and matrix calculus, **Useful Identities for Computing Gradients** refer to a standardized set of derivative rules expressed in matrix/vector form that allow us to efficiently compute partial derivatives of scalar, vector, and matrix-valued functions with respect to their parameters. These identities generalize single-variable calculus rules to multi-dimensional settings, forming the mathematical backbone of optimization in neural networks.

The most critical identities include:
- The **chain rule** for nested functions: $\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$
- Gradient of a **dot product**: $\frac{\partial (\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{b} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{a}$
- Gradient of an **affine transformation**: $\frac{\partial (\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}^T$
- Gradient of a **quadratic form**: $\frac{\partial (\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^T)\mathbf{x}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Mastering these identities is essential because direct application of partial derivative rules becomes infeasible in deep networks with **millions of parameters**. The identities provide an $O(n)$ instead of $O(n^2)$ route to gradient computation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a **mountain hiker** lost in dense fog at the base of a huge mountain range. You cannot see the peak, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. To reach the top (minimum loss), you need to know which direction is steepest downhill — this direction is exactly the **negative gradient**.

In a deep neural network:
- The **terrain** = the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$
- The **ground slope at each point** = the gradient $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}$
- **Useful identities** = pre-computed "slope maps" for common terrain features (ridges, valleys, plains). Instead of measuring slope from scratch at every step, you refer to a known map.

When you have a chain of mountains (layer after layer of neural network), you cannot directly measure the slope at the far peak. You use the **chain rule** — measure slope from peak to base camp, then from base camp to your position, and multiply them. This is precisely **backpropagation**.

---

## 1.2 Back Propagation

### Formal Definition
**Back Propagation (Backprop)** is a specialized application of the reverse-mode automatic differentiation algorithm, designed to efficiently compute gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight in a neural network. It leverages the **chain rule of calculus** on a directed acyclic computational graph, propagating error signals from the output layer backwards to the input layer.

Mathematically, given a network output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ and loss $\mathcal{L} = L(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$, backprop computes:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

applied recursively through every intermediate layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Backprop is NOT a learning algorithm by itself. It is purely a **gradient computation algorithm**. The actual learning happens in the optimizer (SGD, Adam, etc.) that uses these gradients to update weights.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **factory assembly line** that produces a final product (prediction). If the final product is defective (high loss), how do you figure out which workstation caused the defect?

A naive approach: shut down the whole factory and test each worker individually. (This is what **numerical differentiation** does — perturb each weight, recompute the loss.) It works, but is brutally slow.

A smart approach: each workstation reports how much its output influenced the final defect, and sends this "blame signal" backward. Worker A says "I'm 30% to blame," Worker B says "I'm 50%," and so on. This blame is the **gradient** — how much each weight contributed to the error. This is **backpropagation**.

---

## 1.3 Automatic Differentiation

### Formal Definition
**Automatic Differentiation (Autodiff)** is a set of techniques to numerically evaluate derivatives of computer programs. Unlike symbolic differentiation (which manipulates expressions) or numerical differentiation (which approximates via finite differences), autodiff computes derivatives to **machine precision** by applying the chain rule to a sequence of elementary arithmetic operations.

There are two primary modes:
- **Forward Mode (Forward Accumulation)**: Propagates derivatives from inputs to outputs. Efficient when #inputs $\ll$ #outputs.
- **Reverse Mode (Reverse Accumulation)**: Propagates derivatives from outputs to inputs. Efficient when #outputs $\ll$ #inputs. This is backpropagation when applied to neural networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Perspective**: When asked "what is automatic differentiation?" do not confuse it with symbolic differentiation (which Mathematica does) or numerical differentiation (finite differences). Autodiff is a **hybrid** that uses symbolic rules on elementary operations but evaluates numerically.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **Rube Goldberg machine** — a chain of simple gadgets where a marble rolls through levers, pulleys, and ramps to eventually ring a bell. The output (bell ringing) depends on every gadget's position.

If you want to know how sensitive the bell ring is to a particular lever's angle:
- **Numerical differentiation**: nudge the lever a tiny bit, restart the machine, measure the bell. Slow and noisy.
- **Symbolic differentiation**: derive the entire machine's mathematical equation first. Brutally complex for big machines.
- **Forward-mode autodiff**: place a "shadow marble" alongside the real one, carrying a derivative value at each gadget. One pass forward.
- **Reverse-mode autodiff**: run the machine forward, recording state at each gadget. Then run a "blame tape" backward from the bell to the lever. Two passes, but extremely efficient when there's one bell and many levers (exactly the deep learning case!).

---

## 1.4 Gradients in Deep Networks

### Formal Definition
**Gradients in Deep Networks** refers to the analysis of how loss-function derivatives behave as they propagate through many layers of a neural network. The phenomenon encompasses:
- **Vanishing gradients**: $\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} \right\vert \to 0$ as $l \to 1$ (early layers)
- **Exploding gradients**: $\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} \right\vert \to \infty$ as $l \to 1$

For an $L$-layer network, the gradient with respect to weight $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ in layer $l$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} \cdot \left[ \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k-1)}} \right] \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$$

The bracketed product is the **gradient path product**, whose repeated multiplication is the root cause of vanishing/exploding gradients.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a game of **telephone** (Chinese whispers) with 100 people in a row. The first person whispers a message, and the 100th person must report it back to the original. By the time the message reaches the end, it is either:
- **Garbled beyond recognition** (vanishing gradient) — each whisper attenuates the signal.
- **Amplified into gibberish** (exploding gradient) — each whisper exaggerates it.

Either way, the original messenger has no useful feedback. This is precisely why **very deep networks** were untrainable before innovations like **ResNet's skip connections**, **Batch Normalization**, and careful **weight initialization** schemes (Xavier, He) became standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Indicator**: For typical sigmoid activations, gradients saturate to roughly **0.25** at the maximum. Multiplying 50 such factors: $0.25^{50} \approx 10^{-30}$. Hence vanishing gradients are mathematically inevitable without intervention.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why These Identities Matter — Operational Logic

### The Problem of Brute-Force Differentiation

Consider a 3-layer network with weight matrices $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}, \mathbf{W}^{(2)}, \mathbf{W}^{(3)}$. The total parameter count could be $10^4$ to $10^7$ for modern networks. Computing each gradient via definition:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{ij}^{(l)}} = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta} + \epsilon \mathbf{E}_{ij}) - \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\epsilon}$$

would require **two full forward passes per parameter**. For a network with $10^7$ parameters, this is computationally impossible.

The structured matrix identities reduce this to **a single forward + backward pass**, regardless of parameter count.

### Logical Steps of the Framework

**Step 1 — Represent the network as a computational graph.**
Each node is a tensor; each edge is an elementary operation ($\times$, $+$, $\sigma$, etc.).

**Step 2 — Forward pass** computes all intermediate activations $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ and the final loss $\mathcal{L}$.

**Step 3 — Initialize the output gradient** $\bar{\mathcal{L}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathcal{L}} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Backward pass** traverses the graph in reverse, applying the **local gradient rule** at each node:

$$\bar{x}_{\text{input}} = \bar{x}_{\text{output}} \cdot \frac{\partial x_{\text{output}}}{\partial x_{\text{input}}}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate** gradients for parameters via the chain rule to obtain $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}} \mathcal{L}$ for all layers.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every derivative identity and backprop equation expected in KTU 2024 ESE/IA questions on this topic. Memorize the formulas and their preconditions.

| **#** | **Function / Identity** | **Gradient Expression** | **Conditions / Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Dot product $f = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b}$ | $\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{b} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{a}$ | $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ depend on $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 2 | Affine map $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$ | $\frac{\partial \mathbf{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}^T$ | Jacobian is $\mathbf{W}^T$ |
| 3 | Quadratic form $f = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}$ | $\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^T)\mathbf{x}$ | Reduces to $2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ if symmetric |
| 4 | Element-wise activation $y = \sigma(z)$ | $\frac{\partial \mathbf{y}}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{z}))$ | Diagonal Jacobian |
| 5 | Cross-entropy loss $L = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} = -\frac{\mathbf{y}}{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}$ | Element-wise division |
| 6 | MSE loss $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum(\hat{y} - y)^2$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \frac{2}{N}(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$ | Linear in error |
| 7 | Softmax + Cross-Entropy combined | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | Elegant simplification |
| 8 | Layer $l$ weight gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l)} \cdot (\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})^T$ | Outer product form |
| 9 | Layer $l$ bias gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l)}$ | Direct copy of upstream |
| 10 | Pre-activation gradient | $\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)} = \bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l)} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Hadamard (element-wise) product |
| 11 | Backprop recursion | $\bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l-1)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(l)})^T \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)}$ | Linear backward pass |
| 12 | Chain rule (matrix form) | $\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}$ | Composed mappings |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Precondition Check**: Identity 3 reduces to $2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ **only if** $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric. For non-symmetric $\mathbf{A}$, you must use the full expression. KTU examiners love testing this subtle distinction.

---

## 2.3 Three Paradigms of Differentiation — Comparative Analysis

| **Paradigm** | **Method** | **Accuracy** | **Speed** | **Memory** | **Used For** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Manual/Symbolic | Expression manipulation | Exact | Fast (after compile) | High | Math software, formula derivation |
| Numerical | Finite differences | Approximate | Very slow | Low | Debugging, sanity checks |
| Forward-mode AD | Dual numbers / Taylor | Exact | Fast (few inputs) | Low | Control, small-input ML |
| Reverse-mode AD | Tape + reverse traversal | Exact | Fast (few outputs) | High | **Deep learning (backprop)** |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision (CNNs)**: Backprop enables training of 50–152 layer ResNets for image classification.
- **Natural Language Processing (Transformers)**: Reverse-mode AD trains models with billions of parameters (GPT, BERT).
- **Reinforcement Learning**: Policy gradient methods rely on backprop through reward functions.
- **Scientific ML (PINNs)**: Physics-Informed Neural Networks use autodiff to embed PDE residuals into loss.
- **Autonomous Systems**: Real-time trajectory optimization uses forward-mode AD for sensitivity analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> All modern frameworks — **PyTorch (Autograd), TensorFlow (GradientTape), JAX** — implement **reverse-mode automatic differentiation** as their core engine. Understanding this topic is equivalent to understanding the foundation of every deep learning library.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of the Dot Product

**Statement**: If $f = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b}$ where both $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ are functions of $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^m$, prove that:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{b} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{a}$$

### Step 1 — Expand the scalar explicitly

Since $f$ is a scalar, $f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i(\mathbf{x}) b_i(\mathbf{x})$. The partial derivative with respect to a single component $x_k$ is:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial a_i}{\partial x_k} b_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \frac{\partial b_i}{\partial x_k}$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the matrix-vector product structure

The first sum can be written as the $k$-th row of $\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ dotted with $\mathbf{b}$. The second sum is the $k$-th row of $\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ dotted with $\mathbf{a}$. Hence:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k} = \left[\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{b}\right]_k + \left[\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{a}\right]_k$$

### Step 3 — Stack components into vector form

Combining all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ components yields:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{b} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{b}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right)^T \mathbf{a} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case**: When $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{x}$, we get $\frac{\partial (\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = 2\mathbf{x}$, confirming the well-known gradient of the squared norm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Backprop Through a Single Fully-Connected Layer

Consider a single layer with:
- Input $\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$
- Pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$
- Output $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$
- Downstream gradient received: $\bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l)}}$

### Step 1 — Compute gradient w.r.t. pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$

Apply the chain rule element-wise. For each component $i$:

$$\bar{z}_i^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_j^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_j^{(l)}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}}$$

Since $\frac{\partial h_j^{(l)}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} = \sigma'(z_i^{(l)}) \delta_{ij}$, we get:

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)} = \bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l)} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

where $\odot$ denotes element-wise multiplication.

### Step 2 — Compute gradient w.r.t. weight $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$

Using $z_i^{(l)} = \sum_k W_{ik}^{(l)} h_k^{(l-1)} + b_i^{(l)}$:

$$\frac{\partial z_i^{(l)}}{\partial W_{ij}^{(l)}} = h_j^{(l-1)}$$

Apply chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{ij}^{(l)}} = \bar{z}_i^{(l)} \cdot h_j^{(l-1)}$$

In matrix form, this is the outer product:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)} \cdot (\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})^T$$

### Step 3 — Compute gradient w.r.t. bias $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$

Since $\frac{\partial z_i^{(l)}}{\partial b_i^{(l)}} = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)}$$

### Step 4 — Compute gradient w.r.t. input $\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)}$ (to propagate backward)

$$\frac{\partial z_i^{(l)}}{\partial h_j^{(l-1)}} = W_{ij}^{(l)}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l-1)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(l)})^T \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Cost**: This four-step process requires only $O(mn)$ operations, matching a single forward pass. Without backprop, computing the gradient would require $O(mn \cdot L)$ operations for $L$ layers via numerical differentiation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Two-Layer Network with MSE Loss

**Network architecture**:
- Layer 1: $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$, $\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$
- Layer 2: $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \vert\vert^2$

**Forward pass**:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}, \quad \mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}), \quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Backward pass — initialize**:

$$\bar{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$$

**Step A — Gradient w.r.t. Layer 2 parameters**:

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} = \bar{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} \quad (\text{linear layer, no activation})$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} \cdot (\mathbf{h}^{(1)})^T = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \cdot (\mathbf{h}^{(1)})^T$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(2)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$$

**Step B — Propagate to Layer 1**:

$$\bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(1)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$$

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} = \bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(1)} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

**Step C — Gradient w.r.t. Layer 1 parameters**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} \cdot \mathbf{x}^T = \left[(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})\right] \cdot \mathbf{x}^T$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} = \left[(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check**: If $\sigma = \text{ReLU}$ and we are in the active region, $\sigma'(z) = 1$. Then $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$ reduces to $(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \mathbf{x}^T$. The upstream gradient simply gets multiplied by the transpose of the next layer's weights — illustrating why **weight magnitude directly controls gradient flow**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Activation-Specific Gradient Flow

| **Activation** | **$\sigma'(z)$** | **Max $\sigma'$** | **Vanishing Risk** | **Used In** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | **0.25** | High | Shallow nets, gates |
| Tanh | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | **1.00** | Moderate | RNNs, LSTMs |
| ReLU | $\max(0, 1)$ | **1.00** | Low (one-sided) | Modern CNNs |
| Leaky ReLU | $\max(\alpha, 1)$ | **1.00** | Low | Deep vision |
| GELU | $\Phi(z) + z\phi(z)$ | **~1.0** | Low | Transformers |
| Swish | $\sigma(z)(1 + z(1-\sigma(z)))$ | **~1.1** | Very Low | EfficientNet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: For a 50-layer sigmoid network, the worst-case gradient attenuation is $0.25^{50} \approx 8.9 \times 10^{-31}$. For tanh it is $1.0^{50} = 1$ at best, but typically much less in saturated regions. For ReLU, the attenuation is $0.5^{50} \approx 8.9 \times 10^{-16}$ in the worst case (50% dead neurons), but actively firing paths remain at $1.0^{50} = 1$.
