# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between forward-mode and reverse-mode automatic differentiation. In which scenario is reverse-mode preferred for deep learning, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Forward-mode AD propagates derivatives from inputs to outputs using dual numbers; it is efficient when the function has **few inputs but many outputs** ($n \ll m$). Reverse-mode AD propagates derivatives from outputs back to inputs using a recorded tape; it is efficient when the function has **few outputs but many inputs** ($m \ll n$).

In deep learning, we have a **single scalar loss** with respect to **millions of parameters** ($n \approx 10^6$–$10^9$, $m = 1$). This is the canonical reverse-mode scenario, where one backward pass yields **all gradients simultaneously**, making training computationally tractable. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the matrix-form chain rule and explain why it is fundamental to backpropagation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The matrix-form chain rule states that for composed functions $f(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{u}}$$

It is fundamental to backpropagation because neural networks are compositions of many layer functions $f = f_L \circ f_{L-1} \circ \cdots \circ f_1$. Applying the chain rule recursively from the loss back to the input yields the gradient of the loss w.r.t. any intermediate weight in $O(1)$ extra cost per layer, avoiding the $O(n)$ cost of computing each gradient independently. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 Internal Choice Set A]`

**Consider the two-layer neural network defined as:**

$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}), \quad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}, \quad \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \vert\vert^2$$

**where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation.**

**(a)** Derive the complete backward pass expressions for $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}$, $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(2)}}$, and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$, clearly showing every step using the chain rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that if $\sigma$ is replaced by ReLU and the network is deepened to $L$ layers, the gradient norm satisfies $\vert\vert \nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \mathcal{L} \vert\vert \approx \prod_{l=2}^{L} \vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} \mathcal{L} \vert\vert$. Hence explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem**, and mention **two techniques** to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A

**Part (a) — Full Backprop Derivation**

**Step 1: Forward pass output.**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}) + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

**Step 2: Initial upstream gradient from MSE loss.**

$$\bar{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3: Layer 2 weight gradient.** Since $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is linear in $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$:

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} = \bar{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(2)} \cdot \mathbf{h}^T = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \cdot \mathbf{h}^T \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Layer 2 bias gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(2)}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Backward through Layer 2 linear map.**

$$\bar{\mathbf{h}} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T \bar{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Step 6: Backward through sigmoid activation.**

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} = \bar{\mathbf{h}} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \left[\sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) \odot (1 - \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}))\right]$$

**Step 7: Layer 1 weight gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} \cdot \mathbf{x}^T = \left[(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^T (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) \odot (1 - \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}))\right] \cdot \mathbf{x}^T \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Layer 1 bias gradient (bonus, for completeness):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks total in part a)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Deep Network Gradient Analysis**

**Derivation**: For an $L$-layer ReLU network with no saturating nonlinearities (in active regions, $\sigma'(z) = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)} (\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})^T$$

Recursively applying the chain rule through $L-1$ linear layers:

$$\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} = \prod_{l=2}^{L} (\mathbf{W}^{(l)})^T \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$$

Taking operator norms:

$$\vert\vert \bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(1)} \vert\vert \approx \prod_{l=2}^{L} \vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \vert\vert \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Vanishing gradient** occurs when each $\vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \vert\vert < 1$ (e.g., $0.9$), causing $\prod 0.9^{L-1} \to 0$ as $L \to \infty$. Early layers receive negligible updates and never learn.

**Exploding gradient** occurs when $\vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \vert\vert > 1$ (e.g., $1.1$), causing $\prod 1.1^{L-1} \to \infty$. Updates become numerically unstable, causing NaN losses. **(2 marks)**

**Two mitigation techniques**:
1. **Xavier/He initialization**: Sets $\text{Var}(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ so that $\vert\vert \mathbf{W} \vert\vert \approx 1$ in expectation, keeping the product balanced.
2. **Batch Normalization**: Normalizes activations per layer, so the gradient path product is approximately unit-magnitude regardless of depth.
3. **Residual connections (ResNet)**: Adds skip paths $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + F(\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})$, making gradient product approximately additive instead of multiplicative.
4. **Gradient clipping**: Caps gradient norm to prevent explosion. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1**: In part (a), students frequently forget to apply the **sigmoid derivative element-wise** and instead write the gradient as if the activation were linear. This loses 2–3 marks. Always write $\sigma'(\mathbf{z}) = \sigma(\mathbf{z}) \odot (1 - \sigma(\mathbf{z}))$ explicitly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2**: In part (b), many students confuse "$\vert\vert \mathbf{W} \vert\vert$" with "the determinant" or "the trace". The intended meaning is the **spectral norm** (largest singular value). Writing the product correctly with operator norm notation is what earns full marks.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 Internal Choice Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of automatic differentiation** with an example computation graph. Compute, using **reverse-mode automatic differentiation**, the derivative $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}$ for the function $f(x_1, x_2) = \ln(x_1 x_2) + \sin(x_1 + x_2)$ at the point $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 1)$. Show the forward pass values and the backward pass adjoints step by step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in deep networks. Derive the general expression for the gradient in an $L$-layer feed-forward network and show mathematically how it leads to instability. Propose and justify **three architectural solutions**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B

**Part (a) — Reverse-Mode Autodiff**

**Define intermediates:**

$$a = x_1, \quad b = x_2, \quad c = a \cdot b, \quad d = a + b, \quad e = \sin(d), \quad f_1 = \ln(c), \quad f = f_1 + e$$

**Forward pass at $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 1)$:**

$$a = 2, \quad b = 1, \quad c = 2, \quad d = 3, \quad e = \sin(3) \approx 0.1411, \quad f_1 = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

$$f = 0.6931 + 0.1411 = 0.8342$$

**Backward pass — initialize:**

$$\bar{f} = 1$$

**Adjoint of $f$:**

$$\bar{f_1} = \bar{f} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial f_1} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1, \quad \bar{e} = \bar{f} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial e} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

**Adjoint of $e = \sin(d)$:**

$$\bar{d} = \bar{e} \cdot \cos(d) = 1 \cdot \cos(3) \approx -0.9900$$

**Adjoint of $f_1 = \ln(c)$:**

$$\bar{c} = \bar{f_1} \cdot \frac{1}{c} = 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Adjoint of $c = a \cdot b$:**

$$\bar{a} \mathrel{+}= \bar{c} \cdot b = 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5, \quad \bar{b} \mathrel{+}= \bar{c} \cdot a = 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1.0$$

**Adjoint of $d = a + b$:**

$$\bar{a} \mathrel{+}= \bar{d} \cdot 1 = -0.9900, \quad \bar{b} \mathrel{+}= \bar{d} \cdot 1 = -0.9900$$

**Final adjoints (sum of contributions):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = \bar{a} = 0.5 + (-0.9900) = -0.4900 \quad \textbf{(4 marks for derivation)}$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = \bar{b} = 1.0 + (-0.9900) = 0.0100 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Verification by direct differentiation:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = \frac{1}{x_1} + \cos(x_1 + x_2) = \frac{1}{2} + \cos(3) = 0.5 - 0.9900 = -0.4900 \quad \checkmark \quad \textbf{(2 marks for verification)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Analysis**

**Setup**: Consider an $L$-layer feed-forward network:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}), \quad l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$

with $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{h}^{(L)}$.

**General gradient expression**: Applying the chain rule repeatedly:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} \cdot \left[ \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k-1)}} \right] \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$$

Each Jacobian factor expands as:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k-1)}} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(k)})) \cdot \mathbf{W}^{(k)}$$

**Norm analysis**: Taking operator norms:

$$\left\vert\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k-1)}} \right\vert\right\vert \leq \vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(k)} \vert\vert \cdot \max_j \vert \sigma'(z_j^{(k)}) \vert$$

Let $\gamma = \max(\vert \sigma' \vert)$ and $\rho = \vert\vert \mathbf{W} \vert\vert$ (assumed uniform). Then:

$$\left\vert\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} \right\vert\right\vert \leq (\gamma \rho)^{L-l} \cdot \left\vert\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}} \right\vert\right\vert \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Vanishing regime**: If $\gamma \rho < 1$ (typical for sigmoid networks where $\gamma \leq 0.25$ and weights shrink), then $(\gamma \rho)^{L-l} \to 0$ exponentially as $l \to 1$. Early-layer weights receive near-zero gradients and stop learning. **(1 mark)**

**Exploding regime**: If $\gamma \rho > 1$ (e.g., deep ReLU networks with poorly initialized weights), then $(\gamma \rho)^{L-l} \to \infty$. Gradient updates cause weight oscillation, NaN losses, and divergence. **(1 mark)**

**Three architectural solutions with justifications**:

1. **Residual connections (He et al., ResNet 2015)**: Defines $\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + F(\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)})$, so the gradient path becomes $1 + \frac{\partial F}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)}}$. The additive "1" prevents the product from collapsing to zero, enabling training of 100+ layer networks.

2. **Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015)**: Normalizes each layer's pre-activations to have zero mean and unit variance. This effectively keeps $\gamma \approx 1$ and stabilizes the distribution, ensuring $\gamma \rho \approx 1$.

3. **Careful initialization (He, Xavier/Glorot)**: Choose weight variances as $\text{Var}(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (He, for ReLU) or $\text{Var}(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{1}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}$ (Xavier, for tanh). This sets $\rho \approx \sqrt{2/n_{\text{in}}}$, balancing forward signal variance and backward gradient variance across layers.

4. **LSTM gating (Hochreiter, 1997)**: For RNNs, the forget gate's sigmoid output directly controls $\gamma$ in the gradient path, allowing the network to learn when to let gradients pass and when to block them.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3**: In part (a), students often forget that **intermediate variables** like $x_1$ can appear in **multiple paths** of the graph (here, $a = x_1$ appears in $c = ab$ and $d = a + b$). The adjoint $\bar{a}$ must be the **sum of contributions from all paths**. Forgetting to accumulate via $\mathrel{+}=$ loses 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4**: In part (b), a common error is to assume all weight matrices have the same norm and write $\vert\vert \mathbf{W} \vert\vert^L$ instead of the product $\prod_{l=1}^{L} \vert\vert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \vert\vert$. Full credit requires the product notation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** — Memorize these before entering the exam hall.

### Core Identities
- Dot product gradient: uses **sum of two terms**, one per operand.
- Affine map Jacobian: equals $\mathbf{W}^T$, NOT $\mathbf{W}$.
- Quadratic form gradient: $(\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^T)\mathbf{x}$, simplifies to $2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ only when symmetric.
- Element-wise activation Jacobian: **diagonal** matrix of element-wise derivatives.

### Backpropagation Essentials
- Backprop = **reverse-mode automatic differentiation** applied to neural networks.
- It is a **gradient computation algorithm**, not a learning algorithm.
- Forward pass stores intermediate activations; backward pass computes adjoints.
- Each layer's gradient has **four standard quantities**: $\bar{\mathbf{z}}^{(l)}$, $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$, $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}$, $\bar{\mathbf{h}}^{(l-1)}$.

### Activation Derivatives (Most Tested)
- Sigmoid: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$, max value **0.25**.
- Tanh: $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$, max value **1.00**.
- ReLU: $\sigma'(z) = 1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$.

### Gradient Stability
- Gradient norm scales as $\approx (\gamma \rho)^{L-l}$ — **exponential** in depth.
- Vanishing: $\gamma \rho < 1$. Exploding: $\gamma \rho > 1$.
- **Stable**: $\gamma \rho \approx 1$ (achieved via proper initialization).

### Automatic Differentiation Modes
- **Forward mode**: efficient for many outputs, few inputs. Carries derivatives alongside values.
- **Reverse mode**: efficient for few outputs (typically 1, the loss), many inputs. Requires tape.
- For deep learning: reverse mode wins by orders of magnitude.

### Cost & Complexity
- One forward pass: $O(\text{layer cost})$.
- One backward pass: $O(\text{layer cost})$ — same asymptotic cost.
- Total per training step: ~$2 \times$ forward cost, regardless of network size.

### Mitigation Strategies (Cite All Four)
- Skip / residual connections.
- Batch normalization.
- Xavier / He initialization.
- Gradient clipping (for explosion only).
- LSTM gating (RNN-specific).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Mantra**: *"Backpropagation is reverse-mode autodiff. Its efficiency comes from the chain rule. Its instability comes from repeated multiplication. Its solutions come from architectural choices that keep multiplicative products bounded."* — Memorize this line; it summarizes an entire module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Differentiation, Higher Order Derivatives, Linearization and Multivariate Taylor Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Differentiation, Higher Order Derivatives, Linearization and Multivariate Taylor Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automatic Differentiation (AD)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Automatic Differentiation (also called *Algorithmic Differentiation* or *Computational Differentiation*) is a set of techniques that evaluate the derivative of a function specified by a computer program by applying the **chain rule** of calculus automatically through a sequence of elementary arithmetic operations and elementary functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** AD is **neither** symbolic differentiation (which manipulates algebraic expressions) **nor** numerical differentiation (which uses finite differences). It is a hybrid that produces exact derivatives (up to floating-point round-off) by decomposing the program into elementary ops.

### Intuition: The "Tape Recorder" Analogy

Imagine a chef (the program) preparing a complex dish (the function value). As each ingredient is added (each elementary operation), a notebook (the **Wengert tape** or **computational graph**) records:
- The intermediate quantity produced.
- How it depends on the inputs.

When the dish is finished, the chef can rewind the tape and ask: *"If I had added 1 extra gram of salt at step 3, how would the final taste change?"* That "how would it change" number is the **derivative**. AD does this automatically—either forwards (Forward Mode) or backwards (Reverse Mode).

### Two Modes of AD

| Mode | Direction of Derivative Flow | Best Suited For | Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forward Mode** | Inputs $\to$ Outputs | Functions $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ with $n \ll m$ | Tracing a river from source to mouth |
| **Reverse Mode** | Outputs $\to$ Inputs | Functions $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ with $m \ll n$ | Tracing tributaries backward from mouth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AD matters in ML:** In a neural network with **millions of parameters** but a single scalar loss, Reverse Mode AD (i.e., *backpropagation*) computes the full gradient in a single backward pass at the cost of roughly one forward pass. This is the foundation of training every modern deep-learning model.

---

## 1.2 Higher-Order Derivatives

**Formal Definition:**
A *higher-order derivative* is the derivative of a derivative. The $n$-th order derivative $f^{(n)}(x)$ is obtained by differentiating $f$ successively $n$ times. For multivariate functions, higher-order derivatives are organized into **tensors**: e.g., the Hessian is the matrix of all second-order partial derivatives.

### Intuition: The "Acceleration" Analogy

If $f(t)$ gives your **position** at time $t$:
- $f'(t)$ = your **velocity** (first derivative, rate of change).
- $f''(t)$ = your **acceleration** (second derivative, rate of change of velocity).
- $f'''(t)$ = your **jerk** (rate of change of acceleration).

Each higher derivative captures how the previous one is changing — like adding instruments to your dashboard.

---

## 1.3 Linearization

**Formal Definition:**
Linearization is the process of approximating a nonlinear function $f(x)$ by a **linear function** $L(x)$ that is tangent to $f$ at a chosen point $x = a$. The first-order Taylor polynomial serves as this linear approximation:
$$L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$$

### Intuition: The "Tangent Line Telescope" Analogy

Stand at a point on a curved road and look through a telescope. The road's curve is invisible up close — you only see a straight tangent line stretching ahead. That tangent line is the **linearization**. The closer you stay to the anchor point $a$, the more accurate the linear model becomes. Move too far away, and the approximation diverges from the true curve.

> [!TIP]
> Linearization is the mathematical heart of **Newton's method**, **gradient descent**, and the **Gauss-Newton algorithm** used in nonlinear least squares.

---

## 1.4 Multivariate Taylor Series

**Formal Definition:**
The Multivariate Taylor Series generalizes the single-variable Taylor expansion to functions of several variables. For a scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ that is sufficiently smooth at a point $\mathbf{a}$, the expansion around $\mathbf{a}$ is:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k!} \left[ (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a}) \cdot \nabla \right]^{k} f \, \bigg|_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{a}}$$

where $\nabla f$ is the gradient and $(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a}) \cdot \nabla$ is the directional derivative operator.

### Intuition: The "Multidimensional Map" Analogy

A one-variable Taylor series is like flattening a hilly terrain into a 1-D road profile. The multivariate Taylor series instead maps a full 2-D/3-D landscape, using:
- **Gradient** = slope (compass needle showing the steepest ascent).
- **Hessian** = curvature (how the slope bends — a bowl, a saddle, or a dome).
- **Higher-order tensors** = finer and finer curvature details.

Each term adds one more "layer of detail" to the local approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Constants in the Formulas:**
> - The Taylor series **convergence radius** depends on the distance to the nearest singularity.
> - The **Hessian matrix** $H$ is symmetric when $f$ is $C^{2}$.
> - Standard polynomial coefficient: $\frac{1}{k!}$ (factorial normalisation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Computational Graph — Foundation of AD

A **computational graph** is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) in which:
- **Nodes** represent variables (inputs, intermediates, outputs).
- **Edges** represent elementary operations ($+, \times, \sin, \exp, \dots$).

Every composite function can be decomposed into a sequence of elementary ops, each having a known elementary derivative. The chain rule then stitches these elementary derivatives into the full derivative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wengert List (Tape):** Each node stores two pieces of information — its *value* $v_i$ and its *derivative* $\dot{v}_i$ (forward mode) or *adjoint* $\bar{v}_i$ (reverse mode).

---

## 2.2 Forward Mode Automatic Differentiation

### 2.2.1 Dual Numbers

A **dual number** has the form $\hat{x} = a + b\varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is a nilpotent symbol with $\varepsilon^{2} = 0$. The component $a$ is the primal value; $b$ is the **tangent** (derivative seed).

Elementary arithmetic on dual numbers follows:
$$(a + b\varepsilon) + (c + d\varepsilon) = (a+c) + (b+d)\varepsilon$$
$$(a + b\varepsilon)(c + d\varepsilon) = ac + (ad+bc)\varepsilon + \cancel{bd\,\varepsilon^{2}}^{0} = ac + (ad+bc)\varepsilon$$

The elementary-function rule is: $f(a + b\varepsilon) = f(a) + f'(a)\,b\,\varepsilon$.

### 2.2.2 Forward Mode Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

1. Assign each intermediate variable a **dual number** $v_i = v_{i}^{\text{primal}} + \dot{v}_{i}\,\varepsilon$.
2. Seed the derivative direction by setting $\dot{x} = 1$ for the chosen input.
3. Propagate forward: at each elementary op, apply the corresponding dual-number arithmetic rule. The primal and tangent components are updated together.
4. At the end, the **tangent component** of the output equals $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ at the chosen seed direction.

> [!TIP]
> Forward mode computes **one directional derivative per pass**. To get the full Jacobian column for one input, only one seed is needed.

### 2.2.3 Computational Cost

For a function with $n$ inputs and one output evaluated in $O(c)$ operations, forward mode costs roughly $2 \cdot O(c)$ — at most **2× the cost of evaluating $f$** for one directional derivative.

---

## 2.3 Reverse Mode Automatic Differentiation

### 2.3.1 Adjoint Variables

Reverse mode introduces **adjoint variables** $\bar{v}_i = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial v_i}$. After a **forward pass** that records all intermediates, a **backward pass** computes adjoints by iterating:
$$\bar{v}_j \;+=\; \bar{v}_i \cdot \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial v_j}$$
for every edge $v_j \to v_i$ in the graph.

### 2.3.2 Reverse Mode Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

1. **Forward pass:** Compute all $v_i$ and store them on the tape.
2. **Seed:** Set $\bar{y} = 1$ (the output's adjoint).
3. **Backward pass:** Traverse the graph in reverse topological order; for each variable, accumulate contributions from all its downstream consumers.
4. **Output:** Each input's adjoint $\bar{x}_j = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}$ — a full gradient in one pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation = Reverse Mode AD applied to neural network loss.** The seed $\bar{L}=1$ is the partial derivative of the loss w.r.t. itself.

### 2.3.3 Computational Cost

For $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$, reverse mode costs $O(c)$ for the full gradient — the **same order as evaluating $f$**. This is why training a network with $n = 10^{7}$ parameters is feasible.

---

## 2.4 Higher-Order Derivatives via Nested AD

To compute the $k$-th derivative of $f$, apply AD to $f^{(k-1)}$. Because AD itself returns a function (the derivative), we can compose it again — this is **nested AD**.

For multivariate functions, the **Hessian** $H(f)$ is the matrix:
$$[H(f)]_{ij} = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$

Computing the full Hessian naively costs $O(n^{2})$ forward-mode passes. Efficient strategies like **forward-over-reverse** or **reverse-over-reverse** achieve it in $O(n \cdot c)$.

---

## 2.5 Linearization — Single Variable

The first-order Taylor polynomial is:
$$L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$$

Geometrically, $L(x)$ is the tangent line to $y=f(x)$ at the point $(a, f(a))$. The error term is bounded by the Mean Value Theorem:
$$f(x) - L(x) = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2}(x-a)^{2} \quad \text{for some } \xi \in (a,x)$$

---

## 2.6 Linearization — Multivariate

For $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ at $\mathbf{a}$:
$$L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a})$$

For $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$, replace the gradient with the **Jacobian matrix** $J_{f}(\mathbf{a}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:
$$L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + J_{f}(\mathbf{a})\,(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a})$$

---

## 2.7 Multivariate Taylor Series — Detailed Form

For a scalar $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ expanded around $\mathbf{a}$ up to order $K$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + \frac{1}{2!}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})\,(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + \frac{1}{3!} \sum_{i,j,k} \frac{\partial^{3} f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j \partial x_k}\bigg|_{\mathbf{a}} (x_i-a_i)(x_j-a_j)(x_k-a_k) + \cdots$$

**Multi-index notation** elegantly unifies this. Define $|\boldsymbol{\alpha}| = \alpha_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n$ and $\mathbf{x}^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} = x_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots x_n^{\alpha_n}$. Then:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \frac{1}{\boldsymbol{\alpha}!} D^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} f(\mathbf{a})\, (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\alpha}! = \alpha_1!\cdots\alpha_n!$$

where $D^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} = \frac{\partial^{|\boldsymbol{\alpha}|}}{\partial x_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots\partial x_n^{\alpha_n}}$.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Dimension / Type | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dual Number** | $\hat{x} = a + b\varepsilon,\;\varepsilon^{2}=0$ | Scalar pair $(a,b)$ | $a$ = primal, $b$ = tangent |
| **Forward Mode Tangent** | $\dot{v}_{i} = \sum_{j} \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial v_j} \dot{v}_j$ | Scalar | Chain rule propagation |
| **Reverse Mode Adjoint** | $\bar{v}_j = \sum_{i: v_j \in \text{parents}(v_i)} \bar{v}_i \frac{\partial v_i}{\partial v_j}$ | Scalar | Backward sweep |
| **Single-Var Linearization** | $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$ | Scalar $\to$ Scalar | Truncates Taylor at $k=1$ |
| **Multi-Var Linearization** | $L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ | Truncates at first-order |
| **Jacobian** | $[J_f]_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | First-order multi-output |
| **Hessian** | $[H_f]_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Symmetric if $f \in C^{2}$ |
| **Multivariate Taylor ($K$-th order)** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\mid \boldsymbol{\alpha}\mid \leq K} \dfrac{D^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} f(\mathbf{a})}{\boldsymbol{\alpha}!}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}$ | Scalar series | $\boldsymbol{\alpha} \in \mathbb{N}_0^{n}$ |
| **Multi-Index Factorial** | $\boldsymbol{\alpha}! = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i!$ | Scalar | Normalisation |
| **Second-Order Term** | $\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})$ | Scalar (quadratic) | Captures curvature |
| **Linearization Error Bound** | $\mid f(x)-L(x)\mid \leq \frac{M}{2}(x-a)^{2}$ | Scalar | $M = \max\mid f''\mid$ on interval |
| **AD Forward Cost** | $\approx 2 \cdot \text{cost}(f)$ per direction | — | One direction per pass |
| **AD Reverse Cost** | $\approx 1 \cdot \text{cost}(f)$ for full gradient | — | One scalar output |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Role of AD / Taylor |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Deep Learning Training** | Backprop = reverse-mode AD; optimizers use linearization (gradient descent) |
| **Robotics Control** | Newton-Euler equations linearised via first-order Taylor expansion |
| **Computational Fluid Dynamics** | Higher-order Taylor terms capture flow discontinuities |
| **Trajectory Optimization** | Second-order Taylor expansion gives quadratic programs (SQP methods) |
| **Structural Analysis** | Linearisation of nonlinear material models (plasticity, large deformation) |
| **Bayesian Inference** | Laplace approximation uses second-order multivariate Taylor |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Mode AD on a Composite Function

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ at $x = 2$ for $f(x) = \ln(x) + \sin(x)\,x^{2}$ using forward-mode dual numbers.

### Step 1 — Decompose into Elementary Operations

Define intermediate variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= x \\
v_2 &= \ln(v_1) \\
v_3 &= v_1^{2} \\
v_4 &= \sin(v_1) \\
v_5 &= v_4 \cdot v_3 \\
v_6 &= v_2 + v_5 \\
y &= v_6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Seed Each Variable as a Dual Number

Set $\hat{v}_1 = 2 + 1\cdot\varepsilon$ (we want the derivative w.r.t. $x$, so seed tangent = 1).

### Step 3 — Propagate Dual Numbers Forward

**Compute $v_2$:** Elementary rule $\ln(a+b\varepsilon) = \ln(a) + \dfrac{b}{a}\varepsilon$.

$$
\hat{v}_2 = \ln(2) + \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon = \ln 2 + 0.5\,\varepsilon
$$

**Compute $v_3$:** Rule for power: $(a+b\varepsilon)^{2} = a^{2} + 2ab\,\varepsilon$.

$$
\hat{v}_3 = 2^{2} + 2(2)(1)\varepsilon = 4 + 4\varepsilon
$$

**Compute $v_4$:** Rule for $\sin$: $\sin(a+b\varepsilon) = \sin a + b\cos a \,\varepsilon$.

$$
\hat{v}_4 = \sin 2 + \cos 2 \,\varepsilon
$$

**Compute $v_5$:** Multiplication of dual numbers (sum of tangent cross-terms):

$$
\hat{v}_5 = (\sin 2)(4) + \left[(\cos 2)(4) + (\sin 2)(4)\right]\varepsilon = 4\sin 2 + 4(\cos 2 + \sin 2)\varepsilon
$$

**Compute $v_6$:** Addition:

$$
\hat{v}_6 = \left[\ln 2 + 4\sin 2\right] + \left[0.5 + 4\cos 2 + 4\sin 2\right]\varepsilon
$$

### Step 4 — Extract the Derivative

The tangent component is:

$$
\frac{df}{dx}\bigg|_{x=2} = 0.5 + 4\cos 2 + 4\sin 2
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Symbolically

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[\ln x + x^{2}\sin x\bigr] = \frac{1}{x} + 2x\sin x + x^{2}\cos x
$$

At $x = 2$:

$$
= 0.5 + 4\sin 2 + 4\cos 2 \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reverse Mode AD (Backpropagation) on a 2-Layer Loss

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}$ for $L = (y - \sigma(w_2 \sigma(w_1 x)))^{2}$, treating $w_1, w_2, x, y$ as constants/scalars.

### Step 1 — Forward Pass (Build the Graph)

Define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= w_1 \cdot x \\
h_1 &= \sigma(u_1) \\
u_2 &= w_2 \cdot h_1 \\
h_2 &= \sigma(u_2) \\
L &= (y - h_2)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Seed the Output Adjoint

$$
\bar{L} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial L} = 1
$$

### Step 3 — Backward Sweep

**Step 3a — Adjoint of $L$ w.r.t. $h_2$:**

$$
\bar{h}_2 = \bar{L} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_2} = 1 \cdot 2(y - h_2)(-1) = -2(y - h_2)
$$

**Step 3b — Adjoint of $h_2$ w.r.t. $u_2$:** Recall $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1-\sigma(u)) = h(1-h)$.

$$
\bar{u}_2 = \bar{h}_2 \cdot h_2(1 - h_2)
$$

**Step 3c — Adjoint of $u_2$ w.r.t. $w_2$:**

$$
\bar{w}_2 = \bar{u}_2 \cdot \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial w_2} = \bar{u}_2 \cdot h_1
$$

**Step 3d — Adjoint of $u_2$ w.r.t. $h_1$:**

$$
\bar{h}_1 = \bar{u}_2 \cdot w_2
$$

**Step 3e — Adjoint of $h_1$ w.r.t. $u_1$:**

$$
\bar{u}_1 = \bar{h}_1 \cdot h_1(1 - h_1)
$$

**Step 3f — Adjoint of $u_1$ w.r.t. $w_1$:**

$$
\bar{w}_1 = \bar{u}_1 \cdot x
$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Gradient

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -2(y - h_2) \cdot h_2(1-h_2) \cdot w_2 \cdot h_1(1-h_1) \cdot x
$$

### Step 5 — Computational Cost Check

Each step uses a stored value from the forward pass — total cost $O(1)$ extra per variable, i.e., the reverse pass cost equals the forward pass cost. **No numerical differentiation was required**, and no symbolic graph manipulation beyond the elementary ops was used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Higher-Order Derivative via Nested AD

**Goal:** Compute $f''(x)$ for $f(x) = x^{4} + e^{x}$ at $x = 1$.

### Step 1 — First Derivative via AD (Forward Mode)

$$
g(x) := f'(x) = 4x^{3} + e^{x}
$$

By dual-number propagation on $\hat{x} = 1 + \varepsilon$:

$$
\hat{g}(1) = 4(1^{3}) + e^{1} + \left[12(1^{2}) + e^{1}\right]\varepsilon = (4 + e) + (12 + e)\varepsilon
$$

So $g(1) = 4 + e$ and $g'(1) = 12 + e$.

### Step 2 — Apply AD to $g$ Again (Nested)

Now compute $g'(x) = 12x^{2} + e^{x}$. Apply forward AD with seed $= 1$ at $x=1$:

$$
\hat{g}'(1) = 12(1^{2}) + e^{1} + \left[24(1) + e^{1}\right]\varepsilon = (12 + e) + (24 + e)\varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
f''(1) = 24 + e
$$

### Step 3 — Verification by Symbolic Differentiation

$$
f(x) = x^{4} + e^{x}, \quad f'(x) = 4x^{3} + e^{x}, \quad f''(x) = 12x^{2} + e^{x}
$$

At $x = 1$: $f''(1) = 12 + e$ … wait, $12(1)^2 + e^1 = 12 + e$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note:** Step 2 gave $f''(1) = 24 + e$, but direct symbolic gives $12 + e$. The discrepancy came from treating $g'(x)$ incorrectly in Step 2. The correct nested-AD approach is to take the *tangent component of the dual-number result of the inner AD*. Recomputing properly: $g(1+\varepsilon) = 4(1+\varepsilon)^3 + e^{1+\varepsilon}$. Expanding: $4(1+3\varepsilon) + e\cdot e^{\varepsilon} = (4 + e) + (12 + e)\varepsilon$. So $g'(1) = 12 + e$. ✓

The point: **nested AD is correct when each layer evaluates the primal of the previous layer correctly before differentiating again**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — First-Order Linearization from the Definition of Derivative

**Goal:** Derive the linearization formula $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Recall the Definition of Differentiability

$f$ is differentiable at $a$ if there exists a linear map $L(x) = f(a) + c(x-a)$ such that:

$$
\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - L(x)}{x - a} = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Constant $c$

Plug in the ansatz $L(x) = f(a) + c(x-a)$:

$$
\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a) - c(x-a)}{x-a} = 0
$$

Split the fraction:

$$
\lim_{x \to a} \left[ \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x-a} - c \right] = 0
$$

The limit of the difference equals the difference of limits:

$$
\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} - c = 0
$$

By definition of the derivative, this limit equals $f'(a)$, so:

$$
f'(a) - c = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad c = f'(a)
$$

### Step 3 — Final Linearization Formula

$$
L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Multivariate Linearization via Jacobian

**Goal:** Derive $L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + J_f(\mathbf{a})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})$ for $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

### Step 1 — Differentiability in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$

$f$ is differentiable at $\mathbf{a}$ iff there exists a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ such that:

$$
\lim_{\mathbf{h} \to \mathbf{0}} \frac{\| f(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{h}) - f(\mathbf{a}) - A\mathbf{h} \|}{\|\mathbf{h}\|} = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Linearize Each Coordinate

For component $f_i$:

$$
f_i(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{h}) = f_i(\mathbf{a}) + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}\bigg|_{\mathbf{a}} h_j + o(\|\mathbf{h}\|)
$$

### Step 3 — Stack into Vector Form

Define $J_f(\mathbf{a}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $[J_f]_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}$. Then:

$$
f(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{h}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + J_f(\mathbf{a})\,\mathbf{h} + o(\|\mathbf{h}\|)
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Linearization

With $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{h}$:

$$
L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + J_f(\mathbf{a})\,(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a}) \quad \blacksquare
$$

For scalar $f$ ($m=1$), the Jacobian reduces to the gradient transpose: $J_f = \nabla f^{\top}$.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Multivariate Taylor Series Up to Second Order

**Goal:** Show that the second-order multivariate Taylor expansion is:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + o(\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}\|^{2})$$

### Step 1 — Reduce to One-Variable via the Line $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{h}$

Define $\phi(t) := f(\mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{h})$. By the single-variable Taylor expansion around $t = 0$:

$$
\phi(t) = \phi(0) + \phi'(0)\,t + \tfrac{1}{2}\phi''(0)\,t^{2} + o(t^{2})
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\phi(0)$, $\phi'(0)$, $\phi''(0)$ Using the Chain Rule

**$\phi(0)$:** Direct evaluation:

$$
\phi(0) = f(\mathbf{a})
$$

**$\phi'(0)$:** Apply the chain rule $\phi'(t) = \nabla f(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})^{\top}\mathbf{h}$. At $t=0$:

$$
\phi'(0) = \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}\mathbf{h}
$$

**$\phi''(0)$:** Differentiate once more. Using $\frac{d}{dt}[\nabla f(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})]^{\top} = \mathbf{h}^{\top} H(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})$:

$$
\phi''(t) = \mathbf{h}^{\top} H(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})\,\mathbf{h}
$$

At $t=0$:

$$
\phi''(0) = \mathbf{h}^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})\,\mathbf{h}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Set $t = 1$, $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{a}$

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + o(\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}\|^{2}) \quad \blacksquare
$$

### Step 4 — Verification for $f(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{x}\|^{2}$

Here $\nabla f = 2\mathbf{x}$, $H = 2I$. Expanding around $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\|\mathbf{x}\|^{2} = 0 + 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{\top}(2I)\mathbf{x} = \|\mathbf{x}\|^{2} \;\;\checkmark
$$

The Taylor series is exact because $f$ is already quadratic.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Hessian Computation via Reverse-Over-Reverse AD

**Goal:** Compute the full Hessian $H(f)$ at a point $\mathbf{a}$ for scalar $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$.

### Step 1 — First Reverse Pass: Compute $\nabla f$

Apply reverse-mode AD with seed $\bar{f} = 1$. The result is the gradient vector $g \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ with $g_i = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$.

### Step 2 — Second Reverse Pass Per Coordinate

For each coordinate $i = 1, \dots, n$:
- Take the $i$-th component $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ (a scalar function of $\mathbf{x}$).
- Apply reverse-mode AD with seed $\bar{g}_i = 1$.
- The result is the gradient of $g_i$, which is exactly column $i$ of the Hessian.

### Step 3 — Cost Analysis

Each reverse pass costs $O(c)$ where $c$ is the cost of one $f$-evaluation. With $n$ such passes, total cost is $O(n \cdot c)$ — the same asymptotic order as evaluating $n$ directional derivatives.

### Step 4 — Symmetry Exploitation

Because $\frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = \frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i}$ (Clairaut's theorem, assuming $f \in C^{2}$), we only need to compute the upper triangle and mirror the lower triangle. This halves the work to $O\!\left(\tfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)$ evaluations in the worst case.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Graph for Forward Mode AD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input x_hat = 2 + 1e"] --> nodeA["v1 = x"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["v2 = ln v1"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["v3 = v1^2"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["v4 = sin v1"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["v5 = v4 x v3"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF["v6 = v2 + v5"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> out["Output y_hat = f + f' e"]
    classDef primal fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1e6fb8,color:#000;
    classDef dual fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000;
    classDef result fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000;
    class inp primal
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF dual
    class out result
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each blue node holds a dual number; the orange nodes propagate both the primal value and the tangent coefficient of $\varepsilon$ simultaneously along the arrows. The green node shows the final dual-number output from which the derivative is read off.

---

## 4.2 Forward Mode vs Reverse Mode — Side-by-Side Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FWD["FORWARD MODE BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        fInp["Inputs x with seeds dot_x"] --> fEval["Forward Evaluation Pass"]
        fEval --> fTape["Wengert Tape: v_i, dot_v_i"]
        fTape --> fOut["Outputs y with dot_y = df/dx"]
    end
    subgraph REV["REVERSE MODE BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        rFwd["Forward Pass: Compute v_i"] --> rStore["Store All v_i"]
        rStore --> rBack["Backward Sweep: Topological Reverse"]
        rBack --> rAdjoint["Adjoints bar_x = df/dx"]
    end
    fOut -.dual numbers.-> fMeta["Tangent component"]
    rAdjoint -.backprop.-> rMeta["Full gradient in one pass"]
    classDef fwd fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000;
    classDef rev fill:#ffe1f0,stroke:#75003a,color:#000;
    classDef meta fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b7500,color:#000;
    class fInp,fEval,fTape,fOut fwd
    class rFwd,rStore,rBack,rAdjoint rev
    class fMeta,rMeta meta
```

---

## 4.3 Multivariate Taylor Approximation — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    a["Anchor Point a in R^n"] --> zero["Order 0: Constant f(a)"]
    a --> first["Order 1: Gradient Times Delta x"]
    a --> second["Order 2: Hessian Quadratic Form / 2"]
    a --> third["Order 3: Third-Order Tensor Contracted with Delta x cubed"]
    a --> kth["Order k: k-th Order Tensor Term"]
    zero --> sum["Sum All Terms = Approximation of f at x"]
    first --> sum
    second --> sum
    third --> sum
    kth --> sum
    sum --> approx["Approximated f at x with Error o parallel Delta x parallel power k"]
    classDef anchor fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000;
    classDef order fill:#e6f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000;
    classDef sum fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#000;
    classDef err fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000;
    class a anchor
    class zero,first,second,third,kth order
    class sum approx sum
    class approx err
```

> **Interpretation:** Each "order" block pulls a piece of local differential geometry from the anchor point. Summing them reconstructs the function locally with bounded error.

---

## 4.4 Linearization of a 2-D Function — Visual Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fxy["f of x,y nonlinear surface"] --> taylor["Second Order Taylor at point a,b"]
    taylor --> term0["f of a,b: Constant Plane"]
    taylor --> term1["fx a,b times x-a plus fy a,b times y-b: Tilted Plane"]
    taylor --> term2["One half times Quadratic Form from Hessian: Bowl Shape"]
    term0 --> lin["Linear Approximation L of x,y = first two terms only"]
    term1 --> lin
    term2 --> quad["Quadratic Approximation Q of x,y = all three terms"]
    classDef func fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000;
    classDef taylor fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#fff;
    classDef out fill:#ccffe6,stroke:#006633,color:#000;
    class fxy func
    class taylor,term0,term1,term2 taylor
    class lin,quad out
```

> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** The **linearization** (L) keeps only the constant + first-order terms — yielding the tangent plane. Adding the Hessian term gives the **quadratic approximation** (Q), which captures local curvature. Higher ML algorithms (Newton's method) use Q; gradient descent uses only L.

---

## 4.5 Nested AD for Higher-Order Derivatives

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    f["f of x: Original Function"] --> ad1["Apply Forward AD: Get f prime of x"]
    ad1 --> ad2["Apply AD to f prime: Get f double prime"]
    ad2 --> ad3["Apply AD again: Get f triple prime"]
    ad3 --> adk["Apply AD k times: Get f superscript k"]
    classDef orig fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000;
    classDef deriv fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000;
    classDef higher fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#5e0099,color:#fff;
    class f orig
    class ad1 ad2 ad3 adk deriv
```

> **Note:** Each successive AD application increases cost but maintains machine-precision accuracy — unlike repeated numerical differentiation, which compounds round-off errors exponentially.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Explain Automatic Differentiation. How does it differ from symbolic and numerical differentiation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Automatic Differentiation (AD) is a computational technique that evaluates the derivative of a function specified by a computer program by applying the chain rule to its sequence of elementary arithmetic operations.

| Method | Mechanism | Drawback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Symbolic** | Manipulates algebraic expressions algebraically | Expression swell; slow on large code |
| **Numerical** | Finite differences: $\dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ | Round-off error $O(h)$; truncation error $O(h)$ |
| **AD** | Decomposes program into elementary ops; applies chain rule | None for finite programs; exact up to floating point |

**Key advantage:** AD gives derivatives **as accurate as floating-point arithmetic allows**, at cost $O(c)$ where $c$ is the cost of evaluating $f$.

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define linearization of a single-variable function. State the formula and geometric interpretation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Linearization of $f$ at $a$ is the process of approximating $f$ by a linear function $L$ tangent to $f$ at $(a, f(a))$.

**Formula:** $\;L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$

**Geometric Interpretation:** $L$ is the equation of the **tangent line** to the curve $y=f(x)$ at the point of tangency $(a, f(a))$. The slope is $f'(a)$, the instantaneous rate of change.

**Error Bound:** $\;\mid f(x) - L(x)\mid \leq \dfrac{M}{2}(x-a)^{2}$, where $M = \max_{\xi \in [a,x]} \mid f''(\xi)\mid$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing **Jacobian** (first-order multi-output derivatives) with **Hessian** (second-order scalar derivatives). They are different tensors.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In multivariate Taylor expansion, students often forget the $\tfrac{1}{k!}$ and $\tfrac{1}{\boldsymbol{\alpha}!}$ normalisations — losing 2 marks per sub-part.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the $\varepsilon^{2}=0$ rule when computing dual-number products — leading to wrong tangent components.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using reverse mode AD for a function with many outputs and few inputs (use forward mode instead) — costing time and partial credit.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In linearization, students sometimes write $L(x) = f(a) + f(a)(x-a)$, omitting the derivative $f'(a)$. **Always verify by dimension check.**

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ at $x = 3$ for $f(x) = e^{2x} + \ln(x^{2}+1)$ using **forward-mode automatic differentiation** with dual numbers. Show every step of the propagation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **multivariate Taylor series expansion** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ up to second order, starting from the one-variable Taylor expansion along a line $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{h}$. Provide the final formula involving the gradient and Hessian.

#### Model Solution for B-A

**(a) Forward-Mode AD at $x = 3$:**

Decompose into elementary operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= x \\
v_2 &= 2 v_1 \\
v_3 &= e^{v_2} \\
v_4 &= v_1^{2} \\
v_5 &= v_4 + 1 \\
v_6 &= \ln(v_5) \\
y &= v_3 + v_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seed:** $\hat{v}_1 = 3 + 1\cdot\varepsilon$.

**Propagation:**

$$
\hat{v}_2 = 6 + 2\varepsilon
$$

$$
\hat{v}_3 = e^{6} + 2 e^{6}\varepsilon
$$

$$
\hat{v}_4 = 9 + 6\varepsilon
$$

$$
\hat{v}_5 = 10 + 6\varepsilon
$$

$$
\hat{v}_6 = \ln 10 + \frac{6}{10}\varepsilon = \ln 10 + 0.6\varepsilon
$$

$$
\hat{y} = (e^{6} + \ln 10) + (2e^{6} + 0.6)\varepsilon
$$

**Extracted derivative:**

$$
\frac{df}{dx}\bigg|_{x=3} = 2e^{6} + 0.6
$$

**Verification (symbolic):**

$$
f'(x) = 2e^{2x} + \frac{2x}{x^{2}+1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f'(3) = 2e^{6} + \frac{6}{10} = 2e^{6} + 0.6 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(b) Derivation of Multivariate Taylor (Second Order):**

Define $\phi(t) = f(\mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{h})$. By the single-variable Taylor expansion around $t=0$:

$$
\phi(t) = \phi(0) + \phi'(0)\,t + \frac{1}{2}\phi''(0)\,t^{2} + o(t^{2})
$$

Apply the chain rule:

$$
\phi'(t) = \nabla f(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})^{\top}\mathbf{h}
$$

Differentiate once more using the symmetry of the Hessian:

$$
\phi''(t) = \mathbf{h}^{\top} H(\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{h})\,\mathbf{h}
$$

Evaluated at $t=0$: $\phi(0)=f(\mathbf{a})$, $\phi'(0)=\nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}\mathbf{h}$, $\phi''(0)=\mathbf{h}^{\top}H(\mathbf{a})\mathbf{h}$.

Setting $t=1$ and $\mathbf{h}=\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}$:

$$
\boxed{f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\top} H(\mathbf{a})\,(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}) + o(\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}\|^{2})}
$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** (Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **reverse-mode automatic differentiation** with a clear computational graph example. Compute the gradient of $L = (y - \sigma(w_2 \sigma(w_1 x)))^{2}$ w.r.t. $w_1$ and $w_2$ (treat $x, y$ as given constants and $\sigma$ as sigmoid).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the second-order multivariate Taylor expansion, derive the **Newton update step** for minimising a twice-differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$. State clearly the assumption on the Hessian.

#### Model Solution for B-B

**(a) Reverse-Mode AD (Backpropagation):**

Define forward pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= w_1 x \\
h_1 &= \sigma(u_1) \\
u_2 &= w_2 h_1 \\
h_2 &= \sigma(u_2) \\
L &= (y - h_2)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seed adjoint:** $\bar{L} = 1$.

**Backward sweep:**

$$
\bar{h}_2 = -2(y - h_2)
$$

$$
\bar{u}_2 = \bar{h}_2 \cdot h_2(1-h_2) = -2(y - h_2) h_2 (1-h_2)
$$

$$
\bar{w}_2 = \bar{u}_2 \cdot h_1 = -2(y - h_2)\,h_2(1-h_2)\,h_1
$$

$$
\bar{h}_1 = \bar{u}_2 \cdot w_2 = -2(y-h_2)\,h_2(1-h_2)\,w_2
$$

$$
\bar{u}_1 = \bar{h}_1 \cdot h_1(1-h_1)
$$

$$
\bar{w}_1 = \bar{u}_1 \cdot x = -2(y-h_2)\,h_2(1-h_2)\,w_2\,h_1(1-h_1)\,x
$$

**Final gradients:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = -2(y-h_2)\,h_2(1-h_2)\,h_1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -2(y-h_2)\,h_2(1-h_2)\,w_2\,h_1(1-h_1)\,x
$$

**(b) Newton Update Derivation:**

At current iterate $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$, use the second-order Taylor expansion:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^{\top} H(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{(k)})
$$

To find the next iterate, minimise the right-hand side w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$. Set the gradient of the quadratic model to zero:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + H(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

Solve for $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - H(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})
$$

**Assumption:** The Hessian $H(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ must be **positive definite** (so the quadratic has a unique minimum) and **invertible**. In practice, a small regularisation $H + \lambda I$ is added to guarantee positive-definiteness (Levenberg-Marquardt modification).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Automatic Differentiation** is exact (up to floating point) and uses the **chain rule** on elementary operations stored in a **computational graph**.
- **Forward mode** propagates tangents alongside values using **dual numbers** $\hat{x} = a + b\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon^{2} = 0$. Best for $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$ with **few inputs, many outputs**.
- **Reverse mode** (backpropagation) does a forward pass to record values, then a backward pass to compute **adjoints** $\bar{v}_i = \partial f / \partial v_i$. Best for **many inputs, one scalar output** (e.g., neural network loss).
- **Higher-order derivatives** are obtained by **nested AD** — applying AD repeatedly to the previously computed derivative.
- **Linearization** of a single-variable function at $a$: $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$. Geometrically the **tangent line**.
- **Multivariate linearization**: $L(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})$ — the **tangent hyperplane**.
- **Jacobian** $J_f \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the matrix of all first-order partial derivatives for $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$.
- **Hessian** $H \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the matrix of all second-order partial derivatives; **symmetric** when $f \in C^{2}$ (Clairaut/Schwarz).
- **Multivariate Taylor expansion** to second order:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{a}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{a})^{\top}\mathbf{h} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{h}^{\top} H(\mathbf{a}) \mathbf{h} + o(\|\mathbf{h}\|^{2})$$
- **Multi-index notation** unifies the expansion: $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \dfrac{D^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} f(\mathbf{a})}{\boldsymbol{\alpha}!}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a})^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}$.
- **Newton's method** arises naturally from second-order Taylor — the update $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - H^{-1}\nabla f$ requires $H$ to be positive definite.
- **Reverse mode cost** for full gradient of scalar $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ is $O(c)$ — same as one $f$-evaluation, hence feasible for deep nets.
- **Forward mode cost** for one directional derivative is $\leq 2 \cdot c$.
- **Linearization error** is $O((x-a)^{2})$ in 1-D and $O(\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{a}\|^{2})$ in multi-D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of a Probability Space - Discrete and Continuous Probabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT525 - COMPUTATIONAL FUNDAMENTALS FOR MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Construction of a Probability Space - Discrete and Continuous Probabilities # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Probability Space A **Probability Space** is the foundational mathematical framework that allows us to rigorously define and reason about random experiments. Formally, a probability space is a triple $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ where: $$\text{Probability Space} = (\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$$ Each component of this triple has a precise meaning: | Component | Name | Role | |-----------|------|------| | $\Omega$ | Sample Space | Set of all possible elementary outcomes of a random experiment | | $\mathcal{F}$ | Sigma-Algebra (Event Space) | A collection of subsets of $\Omega$ representing the events we can measure | | $P$ | Probability Measure | A function $P: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ assigning probabilities to events | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A probability space is *not* just a single number — it is a complete mathematical universe that encodes (i) what outcomes are possible, (ii) which groupings of outcomes we call "events," and (iii) the rules assigning likelihood to those events. Every ML algorithm that uses randomness (Naive Bayes, GMMs, VAEs, GANs) implicitly relies on this structure. ## 1.2 Component-Wise Intuition (Real-World Analogy) Think of a probability space as the **rules of a board game**: - **Sample Space ($\Omega$)** = The set of *every possible position* the board can be in (like every configuration of a chess board). - **Sigma-Algebra ($\mathcal{F}$)** = The set of *legitimate questions* we can ask about the board ("Is the white king in check?" — yes/no is a valid event; "Is the universe about to end?" is not a legitimate question because it isn't a subset of board positions). - **Probability Measure ($P$)** = The *rule book* that tells us how likely each question's answer is. ### The Sample Space $\Omega$ $$\Omega = \{\omega : \omega \text{ is a single elementary outcome}\}$$ - **Discrete $\Omega$:** Finite or countable. Example: tossing a coin, $\Omega = \{H, T\}$. - **Continuous $\Omega$:** Uncountable. Example: measuring rainfall in mm, $\Omega = [0, \infty)$. ### The Sigma-Algebra $\mathcal{F}$ A sigma-algebra on $\Omega$ is a collection of subsets $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{\Omega}$ satisfying three axioms: $$\text{(i) } \Omega \in \mathcal{F}$$ $$\text{(ii) } A \in \mathcal{F} \implies A^c \in \mathcal{F} \quad \text{(closure under complementation)}$$ $$\text{(iii) } A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F} \implies \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \in \mathcal{F} \quad \text{(closure under countable unions)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why a Sigma-Algebra?** For continuous random variables, we cannot assign probabilities to *every* subset of $\Omega$ (this leads to paradoxes like the Vitali set). The sigma-algebra carves out exactly those subsets that are "measurable" — the events for which a probability can consistently be defined. ### The Probability Measure $P$ Kolmogorov's three axioms (1933): $$\text{Axiom 1 (Non-negativity): } P(A) \geq 0 \;\; \forall A \in \mathcal{F}$$ $$\text{Axiom 2 (Normalization): } P(\Omega) = 1$$ $$\text{Axiom 3 (Countable additivity): } \text{If } A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j, \text{ then } P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$$ ## 1.3 Discrete vs Continuous Probabilities — The Crucial Distinction | Feature | Discrete Probability | Continuous Probability | |---------|---------------------|------------------------| | $\Omega$ | Finite or countably infinite | Uncountable (typically an interval in $\mathbb{R}^n$) | | Outcome probabilities | $P(\omega_i) > 0$ for each $\omega_i$ | $P(\omega_i) = 0$ for every single point | | Described by | **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** | **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | | Tool | Summation $\sum$ | Integration $\int$ | | Examples | Coin toss, dice roll, word counts | Height, time, temperature, pixel intensities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Deep Insight:** In a continuous probability space, *every single point has probability zero* — yet the total probability over the whole space is **1**. This seems paradoxical, but it is resolved by realizing that we integrate over uncountably many infinitesimal probabilities. Probability becomes an *area under a curve*, not a count of outcomes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructing a Discrete Probability Space ### Step 1: Define the Sample Space $\Omega$ For a discrete experiment, $\Omega$ is either **finite** (e.g., rolling a die, $\Omega = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$) or **countably infinite** (e.g., number of emails received per day, $\Omega = \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$). ### Step 2: Define the Event Space $\mathcal{F}$ For finite $\Omega$, the canonical choice is the **power set**: $$\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega} = \{\text{all subsets of } \Omega\}$$ This works because $2^{\Omega}$ automatically satisfies all sigma-algebra axioms. For example, with $\Omega = \{H, T\}$: $$\mathcal{F} = \{\emptyset, \{H\}, \{T\}, \{H, T\}\}$$ ### Step 3: Define the Probability Measure $P$ The **classical (Laplace) rule** for a finite uniform sample space: $$P(A) = \frac{\vert A \vert}{\vert \Omega \vert} = \frac{\text{number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{total number of outcomes}}$$ For non-uniform discrete spaces, we use a **Probability Mass Function (PMF)**: $$p: \Omega \rightarrow [0, 1], \quad \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} p(\omega) = 1$$ The probability of any event $A \subseteq \Omega$ is then: $$P(A) = \sum_{\omega \in A} p(\omega)$$ ### Worked Example: Two Coin Tosses - $\Omega = \{HH, HT, TH, TT\}$, $\vert\Omega\vert = 4$ - $\mathcal{F} = 2^\Omega$ (16 events) - $P(\omega) = 1/4$ for all $\omega$ (fair coin) - Event $A = $ "exactly one head" $= \{HT, TH\}$ - $P(A) = 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2$ ✓ ## 2.2 Constructing a Continuous Probability Space ### Step 1: Define the Sample Space $\Omega$ Typically $\Omega = \mathbb{R}$ (or an interval, or $\mathbb{R}^n$ for vector-valued random variables). ### Step 2: Define the Event Space $\mathcal{F}$ We use the **Borel sigma-algebra** $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ — the smallest sigma-algebra containing all open intervals. This includes: - All open intervals $(a, b)$ - All closed intervals $[a, b]$ - All half-open intervals $[a, b)$, $(a, b]$ - Countable unions/intersections of these - All singletons $\{x\}$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Borel?** The Borel sigma-algebra is rich enough to describe every physically meaningful event (any set you can write down with standard math notation is Borel) but small enough to avoid the paradoxes that arise when we try to measure "all" subsets of $\mathbb{R}$. ### Step 3: Define the Probability Measure via a PDF A **Probability Density Function** $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow [0, \infty)$ must satisfy: $$f(x) \geq 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$ $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$ The probability of any Borel-measurable event $A$ is: $$P(A) = \int_{A} f(x) \, dx$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Clarification:** $f(x)$ is *not* a probability! $f(x) \, dx$ is the probability that $X$ lies in an infinitesimal interval around $x$. For continuous distributions, $P(X = x) = 0$ for every $x$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Condition / Notes | |---|---------|---------------------|------------------| | 1 | Probability Space | $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ | Triple defining the framework | | 2 | Discrete PMF | $p(\omega_i) \geq 0, \sum_i p(\omega_i) = 1$ | Counts as summing over points | | 3 | Discrete Event Prob. | $P(A) = \sum_{\omega \in A} p(\omega)$ | Sum over outcomes in $A$ | | 4 | Classical (Uniform) | $P(A) = \vert A\vert / \vert\Omega\vert$ | Requires equally likely outcomes | | 5 | Continuous PDF | $f(x) \geq 0, \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ | Integral over whole space = 1 | | 6 | Continuous Event Prob. | $P(A) = \int_A f(x)\,dx$ | Integrates over the event region | | 7 | Complement | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | Universal, both discrete/continuous | | 8 | Union | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Inclusion-exclusion | | 9 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ | Requires $P(B) > 0$ | | 10 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ | Equivalent to $P(A\mid B)=P(A)$ | | 11 | Law of Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | When $\{B_i\}$ partitions $\Omega$ | | 12 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)}{\sum_j P(A \mid B_j) P(B_j)}$ | Foundation of Bayesian ML | | 13 | CDF | $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ | Continuous; $F'(x) = f(x)$ a.e. | | 14 | Expectation (Discrete) | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_i x_i p(x_i)$ | Sum over PMF | | 15 | Expectation (Continuous) | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f(x)\,dx$ | Integral over PDF | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Machine Learning Engineering | ML Application | Probability Space Component Used | |----------------|----------------------------------| | Naive Bayes Classifier | Discrete $\Omega$ with PMF + Bayes' theorem | | Logistic Regression | Continuous Bernoulli-like likelihood space | | Gaussian Mixture Models | Continuous $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^n$, PDFs of Gaussians | | Generative Adversarial Networks | Implicit density estimation on continuous $\Omega$ | | Reinforcement Learning | Discrete/continuous state and action spaces | | Bayesian Neural Networks | Posterior probability over parameter space | | Variational Autoencoders | Continuous latent $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^d$ with Gaussian prior | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Almost every ML model that handles *uncertainty* is, at its mathematical core, defining a probability space over its inputs, parameters, or outputs. Constructing these spaces correctly is the bedrock of probabilistic machine learning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Verifying the Axioms for a Discrete Space **Claim:** The classical rule $P(A) = \vert A\vert / \vert\Omega\vert$ satisfies Kolmogorov's three axioms. **Setup:** Let $\Omega$ be a finite set with $\vert\Omega\vert = N$. Define for any $A \subseteq \Omega$: $$P(A) = \frac{\vert A \vert}{N}$$ ### Proof of Axiom 1 (Non-negativity) $$\vert A \vert \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad N > 0$$ $$\implies P(A) = \frac{\vert A \vert}{N} \geq 0 \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Proof of Axiom 2 (Normalization) Taking $A = \Omega$, we have $\vert A \vert = \vert \Omega \vert = N$: $$P(\Omega) = \frac{N}{N} = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Proof of Axiom 3 (Countable Additivity) Let $A_1, A_2, \ldots$ be pairwise disjoint subsets of $\Omega$. Then: $$\left\vert \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \right\vert = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \vert A_i \vert$$ This is the **additivity of counting measure** for disjoint sets. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) &= \frac{\left\vert \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i \right\vert}{N} \\ &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \vert A_i \vert}{N} \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \frac{\vert A_i \vert}{N} \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i) \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: From PDF to CDF and Back **Setup:** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with PDF $f(x)$. ### Forward Direction (PDF $\rightarrow$ CDF) By definition, the **Cumulative Distribution Function** is: $$F(x) = P(X \leq x)$$ Using the probability measure definition for continuous spaces: $$ \begin{aligned} F(x) &= \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt \\ \frac{d}{dx} F(x) &= \frac{d}{dx} \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt = f(x) \end{aligned} $$ By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the derivative of the CDF recovers the PDF. ### Backward Direction (CDF $\rightarrow$ PDF) For any interval $[a, b]$: $$ \begin{aligned} P(a \leq X \leq b) &= F(b) - F(a) \\ &= \int_{a}^{b} f(t) \, dt \end{aligned} $$ This shows the CDF *encodes* all probability information — knowing $F$ is equivalent to knowing $f$. ### Properties of the CDF $$F(-\infty) = \lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$$ $$F(+\infty) = \lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$$ $$F \text{ is non-decreasing: } a \leq b \implies F(a) \leq F(b)$$ $$F \text{ is right-continuous: } \lim_{x \to a^+} F(x) = F(a)$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Constructing a Continuous Probability Space — Uniform on $[0,1]$ **Goal:** Define $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ such that $P(X \in [a, b]) = b - a$ for $0 \leq a \leq b \leq 1$. ### Step 1: Sample Space $$\Omega = [0, 1] \subset \mathbb{R}$$ ### Step 2: Sigma-Algebra Choose the **Borel sigma-algebra** restricted to $[0,1]$: $$\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{B}([0,1]) = \{A \cap [0,1] : A \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\}$$ ### Step 3: Probability Measure (Lebesgue Measure) Define the PDF: $$f(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Verify normalization:** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx &= \int_{-\infty}^{0} 0 \, dx + \int_{0}^{1} 1 \, dx + \int_{1}^{\infty} 0 \, dx \\ &= 0 + [x]_{0}^{1} + 0 \\ &= 1 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Verify the probability of an interval $[a, b] \subseteq [0,1]$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(a \leq X \leq b) &= \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \\ &= \int_{a}^{b} 1 \, dx \\ &= [x]_{a}^{b} \\ &= b - a \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compute the CDF $$ \begin{aligned} F(x) &= \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt \\ &= \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 1, & x > 1 \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the Continuous Uniform Distribution $U(0,1)$** — the most fundamental continuous probability distribution. Every random number generator in computer science is essentially sampling from a space constructed exactly this way. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Distribution Constructions | Distribution | $\Omega$ | PDF / PMF $f(x)$ or $p(k)$ | Range | |--------------|----------|------------------------------|-------| | Bernoulli($p$) | $\{0,1\}$ | $p^k(1-p)^{1-k}$ | Discrete | | Binomial($n, p$) | $\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$ | $\binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Discrete | | Poisson($\lambda$) | $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ | $\frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | Discrete | | Geometric($p$) | $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ | $(1-p)^{k-1} p$ | Discrete | | Uniform $U(a,b)$ | $[a, b]$ | $\frac{1}{b-a}$ | Continuous | | Normal $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | Continuous | | Exponential($\lambda$) | $[0, \infty)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | Continuous | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Mixed Setup **Problem:** A factory has two machines. Machine A produces 60% of items (with 2% defect rate), Machine B produces 40% (with 5% defect rate). Define the probability space and find $P(\text{defective})$. ### Construction - $\Omega = \{\text{items produced}\}$ - Discrete sample space: each item can be from $\{A, B\}$ and have status $\{\text{good}, \text{defective}\}$ - Sample point: $\omega = (M, S)$ where $M \in \{A, B\}$, $S \in \{\text{good}, \text{defective}\}$ ### Define PMF $$ \begin{aligned} p(A, \text{good}) &= 0.60 \times 0.98 = 0.588 \\ p(A, \text{defective}) &= 0.60 \times 0.02 = 0.012 \\ p(B, \text{good}) &= 0.40 \times 0.95 = 0.380 \\ p(B, \text{defective}) &= 0.40 \times 0.05 = 0.020 \end{aligned} $$ **Verify:** $0.588 + 0.012 + 0.380 + 0.020 = 1.000$ ✓ ### Compute Defective Probability (Law of Total Probability) Let $D = $ "defective" event. Partition $\Omega$ by machine: $$ \begin{aligned} P(D) &= P(D \mid A) P(A) + P(D \mid B) P(B) \\ &= (0.02)(0.60) + (0.05)(0.40) \\ &= 0.012 + 0.020 \\ &= 0.032 \end{aligned} $$ So **3.2%** of items are defective on average. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Probability Space Triple — Conceptual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Omega["OMEGA - Sample Space
All elementary outcomes"] Sigma["SIGMA-ALGEBRA F
Collection of measurable events"] Prob["PROBABILITY MEASURE P
Assigns likelihood to events"] Omega -->|"subsets of Omega"| Sigma Sigma -->|"maps F to 0,1"| Prob Exp["Random Experiment"] -->|"yields all outcomes"| Omega Que["Legitimate questions about outcomes"] -.->|"defines which subsets count as events"| Sigma Rule["Rules of likelihood"] -.->|"defines the assignment"| Prob Prob --> Out1["Kolmogorov Axiom 1: Non-negativity"] Prob --> Out2["Kolmogorov Axiom 2: Normalization"] Prob --> Out3["Kolmogorov Axiom 3: Countable Additivity"] ``` ## 4.2 Discrete vs Continuous Probability Space — Branching Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR PS["Probability Space (Omega, F, P)"] PS --> D{"Is Omega countable?"} D -->|"YES - Discrete"| D1["PMF: p(omega)"] D1 --> D2["P(A) = SUM over omega in A of p(omega)"] D2 --> D3["Examples: Coin toss,
Dice roll, Email count"] D -->|"NO - Continuous"| C1["PDF: f(x) continuous"] C1 --> C2["P(A) = INTEGRAL over A of f(x) dx"] C2 --> C3["Examples: Height,
Temperature, Time"] D3 --> D4["Single outcomes have P > 0"] C3 --> C4["Single points have P = 0"] ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical View of Events (Sigma-Algebra Generation) ```mermaid flowchart TB Top["TOP: OMEGA (whole space)"] Top --> A1["Event A"] Top --> A2["Event B"] Top --> A3["Event C"] A1 --> A1c["A complement
(closure under complement)"] A2 --> A2c["B complement
(closure under complement)"] A1 --> Union1["A UNION B
(closure under union)"] A2 --> Union1 A1 --> Inter1["A INTERSECT B
(De Morgan)"] A2 --> Inter1 A1 --> Union2["A UNION C"] A3 --> Union2 Bottom["BOTTOM: Empty Set
certain impossibility"] A1c --> Bottom A2c --> Bottom ``` ## 4.4 Construction Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["STEP 1: Identify Random Experiment"] S2["STEP 2: List ALL Outcomes"] S3["STEP 3: Form Sample Space OMEGA"] S4["STEP 4: Choose Sigma-Algebra F"] S5["STEP 5: Define Measure P"] S6["STEP 6: Verify Kolmogorov Axioms"] S7["STEP 7: Compute Event Probabilities"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 -->|"Pass"| S7 S6 -->|"Fail"| S5 S7 --> App1["Direct Computation P(A)"] S7 --> App2["Conditional P(A|B)"] S7 --> App3["Independence Checks"] S7 --> App4["Bayesian Inference"] ``` ## 4.5 Mapping to Machine Learning Workflows ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ML["Machine Learning Layer"] NB["Naive Bayes"] GMM["Gaussian Mixture Models"] BNN["Bayesian Neural Networks"] VAE["Variational Autoencoders"] end subgraph PS["Probability Space Layer"] DSpace["Discrete Space:
PMF, SUM"] CSpace["Continuous Space:
PDF, INTEGRAL"] end NB --> DSpace GMM --> CSpace BNN --> CSpace VAE --> CSpace DSpace --> AX["Kolmogorov Axioms"] CSpace --> AX AX --> UT["Utility:
Uncertainty Quantification,
Decision Making,
Generative Modeling"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a probability space. List Kolmogorov's three axioms.** **Model Answer:** A probability space is a mathematical structure $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ where: - **$\Omega$ (Sample Space):** The set of all possible elementary outcomes of a random experiment. - **$\mathcal{F}$ (Sigma-Algebra):** A collection of subsets of $\Omega$ representing all measurable events, closed under complementation and countable unions. - **$P$ (Probability Measure):** A function assigning a number in $[0, 1]$ to each event in $\mathcal{F}$. **Kolmogorov's Three Axioms:** 1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$ for all $A \in \mathcal{F}$. 2. **Normalization:** $P(\Omega) = 1$. 3. **Countable Additivity:** For pairwise disjoint $A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}$, $P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$. ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Distinguish between a Probability Mass Function (PMF) and a Probability Density Function (PDF).** **Model Answer:** | Feature | PMF (Discrete) | PDF (Continuous) | |---------|----------------|------------------| | Applies to | Discrete random variables | Continuous random variables | | Value at point | $p(x) = P(X = x)$ is the actual probability | $f(x)$ is *not* a probability; only $f(x)\,dx$ is | | Normalization | $\sum_x p(x) = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ | | Probability of $X = x$ | $P(X = x) = p(x) > 0$ (generally) | $P(X = x) = 0$ | | Probability of interval | $P(X = a) + P(X = a+1) + \ldots$ | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ | | Tool | Summation | Integration | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Choice A) **(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the probability space for the experiment of rolling two fair dice. Define the appropriate sample space, sigma-algebra, and probability measure. Compute the probability of the event "sum equals 7." **(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a continuous probability space for a random variable $X$ that follows the distribution with PDF $f(x) = 3x^2$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $0$ otherwise. Verify that this defines a valid probability measure and compute $P(0.2 \leq X \leq 0.8)$. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — Sample Space $\Omega$:** Each die outcome is in $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$, so rolling two dice gives ordered pairs: $$\Omega = \{(i, j) : i, j \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\}$$ Therefore $\vert \Omega \vert = 6 \times 6 = 36$. **Step 2 — Sigma-Algebra $\mathcal{F}$:** For a finite sample space, we take the power set: $\mathcal{F} = 2^{\Omega}$, giving $2^{36}$ events. **Step 3 — Probability Measure $P$:** Fair dice and independence give uniform measure: $$P(\{(i, j)\}) = \frac{1}{36} \quad \forall (i,j) \in \Omega$$ **Step 4 — Compute $P(\text{sum} = 7)$:** The favorable outcomes are: $(1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)$ — exactly 6 pairs. $$P(\text{sum} = 7) = \frac{6}{36} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Sample Space:** $\Omega = [0, 1] \subset \mathbb{R}$. **Step 2 — Sigma-Algebra:** $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{B}([0,1])$, the Borel sigma-algebra on $[0,1]$. **Step 3 — Verify Non-negativity:** $f(x) = 3x^2 \geq 0$ for all $x \in [0,1]$. ✓ **Step 4 — Verify Normalization:** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx &= \int_{0}^{1} 3x^2 \, dx \\ &= \left[ x^3 \right]_{0}^{1} \\ &= 1^3 - 0^3 \\ &= 1 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $P(0.2 \leq X \leq 0.8)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(0.2 \leq X \leq 0.8) &= \int_{0.2}^{0.8} 3x^2 \, dx \\ &= \left[ x^3 \right]_{0.2}^{0.8} \\ &= (0.8)^3 - (0.2)^3 \\ &= 0.512 - 0.008 \\ &= 0.504 \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $P(0.2 \leq X \leq 0.8) = 0.504$. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` (Choice B) **(a) [7 Marks]** Define a sigma-algebra. State and explain the three properties that any collection $\mathcal{F}$ of subsets of $\Omega$ must satisfy to be a sigma-algebra.
